topics.title: Introduction to Mechatronics: Structure of Mechatronics System and Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET745 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Mechatronics: Structure of Mechatronics System and Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mechatronics** is the synergistic and systematic integration of **Mechanical Engineering, Electronics, Control Engineering, Computer Engineering, and Information Technology** to design, develop, and manufacture intelligent products, processes, and systems.

The term **Mechatronics** was first coined by **Yasakawa Electric Corporation (Japan)** in **1969** as a combination of the words **"Mecha"** (mechanism) and **"Tronics"** (electronics). A modern mechatronic system is one in which the functional synergy between its mechanical, electronic, and computational sub-systems is central to the design process — they are no longer designed in isolation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Philosophy:** Mechatronics is **NOT** just the addition of electronics to a machine. It is a **holistic design philosophy** where mechanical, electronic, control, and software disciplines are merged at the **conceptual design stage itself**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Human Body"

To a beginner, a mechatronics system works exactly like the **human body**:

| Human Body Component | Mechatronics Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Senses** (eyes, ears, skin) | **Sensors** | Detect physical quantities (light, sound, pressure) |
| **Brain & Nervous System** | **Microcontroller / PLC** | Process data and make decisions |
| **Muscles** | **Actuators** (Motors, Solenoids) | Convert decisions into physical action |
| **Skeleton & Organs** | **Mechanical Structure** | Physical frame and motion transmission |
| **Heart & Blood Vessels** | **Power Supply & Wiring** | Energy distribution |

> [!TIP]
> Think of a **smartphone-controlled robotic vacuum cleaner** — it has a **mechanical chassis** (body), **wheels and motors** (actuators), **IR/ultrasonic sensors** (senses), and a **microcontroller running an algorithm** (brain). The clean integration of all these is **Mechatronics in action**.

## 1.3 Scope and Importance in Engineering

- **Precision Engineering:** Modern CNC machines, robotic arms, and 3D printers.
- **Automotive Systems:** ABS, Electronic Fuel Injection, Active Suspension, ADAS.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Auto-focus cameras, gaming consoles, smart washing machines.
- **Industrial Automation:** SCADA, PLCs, smart conveyor belts.
- **Medical Devices:** Robotic surgery, MRI machines, infusion pumps.

The global standard of quality is driven by the **Six-Sigma** initiative, where mechatronics-based quality control achieves defect rates below **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Generic Structure of a Mechatronics System

A mechatronic system operates on a closed-loop (or open-loop) architecture involving six fundamental stages. Understanding this **signal flow** is the single most important concept for KTU exams.

The signal flows as follows:

- **Input / Stimulus (Physical Variable):** The real-world quantity to be measured or controlled — e.g., temperature, position, force, pressure.
- **Sensing Element (Transducer):** Detects the physical variable and converts it into a measurable electrical signal. *Example: Thermocouple (heat → voltage), LVDT (displacement → voltage).*
- **Signal Conditioning Unit:** Amplifies, filters, linearizes, and digitizes (via ADC) the raw sensor signal to a usable level for the controller. *Example: Wheatstone bridge, instrumentation amplifier.*
- **Controller / Processor:** The "brain" — typically a **Microcontroller (e.g., Arduino, ARM Cortex)**, **DSP**, **PLC**, or **Industrial PC**. It executes the control algorithm (PID, fuzzy logic, etc.).
- **Actuator (Drive):** Converts controller commands into physical action. *Example: DC motor, stepper motor, hydraulic cylinder, piezoelectric stack.*
- **Mechanical System (Plant / Process):** The actual load being controlled — a robot joint, a valve, a vehicle, a manufacturing line.
- **Feedback Path:** The output is measured by a sensor and returned to the controller to **close the loop**, minimizing the **error** `e(t) = r(t) - y(t)`.

## 2.2 Static Performance Characteristics of Sensors (Board-Favourite Topic)

For any sensor, the input-output relationship is described by a **transfer function**:
$$ y = f(x) $$

The **static characteristics** describe performance when the input is **constant or slowly varying**:

- **Range:** The minimum and maximum values of the input the sensor can measure. `Range = x_max - x_min`.
- **Span (Full-Scale Output):** The algebraic difference between output readings at the maximum and minimum input. `Span = y_max - y_min`.
- **Sensitivity (K):** The ratio of change in output to change in input. `K = dy/dx`. Higher sensitivity = better resolution.
- **Linearity:** The degree to which the actual output-input curve deviates from a straight line. Quantified as **maximum deviation as a percentage of full scale**:
$$ \%\text{Linearity} = \frac{\Delta y_{\max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100 $$
- **Hysteresis (H):** The maximum difference in output for the same input, approached from opposite directions. A **hysteresis error** is non-recoverable.
- **Repeatability (Precision):** The ability of a sensor to produce the same output for repeated applications of the same input value, under identical conditions. Given by:
$$ \%\text{Repeatability} = \frac{\Delta y_{\max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100 $$
- **Drift:** Slow change in output over time when the input is held constant. *Example: Zero drift, sensitivity drift.*
- **Dead Zone / Threshold:** The smallest change in input that produces a detectable change in output.
- **Resolution:** The smallest incremental change in input that can be detected (often the smallest bit of an ADC).
- **Accuracy vs. Precision:** **Accuracy** is closeness to the *true value*; **Precision** is closeness among *repeated measurements*.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Memorize this table — questions on sensor characteristics appear almost every semester.

| Quantity | Formula / Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensitivity** | $K = \dfrac{dy}{dx}$ | Output Unit / Input Unit |
| **Range** | $R = x_{\max} - x_{\min}$ | Input Unit |
| **Span** | $S = y_{\max} - y_{\min}$ | Output Unit |
| **% Linearity** | $L_\% = \dfrac{\vert \Delta y_{\max} \vert}{y_{FS}} \times 100$ | % |
| **% Hysteresis** | $H_\% = \dfrac{y_{H,\max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100$ | % |
| **% Repeatability** | $R_\% = \dfrac{\vert \Delta y_{\max} \vert}{y_{FS}} \times 100$ | % |
| **Error** | $e = x_{\text{true}} - x_{\text{measured}}$ | Input Unit |
| **% Error** | $e_\% = \dfrac{e}{x_{\text{true}}} \times 100$ | % |
| **First-Order Time Constant** | $\tau = RC$ (electrical) or $\tau = B/K$ (mechanical) | second (s) |
| **Second-Order Damping Ratio** | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$ | dimensionless |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **automotive engine control**, a **mass air flow (MAF) sensor** must have a linearity of < 1% and a response time of < 10 ms; otherwise, fuel injection timing goes off, increasing emissions. In **aerospace**, **gyroscopes and accelerometers** (now MEMS-based) in an **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** require nano-radian resolution to maintain flight stability — mechatronics makes this affordable. In **biomedical prosthetics**, **myoelectric sensors** (measuring EMG signals) drive actuators that move artificial limbs with a sensitivity tuned to microvolt-level skin potentials.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: First-Order Sensor Step Response (Zero-Order Hold → Rise Time)

Consider a first-order thermal sensor (e.g., a **thermistor** or RTD) whose temperature is suddenly raised from $T_0$ to $T_{\infty}$ at $t=0$. The governing differential equation is:

$$ m c \, \frac{dT(t)}{dt} + hA \, \left[ T(t) - T_\infty \right] = 0 $$

**Step 1:** Let the time constant be $\tau = \dfrac{mc}{hA}$. Divide the entire equation by $hA$:

$$ \tau \, \frac{dT(t)}{dt} + T(t) = T_\infty $$

**Step 2:** Define the temperature deviation from the final value as $\theta(t) = T(t) - T_\infty$. Differentiating gives $\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = \dfrac{dT}{dt}$. Substitute:

$$ \tau \, \frac{d\theta(t)}{dt} + \theta(t) = 0 $$

**Step 3:** This is a first-order linear ODE. Rearrange:

$$ \frac{d\theta}{\theta} = -\frac{1}{\tau} \, dt $$

**Step 4:** Integrate both sides from $t = 0$ to $t$:

$$ \int_{\theta_0}^{\theta(t)} \frac{d\theta}{\theta} = -\frac{1}{\tau} \int_0^t dt $$

$$ \ln\left[ \frac{\theta(t)}{\theta_0} \right] = -\frac{t}{\tau} $$

**Step 5:** Exponentiate both sides:

$$ \theta(t) = \theta_0 \, e^{-t/\tau} $$

**Step 6:** Substitute back $\theta(t) = T(t) - T_\infty$ and $\theta_0 = T_0 - T_\infty$:

$$ T(t) - T_\infty = (T_0 - T_\infty) \, e^{-t/\tau} $$

**Step 7:** Solve for $T(t)$:

$$ T(t) = T_\infty + (T_0 - T_\infty) \, e^{-t/\tau} $$

**Interpretation:** The sensor output rises **exponentially** toward $T_\infty$. At $t = \tau$, the sensor reaches **63.2%** of the final change. At $t = 5\tau$, it reaches **99.3%** (often treated as the **settling time**).

## 3.2 Derivation: Second-Order System Response (Mass-Spring-Damper Sensor)

Many position and force sensors (e.g., accelerometers) are modeled as second-order systems. The standard form is:

$$ m \, \frac{d^2 y(t)}{dt^2} + c \, \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + k \, y(t) = F(t) $$

**Step 1:** Define the natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ and the damping ratio $\zeta = c / (2\sqrt{mk})$. Divide through by $m$:

$$ \frac{d^2 y(t)}{dt^2} + 2\zeta\omega_n \, \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + \omega_n^2 \, y(t) = \frac{F(t)}{m} $$

**Step 2:** For a unit step input $F(t)/m = \omega_n^2 \cdot u(t)$, the solution depends on $\zeta$:

- **Underdamped ($\zeta < 1$):** Oscillatory decay
$$ y(t) = 1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \, e^{-\zeta\omega_n t} \, \sin\!\left(\omega_d t + \phi\right) $$
where $\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ and $\phi = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}{\zeta}\right)$.

- **Critically damped ($\zeta = 1$):** Fastest non-overshooting response
$$ y(t) = 1 - (1 + \omega_n t) \, e^{-\omega_n t} $$

- **Overdamped ($\zeta > 1$):** Slow, sluggish response (two real exponential roots)

**Step 3:** Maximum overshoot (only for underdamped):
$$ M_p = e^{-\pi\zeta / \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \times 100\% $$

**Step 4:** Time to first peak:
$$ t_p = \frac{\pi}{\omega_d} = \frac{\pi}{\omega_n \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} $$

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A pressure sensor has a range of **0 to 200 kPa** and produces a voltage output of **1 V to 5 V**. At a true input of **100 kPa**, it reads **102 kPa**. Find the sensitivity, span, and percentage error.

**Step 1 — Sensitivity ($K$):**

$$ K = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{5 - 1}{200 - 0} = \frac{4}{200} = 0.02 \text{ V/kPa} $$

**Step 2 — Span ($S$):**

$$ S = y_{\max} - y_{\min} = 5 - 1 = 4 \text{ V} $$

**Step 3 — Percentage Error ($e_\%$):**

$$ e = x_{\text{true}} - x_{\text{measured}} = 100 - 102 = -2 \text{ kPa} $$

$$ e_\% = \frac{\vert e \vert}{x_{\text{true}}} \times 100 = \frac{2}{100} \times 100 = 2\% $$

> [!NOTE]
> Always quote the **absolute value** of error when expressing magnitude in percentage form. A negative sign only indicates *direction* of deviation (sensor reading higher than true value).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Mechatronic System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **complete signal flow** of a mechatronic system, from physical stimulus to feedback. Each block is a functional stage with its real-world hardware mapping.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inputA[Physical Input Variable]:::io
    senseB[Sensor and Transducer]:::sensor
    sigC[Signal Conditioning Unit]:::signal
    adcD[Analog to Digital Converter]:::signal
    ctrlE[Controller and Processor]:::control
    dacF[Digital to Analog Converter]:::signal
    actG[Power Amplifier and Driver]:::power
    actH[Actuator and Motor]:::actuator
    mechI[Mechanical System and Plant]:::mech
    outputJ[Physical Output Motion or Action]:::io
    fbK[Feedback Sensor]:::sensor

    inputA --> senseB
    senseB --> sigC
    sigC --> adcD
    adcD --> ctrlE
    ctrlE --> dacF
    dacF --> actG
    actG --> actH
    actH --> mechI
    mechI --> outputJ
    mechI -. measured by .-> fbK
    fbK -. feedback signal .-> sigC

    classDef io fill:#E8F4F8,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sensor fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef signal fill:#D1ECF1,stroke:#0C5460,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#F8D7DA,stroke:#721C24,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef power fill:#D4EDDA,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef actuator fill:#E2D4F0,stroke:#5A2D81,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mech fill:#F5E0E0,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Sensor Classification Hierarchy

A board-friendly classification tree to remember the families of sensors.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rootA[Classification of Sensors]:::root

    rootA --> branchB[Based on Power Requirement]
    rootA --> branchC[Based on Output Signal Type]
    rootA --> branchD[Based on Physical Variable Measured]
    rootA --> branchE[Based on Contact]

    branchB --> b1[Active Sensors - generate own signal]:::leaf
    branchB --> b2[Passive Sensors - need external power]:::leaf

    branchC --> c1[Analog Sensors - continuous output]:::leaf
    branchC --> c2[Digital Sensors - discrete output]:::leaf

    branchD --> d1[Position and Displacement]:::leaf
    branchD --> d2[Velocity and Speed]:::leaf
    branchD --> d3[Force and Pressure]:::leaf
    branchD --> d4[Temperature and Humidity]:::leaf
    branchD --> d5[Light and Optical]:::leaf
    branchD --> d6[Chemical and Gas]:::leaf

    branchE --> e1[Contact Sensors]:::leaf
    branchE --> e2[Non Contact Sensors]:::leaf

    classDef root fill:#003366,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    classDef leaf fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Common Sensor-to-Actuator Mapping (KTU Quick Reference)

| Physical Variable | Sensor Example | Actuator Example | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Displacement** | LVDT, Potentiometer, Encoder | Solenoid, Servo Motor | Robotic arm joints |
| **Velocity** | Tachogenerator, Optical Encoder | DC Motor | Conveyor belt control |
| **Force / Pressure** | Strain Gauge, Piezoelectric Sensor | Hydraulic Cylinder | Brake systems, weighing scales |
| **Temperature** | Thermocouple, RTD, Thermistor | Heater, Peltier Module | HVAC, plastic injection moulding |
| **Proximity** | Inductive, Capacitive, Hall Effect | Relay, Pneumatic Valve | Object detection on assembly line |
| **Light** | Photodiode, LDR, CCD Array | LED, LCD Display, Stepper Motor | Auto-brightness, barcode scanner |
| **Flow** | Orifice plate, Turbine flowmeter | Control Valve | Water treatment plants |
| **Acceleration** | MEMS Accelerometer | Active Suspension Actuator | Airbag deployment, tilt sensing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Whenever you draw or describe a mechatronic system in an exam, **always** clearly mark the **feedback loop** and identify the **error detector** where the reference input is compared with the measured output. Omitting the feedback path is the single most common reason students lose marks in design questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Mechatronics. List and briefly explain the four key disciplines it integrates.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** Mechatronics is the synergistic and systematic integration of mechanical, electronic, control, and computer engineering to design and manufacture intelligent products and systems. The term was coined by Yasakawa Electric Corporation of Japan in 1969.

> **Four Key Disciplines (2 Marks):**
> 1. **Mechanical Engineering** — Physical structure, kinematics, dynamics, and thermal design.
> 2. **Electronics Engineering** — Analog and digital circuits, signal conditioning, power electronics.
> 3. **Control Engineering** — Feedback theory, stability analysis, PID controllers, state-space design.
> 4. **Computer Engineering** — Microcontrollers, embedded firmware, real-time operating systems, communication protocols.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Active and Passive sensors. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Active Sensors (1.5 Marks):** Active sensors generate their own electrical output signal in response to the physical stimulus, without needing an external power source. The physical phenomenon itself produces the signal. *Examples: Thermocouple, Piezoelectric crystal, Photovoltaic cell, Tachogenerator.*

> **Passive Sensors (1.5 Marks):** Passive sensors require an external excitation (power) signal to operate. They act as variable impedances that modulate the external signal. *Examples: Thermistor, Strain gauge, LDR, Capacitive displacement sensor.*

> **Key Contrast:** Active = self-generating; Passive = externally excited.

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the structure of a generic mechatronics system. Describe the function of each block. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define Sensitivity, Span, Range, and Linearity for a sensor. A temperature sensor has a range of 0 to 200 °C and gives an output voltage of 1 V at 0 °C and 9 V at 200 °C. At 100 °C, the true output should be 5 V, but the actual output is 5.2 V. Calculate the sensitivity, span, and percentage linearity error. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(a)** Classify sensors based on (i) power requirement, (ii) output signal type, and (iii) physical variable measured. Give one example for each category. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the step response of a first-order measurement system. A thermistor used in a temperature monitoring system has a time constant of 8 seconds. If it is suddenly subjected to a temperature step of 50 °C (from 25 °C to 75 °C), find the temperature indicated by the sensor after (i) 8 seconds and (ii) 24 seconds. **(7 Marks)**

---

## Detailed Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Block Diagram of Mechatronic System (7 Marks):**

> Refer to the **Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1** of these notes for the full schematic. A clean hand-drawn equivalent in the exam should contain these labelled blocks in sequence:

1. **Input (Physical Variable):** Identify the quantity to be measured, e.g., position of a robot gripper. *(0.5 Mark)*
2. **Sensor:** Converts the physical variable to an electrical signal. Example: rotary encoder for position. *(1 Mark)*
3. **Signal Conditioning:** Amplifies (using instrumentation amplifier), filters noise, and linearizes the signal. *(1 Mark)*
4. **ADC:** Converts the analog signal into a digital number for the microcontroller. *(0.5 Mark)*
5. **Controller:** The "brain" (Arduino/ARM Cortex/DSP). Runs the control algorithm (PID). Generates a control command. *(1.5 Marks)*
6. **DAC and Power Amplifier:** Converts digital command to analog and boosts power to drive the actuator. *(0.5 Mark)*
7. **Actuator:** Electric motor / hydraulic cylinder that produces physical motion. *(1 Mark)*
8. **Mechanical System (Plant):** The actual load — e.g., the robot arm joint. *(0.5 Mark)*
9. **Feedback Loop:** The output position is sensed by another encoder and fed back to be subtracted from the reference. *(0.5 Mark)*

**Part (b) — Sensor Calculations (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Range: 0 °C to 200 °C
- Output: 1 V at 0 °C, 9 V at 200 °C
- At 100 °C: True output = 5 V, Actual output = 5.2 V

**Step 1: Sensitivity (1.5 Marks)**

$$ K = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta T} = \frac{9 - 1}{200 - 0} = \frac{8}{200} = 0.04 \text{ V/°C} $$

**Step 2: Span (1.5 Marks)**

$$ S = V_{\max} - V_{\min} = 9 - 1 = 8 \text{ V} $$

**Step 3: Percentage Linearity Error (4 Marks)**

The ideal (straight-line) output at 100 °C is computed from the linear calibration:

$$ V_{\text{ideal}} = 1 + K \times 100 = 1 + 0.04 \times 100 = 5 \text{ V} $$

Maximum deviation $\vert \Delta V_{\max} \vert = \vert 5.2 - 5 \vert = 0.2 \text{ V}$

Full-scale output $V_{FS} = 8 \text{ V}$

$$ \%\text{Linearity} = \frac{\vert \Delta V_{\max} \vert}{V_{FS}} \times 100 = \frac{0.2}{8} \times 100 = 2.5\% $$

> **Final Answer:** Sensitivity = **0.04 V/°C**, Span = **8 V**, % Linearity Error = **2.5%**.

---

### Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Classification of Sensors (7 Marks):**

**(i) Based on Power Requirement (2 Marks):**
- **Active Sensors:** Self-generating — do not need external power. Produce their own voltage/current in response to the input. *Example: Thermocouple (Seebeck effect), Piezoelectric pressure sensor.*
- **Passive Sensors:** Externally excited — act as variable resistance, capacitance, or inductance. Need an external power supply. *Example: Thermistor, Strain gauge, LDR.*

**(ii) Based on Output Signal Type (2 Marks):**
- **Analog Sensors:** Output is a continuous function of the input. *Example: Thermocouple, LVDT, RTD.*
- **Digital Sensors:** Output is in discrete steps, usually binary pulses or a digital word. *Example: Optical encoder, Hall-effect switch with Schmitt trigger, digital temperature IC (DS18B20).*

**(iii) Based on Physical Variable Measured (3 Marks):**
- **Displacement/Position:** LVDT, Potentiometer, Rotary Encoder.
- **Velocity:** Tachogenerator, Optical Encoder (with differentiator).
- **Force/Pressure:** Strain gauge, Piezoelectric sensor.
- **Temperature:** Thermocouple, RTD, Thermistor, IC sensor.
- **Light:** Photodiode, Phototransistor, LDR, CCD array.
- **Proximity:** Inductive proximity sensor, Capacitive proximity sensor, Hall sensor.
- **Flow:** Turbine flowmeter, Orifice plate, Ultrasonic flowmeter.

**Part (b) — First-Order Step Response (7 Marks):**

**Derivation (4 Marks):**
For a first-order sensor subjected to a step input, the governing ODE is:

$$ \tau \frac{d\theta(t)}{dt} + \theta(t) = 0 $$

where $\theta(t) = T(t) - T_\infty$. Solving with initial condition $T(0) = T_0$:

$$ T(t) = T_\infty + (T_0 - T_\infty) \, e^{-t/\tau} $$

**Calculation (3 Marks):**

**Given:** $\tau = 8$ s, $T_0 = 25$ °C, $T_\infty = 75$ °C.

**(i) At $t = 8$ s = $\tau$:**

$$ T(8) = 75 + (25 - 75) \, e^{-8/8} = 75 + (-50) \, e^{-1} $$

$$ T(8) = 75 - 50 \times 0.3679 = 75 - 18.395 = 56.605 \text{ °C} $$

**(ii) At $t = 24$ s = $3\tau$:**

$$ T(24) = 75 + (-50) \, e^{-24/8} = 75 - 50 \, e^{-3} $$

$$ T(24) = 75 - 50 \times 0.0498 = 75 - 2.49 = 72.51 \text{ °C} $$

> **Final Answer:** $T(8\text{s}) \approx \mathbf{56.6 \text{ °C}}$ and $T(24\text{s}) \approx \mathbf{72.5 \text{ °C}}$. This confirms the rule of thumb: at $t=\tau$, the sensor reaches 63.2% of the final change; at $t=3\tau$, it has already reached 95% and is essentially settled.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. **Confusing Span and Range:** Range refers to the **input** axis (e.g., 0–200 °C); Span refers to the **output** axis (e.g., 1–9 V = 8 V span). Markers specifically test this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting the feedback path in block diagrams:** A mechatronic system without a feedback loop is incomplete. You will lose 1–2 marks if the arrow from output back to input is missing.
> 3. **Wrong units in sensitivity:** Always write sensitivity as a compound unit (e.g., **V/kPa**, not just V). Bare units invite mark deductions.
> 4. **Sign errors in error percentage:** Use the **absolute value** of the error in the numerator. A negative percentage error is meaningless.
> 5. **Forgetting initial conditions in step response derivations:** $T(0) = T_0$ is not automatic — you must state and use it explicitly.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Mechatronics = Mechanics + Electronics + Control + Computing** integrated at the *design* stage (not bolted on later).
- The term was coined by **Yasakawa Electric Corporation (1969)** in Japan.
- A mechatronic system is a **closed-loop system** with **six core elements**: sensor → signal conditioning → controller → actuator → mechanical plant → feedback.
- **Sensors** are classified as **Active (self-generating)** or **Passive (externally excited)**.
- **Static characteristics** of sensors: Range, Span, Sensitivity, Linearity, Hysteresis, Repeatability, Drift, Dead Zone, Resolution.
- **Dynamic characteristics** are described by **first-order** ($\tau$) or **second-order** ($\omega_n, \zeta$) system models.
- **First-order step response:** $T(t) = T_\infty + (T_0 - T_\infty) e^{-t/\tau}$. Sensor reaches **63.2%** of final value at $t = \tau$ and **99.3%** at $t = 5\tau$.
- **Second-order response** is governed by **damping ratio** $\zeta$: underdamped (oscillates), critically damped (fastest, no overshoot), overdamped (sluggish).
- **Sensitivity** = $dy/dx$ — a high sensitivity does **not** always mean a good sensor; linearity and bandwidth matter equally.
- **Accuracy** is closeness to truth; **Precision** is repeatability. A sensor can be precise without being accurate.
- Always **clearly show the feedback path** in any mechatronic system diagram — it is a board-exam favourite.
- Common industrial sensors: **LVDT** (displacement), **RTD/Thermocouple** (temperature), **Strain Gauge** (force), **Tachogenerator** (speed), **MEMS Accelerometer** (acceleration).
- Common actuators: **DC/Servo/Stepper motor** (rotary motion), **Solenoid** (linear short-stroke), **Hydraulic/Pneumatic cylinder** (high-force linear).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Strategy for KTU:** Read the **block diagram in Section 4.1**, the **formula cheat sheet in Section 2.3**, and the **solved numericals in Section 3.3 and Part B of Section 5**. These four items cover roughly 80% of marks asked in any KTU Module 1 mechatronics exam paper.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET745 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Mechatronics, **Characteristics** of a system (or its sub-systems like sensors, actuators, and controllers) describe the **defining quantitative and qualitative features** that govern how the system behaves under static (steady) and dynamic (time-varying) input conditions. They are broadly classified into:

- **Static Characteristics** — Behavior when the input is either constant or varies very slowly such that the system reaches steady-state equilibrium.
- **Dynamic Characteristics** — Behavior when the input changes rapidly with time, before the system reaches steady-state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus (Module 1):** Static and dynamic characteristics of measurement systems, sensors, and actuators form a **HIGH-PRIORITY** topic. Questions of up to **14 marks** are routinely asked, typically combining theoretical definitions with first-order and second-order system analysis.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are testing a **digital bathroom weighing scale** (a mechatronic sensor system):

- **Static characteristics** describe its behavior when you stand *still* on it — how close the reading is to your true weight (accuracy), whether it gives the same reading if you step off and step on again (repeatability), whether it reads 0 kg when empty (zero offset), etc.
- **Dynamic characteristics** describe what happens when you *jump on* the scale — how fast the display settles to your weight (response time), whether it overshoots before settling (damping), and how quickly it can track a changing weight.

So, **static = steady-state behavior**, **dynamic = transient behavior over time**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following metrics are universally applied (SI units unless stated):

| Metric | Standard Unit | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Sensitivity | V/unit of measurand | $K$ |
| Time Constant | seconds (s) | $\tau$ |
| Natural Frequency | rad/s | $\omega_n$ |
| Damping Ratio | dimensionless | $\zeta$ |
| Resolution | unit of measurand | — |
| Hysteresis | % of Full Scale (FS) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** For an optimally designed second-order measurement system, the damping ratio $\zeta$ is targeted between **0.6 and 0.7** to achieve a fast, well-damped response with minimal overshoot (typically < 5%).

### 1.4 Classification Overview

$$
\text{Characteristics} = \underbrace{\text{Static Characteristics}}_{\text{Steady-state behavior}} \cup \underbrace{\text{Dynamic Characteristics}}_{\text{Transient behavior}}
$$

- **Static:** Accuracy, Precision, Sensitivity, Linearity, Hysteresis, Dead Zone, Resolution, Threshold, Repeatability, Drift, Span, Range.
- **Dynamic:** First-order response (time constant $\tau$), Second-order response ($\omega_n, \zeta$), Bandwidth, Rise time, Settling time, Overshoot.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Static Characteristics — Detailed Logic

A measurement system is represented as:

$$
y = f(x)
$$

where $x$ is the input (measurand) and $y$ is the output (measurement). For an *ideal* system, $y = Kx$ (purely linear with constant gain $K$).

#### 2.1.1 Accuracy
- **Definition:** Closeness of the measured value to the **true value**.
- **How:** Computed as $\text{Accuracy} = 1 - \dfrac{\vert x_m - x_t \vert}{\vert x_t \vert}$, where $x_m$ = measured, $x_t$ = true.
- **Why:** Indicates systematic correctness of the instrument.

#### 2.1.2 Precision
- **Definition:** Closeness of repeated measurements to **each other** (reproducibility).
- **How:** Determined statistically via standard deviation $\sigma$.
- **Why:** Indicates random error magnitude.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High Precision $\neq$ High Accuracy.** A precise instrument can still have a systematic bias and thus be inaccurate (imagine a misaligned weighing scale reading 60.0, 60.1, 60.0 for a true 70 kg — precise but not accurate).

#### 2.1.3 Sensitivity ($K$)
- **Definition:** Ratio of change in output to change in input at a steady state.
- **How:** $K = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$.
- **Why:** Determines the smallest detectable change in input.

#### 2.1.4 Linearity
- **Definition:** Degree to which the actual calibration curve conforms to a straight line.
- **How:** Linearity error (in %FS) $= \dfrac{\max \vert y_{\text{actual}} - y_{\text{ideal line}} \vert}{y_{\text{FS}}} \times 100\%$.

#### 2.1.5 Hysteresis
- **Definition:** Difference in output for the *same* input depending on whether the input is increasing or decreasing.
- **How:** Hysteresis error $= \dfrac{\vert y_{\text{increasing}} - y_{\text{decreasing}} \vert_{\max}}{y_{\text{FS}}} \times 100\%$.

#### 2.1.6 Dead Zone / Threshold
- **Dead Zone:** Range of input values for which there is **no change** in output.
- **Threshold:** Smallest input change that produces a detectable output change.

#### 2.1.7 Resolution
- **Definition:** Smallest *discernible* increment in the measured quantity.
- **Why:** Critical in digital systems — determined by the least significant bit (LSB) of the ADC.

#### 2.1.8 Repeatability
- **Definition:** Ability of the system to produce the same output for the same input, under identical conditions, *over multiple trials*.

#### 2.1.9 Drift
- **Definition:** Slow change of output with time *while the input is held constant*.
- **Types:** Zero drift (no input) and Span/Sensitivity drift (at full input).

#### 2.1.10 Span and Range
- **Span:** Difference between maximum and minimum calibrated input values.
- **Range:** Output variation corresponding to the span (e.g., 0–10 V for a 0–100 °C sensor).

### 2.2 Dynamic Characteristics — Detailed Logic

When inputs vary rapidly, the system is described by a **differential equation**. The two most important canonical forms in mechatronics are:

#### 2.2.1 First-Order System
- **Governing ODE:**

$$
\tau \dfrac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K\, x(t)
$$

- $\tau$ = **time constant** (seconds), $K$ = static sensitivity.
- **Standard step response** (for $x(t) = u(t)$, zero initial conditions):

$$
y(t) = K\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)
$$

- At $t = \tau$: $y = 0.632\, K$ (i.e., 63.2% of final value).
- At $t = 4\tau$ and $t = 5\tau$: system is ~98.2% and ~99.3% settled.

#### 2.2.2 Second-Order System
- **Governing ODE:**

$$
\dfrac{1}{\omega_n^2}\dfrac{d^2 y(t)}{dt^2} + \dfrac{2\zeta}{\omega_n}\dfrac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K\, x(t)
$$

- $\omega_n$ = **natural (undamped) frequency** (rad/s).
- $\zeta$ = **damping ratio** (dimensionless).
- **Step Response Regimes:**
  - $\zeta = 0$: Undamped, pure oscillation at $\omega_n$.
  - $0 < \zeta < 1$: Underdamped (oscillatory decay).
  - $\zeta = 1$: Critically damped (fastest non-oscillatory).
  - $\zeta > 1$: Overdamped (slow, no oscillation).

- **Damped natural frequency:** $\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$.
- **Percent overshoot:** $M_p = 100 \cdot e^{-\zeta \pi / \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \, \%$.
- **Settling time (2% criterion):** $t_s \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sensitivity (static) | $K = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ | Output unit / Input unit | Slope of calibration line |
| 2 | Linearity error | $\dfrac{\max \vert y_a - y_i \vert}{y_{\text{FS}}} \times 100$ | %FS | Use $\vert$ or $\mid$, never $\vert \vert$ in row |
| 3 | Hysteresis error | $\dfrac{\max(y_{\uparrow} - y_{\downarrow})}{y_{\text{FS}}} \times 100$ | %FS | Up vs down strokes |
| 4 | First-order step response | $y(t) = K(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | output units | $\tau$ = time constant |
| 5 | First-order steady-state | $y_{\text{ss}} = K x$ | output units | After $t \to \infty$ |
| 6 | First-order freq. response | $\dfrac{Y(j\omega)}{X(j\omega)} = \dfrac{K}{1 + j\omega \tau}$ | — | Magnitude $= \dfrac{K}{\sqrt{1+(\omega\tau)^2}}$ |
| 7 | Second-order natural freq. | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | rad/s | $k$ stiffness, $m$ mass |
| 8 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$ | dimensionless | $c$ = damping coeff. |
| 9 | % Overshoot | $M_p = 100\, e^{-\zeta \pi / \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | % | Valid for $0 < \zeta < 1$ |
| 10 | Settling time (2%) | $t_s \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$ | s | Engineering approx. |
| 11 | Rise time (approx.) | $t_r \approx \dfrac{1.8}{\omega_n}$ | s | For $\zeta \approx 0.6$ |
| 12 | Bandwidth (approx.) | $\text{BW} \approx \omega_n$ (for $\zeta \approx 0.7$) | rad/s | Useful for sensor selection |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sensor Selection (e.g., accelerometer in airbag system):** A high $\omega_n$ is essential so the sensor can faithfully track the millisecond-scale crash pulse.
- **Actuator Design (e.g., robotic arm joint):** A well-damped ($\zeta \approx 0.7$) response minimizes oscillation, ensuring precise positioning.
- **Process Control (e.g., CNC temperature control):** Drift and hysteresis determine long-term calibration stability.
- **Automotive Mechatronics (e.g., ABS wheel-speed sensor):** Bandwidth must exceed the wheel rotation frequency to avoid lag-induced skidding.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU ESE (End Semester Examination), examiners often present a sensor and ask for both its static and dynamic behavior. Mastery of this dual perspective is **mandatory** for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: First-Order Step Response

Consider the canonical first-order ODE:

$$
\tau \dfrac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K\, u(t)
$$

with initial condition $y(0) = 0$ and unit step input $u(t) = 1$ for $t \geq 0$.

**Step 1 — Take the Laplace Transform** (using $\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = \frac{1}{s}$):

$$
\tau\, s\, Y(s) + Y(s) = \dfrac{K}{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Factor and isolate $Y(s)$:**

$$
Y(s)\bigl[\tau s + 1\bigr] = \dfrac{K}{s}
$$

$$
Y(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(\tau s + 1)}
$$

**Step 3 — Partial Fraction Expansion:**

Let $\dfrac{K}{s(\tau s + 1)} = \dfrac{A}{s} + \dfrac{B}{\tau s + 1}$.

Multiplying through:

$$
K = A(\tau s + 1) + B\, s
$$

Equating coefficients:
- Constant term: $K = A \Rightarrow A = K$.
- Coefficient of $s$: $0 = A\tau + B \Rightarrow B = -K\tau$.

Thus:

$$
Y(s) = \dfrac{K}{s} - \dfrac{K\tau}{\tau s + 1} = \dfrac{K}{s} - \dfrac{K}{s + 1/\tau}
$$

**Step 4 — Inverse Laplace Transform:**

$$
y(t) = K\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right), \quad t \geq 0
$$

**Step 5 — Key Time-Domain Checkpoints:**

- At $t = 0$: $y(0) = 0$. ✔
- At $t = \tau$: $y(\tau) = K(1 - e^{-1}) \approx 0.632\, K$. ✔
- As $t \to \infty$: $y \to K$ (steady state). ✔

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Second-Order Step Response (Underdamped Case)

Governing ODE:

$$
\dfrac{d^2 y}{dt^2} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dfrac{dy}{dt} + \omega_n^2 y = K\omega_n^2\, u(t)
$$

**Step 1 — Laplace Transform** (zero IC, unit step):

$$
s^2 Y(s) + 2\zeta\omega_n s Y(s) + \omega_n^2 Y(s) = \dfrac{K\omega_n^2}{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Characteristic Equation:**

$$
s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Roots:**

$$
s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm j\omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2} = -\sigma \pm j\omega_d
$$

where $\sigma = \zeta\omega_n$ (decay rate) and $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ (damped natural frequency).

**Step 4 — Standard Underdamped Step Response:**

$$
y(t) = K\left[1 - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\, e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\, \sin\!\left(\omega_d t + \phi\right)\right]
$$

with phase angle:

$$
\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}{\zeta}\right) = \cos^{-1}(\zeta)
$$

**Step 5 — Derivation of % Overshoot:**

The peak occurs at $t_p = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_d}$. Substituting $t = t_p$ into the response (and noting $\sin(\pi + \phi) = -\sin\phi$):

$$
y(t_p) = K\left[1 + e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\right]
$$

Thus the **percent overshoot** is:

$$
M_p = \dfrac{y(t_p) - y_{\text{ss}}}{y_{\text{ss}}} \times 100\% = 100\cdot e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\, \%
$$

This result is **independent of $\omega_n$** — it is governed solely by $\zeta$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A temperature sensor is modeled as a first-order system with time constant $\tau = 4$ s and sensitivity $K = 0.1$ V/°C. The ambient temperature suddenly rises from 25 °C to 50 °C at $t = 0$. Find the sensor output at $t = 4$ s and the time to reach 95% of final value.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Step input magnitude:
$$
\Delta T = 50 - 25 = 25\ ^\circ\text{C}
$$

Step 2 — Final output:
$$
y_{\text{ss}} = K\,\Delta T = 0.1 \times 25 = 2.5\ \text{V}
$$

Step 3 — Output at $t = 4$ s:
$$
y(4) = 2.5 \left(1 - e^{-4/4}\right) = 2.5(1 - e^{-1}) = 2.5(0.6321) = 1.580\ \text{V}
$$

Step 4 — Time to reach 95%:
$$
0.95 = 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \implies e^{-t/\tau} = 0.05
$$
$$
t = -\tau \ln(0.05) = -4 \times (-2.996) = 11.98\ \text{s} \approx 12\ \text{s}
$$

**Answer:** $y(4) = 1.58$ V, $t_{95\%} \approx 12$ s.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Static vs. Dynamic Characteristics

| Feature | Static Characteristics | Dynamic Characteristics |
|---|---|---|
| Input condition | Constant or quasi-static | Time-varying, fast |
| Output of interest | Steady-state value | Transient response |
| Key metrics | Accuracy, Sensitivity, Linearity, Hysteresis | $\tau$, $\omega_n$, $\zeta$, $M_p$, $t_s$ |
| Governing math | Algebraic $y = f(x)$ | Differential equation |
| Test signal | Step (held for long time) | Step / impulse / sine sweep |
| Typical exam weight | 4–7 marks | 7–14 marks |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Generalized Measurement System

The diagram below maps the **flow of information** through a typical mechatronic sensor system, with the static and dynamic characteristics annotated at their relevant stages.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Measurand X Physical Quantity"] --> B["Sensor Element Transducer"]
    B --> C["Signal Conditioning Amplifier Filter"]
    C --> D["ADC Conversion Stage"]
    D --> E["Digital Output Y Display or Controller"]
    F["Static Characteristics"] -.-> B
    G["Dynamic Characteristics"] -.-> B
    H["Noise Interference"] -.-> C
    I["Quantization Error"] -.-> D
    F --> J["Accuracy Linearity Hysteresis Sensitivity Resolution Drift"]
    G --> K["Time Constant Natural Frequency Damping Ratio Bandwidth"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dashed arrows (`-.->`) indicate that static and dynamic characteristics are **properties of the sensor element itself**, not external influences like noise or quantization error.

---

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Static vs. Dynamic Property Mapping

This block-level architecture illustrates the **classification hierarchy** of characteristics inside a mechatronic system.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M["Characteristics of Mechatronic System"]
    M --> S["Static Characteristics Steady State"]
    M --> D["Dynamic Characteristics Transient State"]
    S --> S1["Accuracy and Precision"]
    S --> S2["Sensitivity K and Linearity"]
    S --> S3["Hysteresis and Dead Zone"]
    S --> S4["Resolution Repeatability Drift"]
    D --> D1["First Order System Time Constant Tau"]
    D --> D2["Second Order System Omega_n and Zeta"]
    D --> D3["Performance Indices Rise Settle Overshoot"]
    D --> D4["Frequency Response Bandwidth Magnitude Phase"]
```

---

### 4.3 Step Response Comparison Chart (Conceptual)

The figure below compares the **step responses** of first-order and underdamped second-order systems to illustrate damping effect.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X["Step Input at t=0 Amplitude 1"] --> FO["First Order System Tau=1 sec"]
    X --> SO["Second Order System Omega_n=3 Zeta=0.4"]
    FO --> FOout["Smooth Exponential Rise 63.2 percent at t=Tau No Overshoot"]
    SO --> SOout["Oscillatory Rise Peak Above 1 Damped Oscillations Settle at 1"]
```

---

### 4.4 Sensor Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"Is input constant or slowly varying"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> A1["Focus on Static Characteristics Accuracy Linearity Hysteresis"]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Does input have high frequency components"}
    Q2 -- Yes --> A2["Focus on Dynamic Characteristics Bandwidth must exceed input frequency"]
    Q2 -- No --> A3["Both Static and Dynamic must be considered"]
    A1 --> R["Choose Sensor with Required Specs"]
    A2 --> R
    A3 --> R
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)**

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the following terms with respect to a measurement system: (i) Sensitivity, (ii) Hysteresis, (iii) Resolution.**

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Sensitivity** is defined as the ratio of the change in output to the corresponding change in input producing it. Mathematically, $K = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$. A highly sensitive instrument produces a large output for a small change in input.

(ii) **Hysteresis** is the maximum difference in output obtained when the input is varied first in one direction (increasing) and then in the opposite direction (decreasing), for the same value of input. It is usually expressed as a percentage of full-scale output.

(iii) **Resolution** is the smallest measurable increment of the input quantity that produces a detectable change in output. In digital systems, it is determined by the LSB of the ADC: $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{\text{Full-scale input}}{2^n}$, where $n$ is the number of bits.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Distinguish between accuracy and precision in a measurement system with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Accuracy | Precision |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Closeness of measured value to the **true value** | Closeness of repeated measurements to **each other** |
| Quantified by | Error = (Measured – True) | Standard deviation $\sigma$ |
| Affected by | Systematic errors (calibration, bias) | Random errors (noise, repeatability) |
| Example | A voltmeter reads 10.1 V when the true value is 10.0 V — accurate | A voltmeter reads 9.4, 9.5, 9.4 V repeatedly when true is 10.0 V — precise but not accurate |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often use the terms interchangeably. Examiners *will* deduct marks for failing to differentiate them explicitly. Always state that an instrument can be **precise without being accurate** (e.g., due to a zero-offset error).

---

### **Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **static characteristics of a measurement system**. Define any **five** static characteristics in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **thermocouple-based temperature sensor** has a first-order dynamic response with time constant $\tau = 5$ s and static sensitivity $K = 0.05$ V/°C. The sensor is initially at 30 °C. At $t = 0$, the temperature suddenly changes to 80 °C. **(7 marks)**
- (i) Write the governing differential equation.
- (ii) Derive and plot (qualitatively) the step response.
- (iii) Find the sensor output at $t = 5$ s, $t = 10$ s, and at steady state.
- (iv) Find the time required to reach 98% of the final value.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Static characteristics describe the steady-state performance of a measurement system when the input is either constant or varies so slowly that dynamic effects are negligible. The block diagram of a generalized measurement chain is:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Measurand Physical Input"] --> B["Primary Sensor Element"]
    B --> C["Variable Conversion Element"]
    C --> D["Variable Manipulation Element"]
    D --> E["Data Transmission Element"]
    E --> F["Data Presentation Element Output"]
```

The five static characteristics are:

1. **Accuracy:** Closeness of the measured value to the true value. Quantified as $\text{Accuracy} = 1 - \dfrac{|x_m - x_t|}{|x_t|}$.

2. **Sensitivity:** Ratio $\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$. Determines the smallest detectable input change.

3. **Linearity:** Conformity of the actual output-input curve to a straight line. Linearity error = $\dfrac{\max|y_{\text{actual}} - y_{\text{ideal line}}|}{y_{\text{FS}}} \times 100\%$.

4. **Hysteresis:** Difference in output for the same input reached from opposite directions. Caused by friction, backlash, or magnetic effects.

5. **Drift:** Slow change of output with time for a constant input. Includes zero drift and span drift.

---

**(b)** Given: $\tau = 5$ s, $K = 0.05$ V/°C, $T_i = 30$ °C, $T_f = 80$ °C.

**(i) Governing ODE:**

$$
\tau \dfrac{dy}{dt} + y = K\,T(t)
$$

$$
5\dfrac{dy}{dt} + y = 0.05\, T(t)
$$

**(ii) Step Response Derivation:**

Step input: $\Delta T = 50$ °C, $y(0) = 0$ (assuming zero-initial output offset).

Laplace domain:

$$
Y(s) = \dfrac{K \Delta T}{s(\tau s + 1)} = \dfrac{0.05 \times 50}{s(5s + 1)} = \dfrac{2.5}{s(5s + 1)}
$$

Inverse Laplace:

$$
y(t) = 2.5\left(1 - e^{-t/5}\right)\ \text{V}, \quad t \geq 0
$$

Qualitative plot: exponential rise from 0 V, asymptotically approaching 2.5 V, with 63.2% (1.58 V) at $t = 5$ s.

**(iii) Specific Values:**

At $t = 5$ s:
$$
y(5) = 2.5(1 - e^{-1}) = 2.5 \times 0.6321 = 1.58\ \text{V}
$$

At $t = 10$ s:
$$
y(10) = 2.5(1 - e^{-2}) = 2.5 \times 0.8647 = 2.16\ \text{V}
$$

At steady state ($t \to \infty$):
$$
y_{\text{ss}} = 2.5\ \text{V}
$$

**(iv) Time for 98% response:**

$$
0.98 = 1 - e^{-t/5} \implies e^{-t/5} = 0.02
$$
$$
t = -5\ln(0.02) = -5 \times (-3.912) = 19.56\ \text{s}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the **dynamic characteristics of a second-order measurement system**. Derive the expression for **percent overshoot** in terms of the damping ratio. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A piezoelectric accelerometer used in vibration measurement can be modeled as a second-order system with $\omega_n = 2500$ rad/s and $\zeta = 0.3$. Determine: **(7 marks)**
- (i) The damped natural frequency $\omega_d$.
- (ii) The percent overshoot $M_p$ for a step input.
- (iii) The settling time $t_s$ (using 2% criterion).
- (iv) Comment on whether this sensor is suitable for measuring vibrations in the range 0–400 Hz.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *Dynamic Characteristics of a Second-Order System:*

A second-order system is governed by:

$$
\dfrac{1}{\omega_n^2}\dfrac{d^2 y}{dt^2} + \dfrac{2\zeta}{\omega_n}\dfrac{dy}{dt} + y = K\, x(t)
$$

Its step response depends on $\zeta$:
- $\zeta = 0$: Undamped, pure sinusoidal oscillation at $\omega_n$.
- $0 < \zeta < 1$: Underdamped — oscillatory decay.
- $\zeta = 1$: Critically damped — fastest non-oscillatory.
- $\zeta > 1$: Overdamped — slow, no oscillation.

**Derivation of % Overshoot:**

The standard underdamped unit-step response (from §3.2) is:

$$
y(t) = K\left[1 - \dfrac{e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\sin(\omega_d t + \phi)\right]
$$

with $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ and $\phi = \cos^{-1}(\zeta)$.

The first peak occurs at $t_p = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_d}$. Substituting and using $\sin(\pi + \phi) = -\sin\phi$:

$$
y(t_p) = K\left[1 + e^{-\zeta\omega_n \pi/\omega_d}\right] = K\left[1 + e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\right]
$$

Therefore:

$$
M_p = \dfrac{y(t_p) - y_{\text{ss}}}{y_{\text{ss}}} \times 100\% = 100 \cdot e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\,\%
$$

---

**(b)** Given: $\omega_n = 2500$ rad/s, $\zeta = 0.3$.

**(i) Damped natural frequency:**

$$
\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2} = 2500 \sqrt{1 - 0.09} = 2500 \times 0.9539 = 2384.8\ \text{rad/s}
$$

**(ii) Percent overshoot:**

$$
M_p = 100 \cdot e^{-0.3 \pi / \sqrt{1 - 0.09}} = 100 \cdot e^{-0.9425/0.9539} = 100 \cdot e^{-0.9880} = 37.2\%
$$

**(iii) Settling time (2% criterion):**

$$
t_s \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta \omega_n} = \dfrac{4}{0.3 \times 2500} = \dfrac{4}{750} = 0.00533\ \text{s} = 5.33\ \text{ms}
$$

**(iv) Suitability for 0–400 Hz vibration:**

Convert upper frequency: $f = 400$ Hz $\Rightarrow \omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 400 = 2513$ rad/s.

The sensor's natural frequency is $\omega_n = 2500$ rad/s, which is **less than** the input frequency 2513 rad/s. This means the sensor is operating **near or above its resonance**, leading to severe amplitude distortion (output magnitude amplifies non-uniformly). 

**Conclusion: NOT suitable.** For accurate measurement, the operating frequency should be at most $\approx 0.3\,\omega_n$ (i.e., below 750 rad/s ≈ 120 Hz). The chosen accelerometer must be redesigned with a higher $\omega_n$ (e.g., $\omega_n \geq 10 \times 2513 \approx 25{,}130$ rad/s, i.e., $\geq 4$ kHz).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Trap:** When computing frequency in Hz vs. rad/s, students frequently forget the $2\pi$ factor — losing **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Steady-State Assumption Trap:** In first-order problems, the sensor's *initial* output (corresponding to the initial temperature) must be **subtracted** before applying the step response formula. Many students skip this and end up with a $y(0) = K\Delta T$ which is wrong.
> 3. **Damping Regime Identification:** If $\zeta > 1$, the percent overshoot formula **does not apply**. Writing $M_p = 0$ for overdamped systems earns full credit; incorrectly plugging into the underdamped formula loses marks.
> 4. **Bandwidth vs. Natural Frequency:** Don't confuse the two. Bandwidth is the *operating* frequency up to which the sensor output remains within a tolerance (typically $\pm 3$ dB), not equal to $\omega_n$.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams:**

- ☐ **Static characteristics** are evaluated at steady state. Always quote the unit (typically **%FS** for errors, or V/unit for sensitivity).
- ☐ **Sensitivity** is the slope of the calibration curve. High sensitivity does **not** imply high accuracy.
- ☐ **Linearity** is preferred for ease of calibration; non-linearity requires look-up tables or polynomial compensation.
- ☐ **Hysteresis** is energy-loss related (friction, magnetic domains). It is *always* a non-linear error.
- ☐ **Drift** is time-dependent. Always mention *zero drift* vs. *span drift* explicitly.
- ☐ A **first-order system** is fully described by **two parameters**: static gain $K$ and time constant $\tau$. The step response is $y(t) = K(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$.
- ☐ The **63.2% rule** ($y = 0.632K$ at $t = \tau$) is the fastest way to identify $\tau$ from an experimental response curve.
- ☐ A **second-order system** requires **three parameters**: $K$, $\omega_n$, and $\zeta$. Knowing $\zeta$ alone tells you the **shape**; knowing $\omega_n$ alone tells you the **speed**.
- ☐ **Percent overshoot $M_p$ depends ONLY on $\zeta$** — not on $\omega_n$. Memorize: $\zeta = 0.7 \Rightarrow M_p \approx 4.6\%$, $\zeta = 0.5 \Rightarrow M_p \approx 16.3\%$, $\zeta = 0.3 \Rightarrow M_p \approx 37.2\%$.
- ☐ **Settling time** $t_s \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta\omega_n}$ is **inversely proportional** to both $\zeta$ and $\omega_n$. Increase either to settle faster.
- ☐ For a measurement sensor, the **operational bandwidth** must be **at most** $\approx 0.3 \omega_n$ to avoid resonance amplification.
- ☐ Always state **assumptions** in derivations: zero initial conditions, ideal step input, linear time-invariant (LTI) system.
- ☐ Use $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ or $\mathrm{abs}(\cdot)$ for absolute value in written solutions to avoid markdown table breakage.
- ☐ Examiner reward: **clear diagrams, labeled axes, units in every numerical answer, and proper rounding to 2–3 significant digits.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temperature, Flow, and Pressure Sensors; Displacement, Position, and Proximity Sensing by Magnetic, Optical, Ultrasonic, Inductive, Capacitive, and Eddy Current Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET745 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Temperature, Flow, and Pressure Sensors; Displacement, Position, and Proximity Sensing by Magnetic, Optical, Ultrasonic, Inductive, Capacitive, and Eddy Current Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Sensors in Mechatronics — The Foundational Role

In Mechatronics, a **sensor** is a physical device that detects a physical, chemical, or biological stimulus (measurand) and converts it into a usable electrical signal. Per the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus, sensors form the *sensory layer* of any mechatronic system, feeding data to the **Signal Conditioning → Controller → Actuator** chain. Without accurate sensing, closed-loop feedback control is impossible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Examination Standard):**
> A *sensor* is a device that responds to a physical stimulus (such as heat, light, sound, pressure, magnetism, or motion) and transmits the resulting impulse for measurement and/or control of an associated mechanism. A *transducer* is the broader term — any device that converts energy from one form to another. **All sensors are transducers, but not all transducers are sensors.**

### 1.2 Categorical Snapshot of Module 1 Sensors

| Measurand | Primary Sensor Classes |
|---|---|
| **Temperature** | Thermocouple, RTD (Pt100), Thermistor (NTC/PTC), IC sensor (LM35) |
| **Flow** | Turbine flowmeter, Orifice + Differential Pressure, Ultrasonic, Coriolis |
| **Pressure** | Strain-gauge, Capacitive, Piezoelectric, Bourdon tube (mechanical) |
| **Displacement/Position/Proximity** | Hall-effect (Magnetic), LVDT (Inductive), Eddy current, Capacitive, Optical (Encoder), Ultrasonic |

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Human Body"

Think of a mechatronic system as the human body:

- **Sensors** = your *nervous system* (skin feels heat, eyes see light, ears hear sound).
- **Controllers** = your *brain* (decides what to do).
- **Actuators** = your *muscles* (execute the action).

Just as your skin has different nerve endings for pressure, heat, and pain, a mechatronic system requires *different specialized sensors* for temperature, flow, pressure, and position. This is why a single "universal sensor" does not exist — physics dictates the design choice.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Constants to Memorize:**
> - Absolute zero = **0 K = –273.15 °C**
> - Standard Reference Temperature for thermocouples = **0 °C (Ice Point)**
> - Pt100 RTD resistance at 0 °C = **100 Ω exactly**
> - Hall coefficient for standard InSb = –0.0001 m³/C (approx.)
> - Speed of sound in air (20 °C) = **343 m/s**
> - Permittivity of free space ε₀ = **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**

---

## 1.4 Temperature Sensors — Conceptual Overview

Temperature is one of the most measured parameters in industry. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes four dominant electrical temperature sensors, each based on a different physical principle.

### A. Thermocouple (Seebeck Effect)
- **Principle:** Two dissimilar metals joined at a junction produce an EMF proportional to temperature difference (Seebeck Effect, discovered 1821).
- **Intuition:** Hold a copper wire and an iron wire twisted together at one end, then heat the twisted end — a small DC voltage appears across the cold ends. That tiny voltage *is* the temperature signal.
- **Range:** –270 °C to **+2300 °C** (widest of all sensors).

### B. RTD — Resistance Temperature Detector
- **Principle:** The electrical resistance of pure metal (typically platinum) increases linearly with temperature.
- **Intuition:** As the wire heats up, atoms vibrate more, blocking electron flow — resistance rises predictably.
- **Range:** –200 °C to **+850 °C**. **Pt100** is the industrial standard (100 Ω at 0 °C).

### C. Thermistor (Thermal Resistor)
- **Principle:** Semiconductor oxides (Mn, Ni, Co) exhibit large non-linear resistance change with temperature.
- **Types:** **NTC** (resistance decreases with ↑T) and **PTC** (resistance increases sharply after a Curie point).
- **Range:** –55 °C to **+150 °C**.

### D. IC Temperature Sensor (LM35, AD590)
- **Principle:** Bandgap reference circuit produces a current or voltage proportional to absolute temperature.
- **Intuition:** The transistor's V_BE voltage changes at exactly –2 mV/°C — clever circuitry amplifies this into a clean linear output.
- **Range:** –55 °C to **+150 °C**. Excellent linearity, low cost, embedded in microcontrollers.

---

## 1.5 Flow Sensors — Conceptual Overview

Flow is the volume or mass of fluid passing per unit time. Direct mechanical measurement is impossible, so flow sensors infer flow via *secondary effects*.

### A. Turbine Flowmeter
- A multi-bladed rotor sits in the flow stream. Fluid strikes blades causing rotation; an RF pickup counts blade passes.
- *Intuition:* Like a tiny pinwheel in a stream — each spin = a known volume passed.

### B. Orifice Plate (Differential Pressure Flowmeter)
- A constriction in the pipe causes a pressure drop. By **Bernoulli's principle**, flow ∝ √ΔP.
- *Intuition:* Put your thumb over a hose — pressure builds behind it; flow at the nozzle rises.

### C. Ultrasonic Flowmeter
- Uses transit-time difference of sound waves traveling *with* and *against* the flow (Doppler or transit-time principle).
- *Intuition:* Like a swimmer — swimming upstream is slower than downstream.

### D. Coriolis Mass Flowmeter
- The flow tube is vibrated; the fluid's inertia twists the tube proportionally to **mass flow rate**.
- *Intuition:* Like swinging a bucket of water — the water resists changing direction, and that resistance *is* the measurement.

---

## 1.6 Pressure Sensors — Conceptual Overview

Pressure (P = F/A) is fundamental in hydraulics, pneumatics, and process control. The KTU syllabus focuses on **electrical** pressure transducers.

### A. Strain-Gauge Pressure Sensor
- A diaphragm deflects under pressure; strain gauges bonded to it change resistance (∆R/R ∝ strain ∝ pressure).
- *Intuition:* Press your hand on a rubber sheet with wires glued on it — the wires stretch, resistance changes.

### B. Capacitive Pressure Sensor
- Pressure deflects a diaphragm, changing the gap *d* of a parallel-plate capacitor.
- *Intuition:* Like a tuning fork's capacitor — push the plates closer, capacitance rises sharply.

### C. Piezoelectric Pressure Sensor
- Certain crystals (quartz, PZT) generate charge when stressed — dynamic-only, ideal for shockwaves and vibration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Transducer Classification:**
> - **Active sensors** generate their own voltage/current (thermocouple, piezoelectric, photovoltaic).
> - **Passive sensors** require external excitation (RTD, thermistor, strain gauge, LVDT).
> This classification is a **favourite KTU short-answer (Part A 3-mark) question.**

---

## 1.7 Displacement, Position & Proximity Sensors — Conceptual Overview

These sensors detect the presence, absence, location, or distance of an object **without physical contact** (non-contact) or with minimal contact. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists **six sensing principles**:

| Principle | Physical Law Exploited | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Magnetic (Hall Effect, Reed Switch)** | Lorentz force on charge carriers / Reed contact | 0–few cm |
| **Optical (Photoelectric, Laser, Encoder)** | Photon absorption / interruption | mm–hundreds of m |
| **Ultrasonic** | Sound wave reflection (echo ranging) | cm–10 m |
| **Inductive (LVDT, Proximity)** | Mutual inductance | 0.1–50 mm |
| **Capacitive** | Capacitance change with gap/area/dielectric | 0.1–50 mm |
| **Eddy Current** | Induced eddy currents in target | 0.1–80 mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **"Proximity" vs "Displacement"** — A *proximity sensor* gives a binary "near/far" output (presence detection). A *displacement sensor* gives a *continuous* analog output proportional to position. Some sensors (LVDT, optical encoder) can do both.

---

## 1.8 Why This Matters in Mechatronics

Every modern robot, CNC machine, ABS brake, smartphone (accelerometer + proximity), and HVAC system relies on at least one of these sensor classes. The **sensor choice dictates system accuracy, response time, cost, and reliability** — making this Module 1 topic the *bedrock* on which all of mechatronic design is built.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermocouple — Deep Theory

### Operating Principle: Seebeck Effect
When two dissimilar conductors A and B are joined at one junction (hot junction, T_H) and the other ends are kept at a reference temperature T_C, an EMF **E_AB** is generated:

**E_AB = α(T_H – T_C) + β(T_H² – T_C²) + ...**

where α, β are Seebeck coefficients of the thermocouple pair.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The cold (reference) junction must be maintained at a known temperature — modern systems use **Cold Junction Compensation (CJC)** with an IC sensor like LM35 to electronically simulate a 0 °C reference.

### Standard Thermocouple Types (KTU must-know)

| Type | Materials | Range (°C) | Sensitivity (µV/°C) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **K** | Chromel–Alumel | –270 to +1370 | ~41 |
| **J** | Iron–Constantan | –210 to +1200 | ~50 |
| **T** | Copper–Constantan | –270 to +400 | ~43 |
| **R/S** | Pt–Rh / Pt | –50 to +1768 | ~10 |
| **E** | Chromel–Constantan | –270 to +1000 | ~68 |
| **B** | Pt-Rh / Pt-Rh | 0 to +1820 | ~10 |

**Thermopile**: Series connection of many thermocouples to amplify the small EMF — used in non-contact IR thermometers.

---

## 2.2 RTD — Deep Theory

### Callendar–Van Dusen Equation (Industrial Standard)

$$R_T = R_0 \left[1 + A T + B T^2 + C (T - 100) T^3 \right]$$

For T ≥ 0 °C:

$$R_T = R_0 (1 + A T + B T^2)$$

For T < 0 °C, the cubic term activates.

### Constants for Platinum (IEC 60751)
- **A = 3.9083 × 10⁻³ °C⁻¹**
- **B = –5.775 × 10⁻⁷ °C⁻²**
- **C = –4.183 × 10⁻¹² °C⁻⁴**

### Temperature Coefficient (α)

$$\alpha = \frac{R_{100} - R_0}{100 \cdot R_0} = 0.003851 \text{ °C}^{-1} \text{ for Pt}$$

### Simplified Linear Approximation (Engineering Use)

$$R_T \approx R_0 (1 + \alpha T)$$

This holds to within ±0.4 % from 0–100 °C.

---

## 2.3 Thermistor — Deep Theory

### Steinhart–Hart Equation (Most Accurate Model)

$$\frac{1}{T} = A + B \ln(R) + C [\ln(R)]^3$$

where T is in Kelvin.

### B-Parameter (Simplified 2-coefficient) Equation

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$

where:
- R_T = resistance at temperature T (K)
- R_0 = resistance at reference temperature T_0 (K), typically 298.15 K (25 °C)
- B = β-coefficient (typically 3000–5000 K)

---

## 2.4 IC Temperature Sensor — Deep Theory

### LM35 Output Equation

$$V_{out} = 10 \text{ mV/°C} \times T \quad (T \text{ in °C})$$

At 25 °C → V_out = 250 mV. At 100 °C → V_out = 1000 mV = 1.000 V.

### AD590 Output Equation

$$I_{out} = 1 \text{ µA/K} \times T \quad (T \text{ in K})$$

At 273.15 K (0 °C) → I_out = 273.15 µA.

---

## 2.5 Flow Sensors — Deep Theory

### Orifice Plate (Bernoulli + Continuity)

For an incompressible fluid through an orifice:

$$Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]}}$$

where:
- Q = volumetric flow rate (m³/s)
- C_d = discharge coefficient (≈0.61–0.65 for sharp-edged orifice)
- A_1 = pipe cross-section, A_2 = orifice cross-section (m²)
- ΔP = pressure drop across orifice (Pa)
- ρ = fluid density (kg/m³)

For A₂ ≪ A₁ (simplified):

$$Q \approx C_d A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

### Turbine Flowmeter

$$Q = f \cdot V_{cal}$$

where f = pulse frequency (Hz) and V_cal = K-factor calibration volume (e.g., mL/pulse).

### Ultrasonic Transit-Time Flowmeter

$$\Delta t = t_{down} - t_{up} = \frac{2 L \cdot v}{c^2 - v^2}$$

For v ≪ c:

$$v \approx \frac{c^2}{2 L} \Delta t$$

### Coriolis Mass Flowmeter

$$m = \frac{F_C}{2 \omega L}$$

where F_C = Coriolis force, ω = vibration angular frequency, L = tube length.

---

## 2.6 Pressure Sensors — Deep Theory

### Strain Gauge Equation

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$$

where G = Gauge factor (≈2.0 for metal, ≈100+ for semiconductor), ε = strain (ΔL/L).

For a circular diaphragm under pressure P, the strain at radius r:

$$\varepsilon_r = \frac{3 P}{8 E t^2} (1 - \nu) (a^2 - 3 r^2)$$

where E = Young's modulus, ν = Poisson's ratio, t = diaphragm thickness, a = diaphragm radius.

### Capacitive Pressure Sensor

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

Pressure reduces gap *d* → capacitance rises. Sensitivity:

$$\frac{dC}{C} = -\frac{dd}{d}$$

### Piezoelectric Pressure (Charge Mode)

$$Q = d_{33} \cdot F = d_{33} \cdot P \cdot A$$

---

## 2.7 Displacement / Position / Proximity Sensors — Deep Theory

### A. Magnetic — Hall Effect Sensor
Hall voltage across a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field B:

$$V_H = \frac{I \cdot B}{n_e e t} = R_H \cdot \frac{I \cdot B}{t}$$

where:
- R_H = Hall coefficient
- I = current through sensor
- B = magnetic flux density (Tesla)
- t = thickness of sensing element
- n_e = charge carrier density

**Digital Hall switch** outputs logic HIGH/LOW when B exceeds a threshold. Used in BLDC motor commutation, smartphone lid detection, anti-pinch car windows.

**Reed Switch** — Two ferromagnetic reeds sealed in a glass envelope; B field pulls them together → contact closes (passive, no power needed).

### B. Inductive — LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)

LVDT is a transformer with one primary (P) and two identical secondaries (S₁, S₂) wound on a movable ferrite core.

**Mutual Inductance Equations:**

$$E_{S_1} = k \cdot x \quad ; \quad E_{S_2} = k \cdot (-x)$$

where k = sensitivity constant, x = core displacement from null.

**Differential Output:**

$$E_{out} = E_{S_1} - E_{S_2} = 2 k x$$

**Ratiometric Output (Averaging to remove temperature drift):**

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{E_{out}}{E_{S_1} + E_{S_2}} = \frac{x}{x_{max}}$$

LVDT range: ±0.1 mm to ±250 mm. Resolution: <0.1 µm. Linearity: ±0.25 %.

### C. Eddy Current Proximity Sensor

When a high-frequency AC coil is brought near a conductive target, **eddy currents** are induced in the target. These currents oppose the primary coil via Lenz's law, reducing the coil's inductance.

Inductance change:

$$L(x) = L_0 \left(1 - \frac{\pi r^2}{2 x^2}\right) \quad \text{(simplified)}$$

Output is a function of distance *x* — non-contact, sensitive to non-ferrous metals especially.

### D. Capacitive Proximity / Displacement Sensor

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} \quad \text{or} \quad C = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 L}{\ln(R/r)}$$

Can detect both metallic and non-metallic (plastic, wood, liquid) targets — a major advantage over inductive types.

### E. Optical Sensors
- **Through-beam**: Source + detector separated; object breaks the beam.
- **Retro-reflective**: Reflector returns beam; object blocks return.
- **Diffuse reflective**: Object *is* the reflector.
- **Optical Encoder**: Slotted disc + photodiode — pulses proportional to rotation angle/position.
- **Laser Triangulation**: Position-sensitive detector (PSD) senses reflected spot location → micro-resolution distance.

### F. Ultrasonic Proximity Sensor

Time-of-flight equation:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

where c = speed of sound in medium (343 m/s in air at 20 °C), t = round-trip echo time.

For c with temperature correction:

$$c = 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot T \quad \text{(T in °C, c in m/s)}$$

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Sensor / Concept | Key Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Thermocouple EMF | $E_{AB} = \alpha \Delta T$ | mV |
| 2 | RTD Resistance | $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$ | Ω |
| 3 | Callendar–Van Dusen | $R_T = R_0[1 + AT + BT^2]$ | Ω |
| 4 | Steinhart–Hart | $1/T = A + B\ln R + C(\ln R)^3$ | K⁻¹ |
| 5 | Thermistor (B-eqn) | $R_T = R_0 e^{B(1/T - 1/T_0)}$ | Ω |
| 6 | LM35 Output | $V = 10\text{ mV/°C} \times T$ | V |
| 7 | AD590 Output | $I = 1\text{ µA/K} \times T$ | µA |
| 8 | Orifice Flow | $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{2\Delta P/\rho}$ | m³/s |
| 9 | Turbine Flow | $Q = f \cdot V_{cal}$ | m³/s |
| 10 | Ultrasonic Distance | $d = ct/2$ | m |
| 11 | Strain Gauge | $\Delta R/R = G \varepsilon$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Hall Voltage | $V_H = R_H IB/t$ | V |
| 13 | LVDT Output | $E_{out} = E_{S_1} - E_{S_2} = 2kx$ | V |
| 14 | Capacitive | $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A/d$ | F |
| 15 | Piezo Charge | $Q = d_{33} F$ | C |
| 16 | Speed of Sound | $c = 331.4 + 0.6 T$ | m/s |
| 17 | Seebeck | $\alpha = \Delta E/\Delta T$ | µV/°C |
| 18 | RTD α | $\alpha = (R_{100} - R_0)/(100 R_0)$ | °C⁻¹ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In the formula $d = ct/2$, the factor of **2** appears because ultrasonic waves travel *to* the target and *back*. Students commonly forget this and get answers double the actual distance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Thermocouple Sensitivity from Seebeck Coefficients

### Statement
Derive the total Seebeck EMF for a two-junction thermocouple measuring a hot junction T_H with cold junction T_C.

### Step 1 — Define the Seebeck Coefficient

The Seebeck coefficient S_A of material A is defined as the EMF produced per unit temperature difference:

$$S_A = \frac{dE_A}{dT}$$

### Step 2 — EMF for One Material from T_C to T_H

$$E_A = \int_{T_C}^{T_H} S_A(T) \, dT$$

### Step 3 — EMF for Material B

$$E_B = \int_{T_C}^{T_H} S_B(T) \, dT$$

### Step 4 — Loop EMF (Thermocouple EMF)

In a series loop, both materials contribute. The differential Seebeck coefficient is:

$$S_{AB}(T) = S_A(T) - S_B(T)$$

### Step 5 — Total Thermocouple EMF

$$E_{AB} = \int_{T_C}^{T_H} \big[S_A(T) - S_B(T)\big] \, dT$$

### Step 6 — Linear Approximation (Constant S_AB)

If S_AB is approximately constant over the operating range:

$$E_{AB} \approx S_{AB} (T_H - T_C)$$

### Step 7 — Numerical Example (Type K, S ≈ 41 µV/°C)

For T_H = 500 °C, T_C = 25 °C:

$$E_{AB} \approx 41 \times (500 - 25) = 41 \times 475 = 19{,}475 \text{ µV} = 19.475 \text{ mV}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: RTD Resistance at Elevated Temperature

### Statement
A Pt100 RTD (R₀ = 100 Ω, α = 0.003851 °C⁻¹) measures a fluid at T = 250 °C. Find its resistance using both the linear and Callendar–Van Dusen models.

### Step 1 — Linear Approximation

$$R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha T) = 100 \times (1 + 0.003851 \times 250)$$

$$R_T = 100 \times (1 + 0.96275) = 100 \times 1.96275 = 196.275 \text{ Ω}$$

### Step 2 — Callendar–Van Dusen (T ≥ 0 °C)

$$R_T = R_0 (1 + A T + B T^2)$$

$$A T = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} \times 250 = 0.977075$$

$$B T^2 = -5.775 \times 10^{-7} \times (250)^2 = -5.775 \times 10^{-7} \times 62{,}500 = -0.036094$$

Sum inside brackets:

$$1 + 0.977075 - 0.036094 = 1.940981$$

### Step 3 — Final Resistance

$$R_T = 100 \times 1.940981 = 194.098 \text{ Ω}$$

### Step 4 — Comparison & Error

Linear model: 196.275 Ω
CVD model: 194.098 Ω

$$\text{Error} = \frac{196.275 - 194.098}{194.098} \times 100 = 1.12 \text{ \%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The linear model overestimates by 1.12 % at 250 °C. Industrial high-accuracy transmitters (e.g., Rosemount 644) always use the full CVD cubic equation — this is why KTU examiners love this comparison question.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Orifice Flowmeter — Full Derivation

### Statement
A 50 mm-diameter pipe has a 25 mm-diameter orifice. Differential pressure = 10 kPa. Fluid density = 1000 kg/m³. C_d = 0.62. Calculate volumetric and mass flow rates.

### Step 1 — Cross-Sectional Areas

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_1^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.050)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.025)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 2 — Area Ratio Squared

$$\beta = \frac{A_2}{A_1} = \left(\frac{0.025}{0.050}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2 = 0.25$$

$$\beta^2 = 0.0625$$

### Step 3 — Apply the General Orifice Equation

$$Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho (1 - \beta^4)}}$$

Compute $1 - \beta^4$:

$$\beta^4 = (0.25)^2 = 0.0625$$

$$1 - \beta^4 = 1 - 0.0625 = 0.9375$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Numbers

$$Q = 0.62 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10{,}000}{1000 \times 0.9375}}$$

Inside the sqrt:

$$\frac{20{,}000}{937.5} = 21.333$$

$$\sqrt{21.333} = 4.619$$

### Step 5 — Final Q

$$Q = 0.62 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 4.619 = 1.405 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q \approx 1.405 \text{ L/s}$$

### Step 6 — Mass Flow Rate

$$\dot{m} = \rho Q = 1000 \times 1.405 \times 10^{-3} = 1.405 \text{ kg/s}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: LVDT Differential Output Signal

### Statement
An LVDT has primary excitation V_p = 10 V_rms at 5 kHz, sensitivity k = 0.5 V/mm. The core is displaced +6 mm from null. Find (i) individual secondary voltages, (ii) differential output.

### Step 1 — Individual Secondaries

For an ideal symmetric LVDT:

$$E_{S_1} = k \cdot x = 0.5 \times 6 = 3.0 \text{ V}$$

$$E_{S_2} = -k \cdot x = -0.5 \times 6 = -3.0 \text{ V}$$

> (The negative sign arises because S₂ is wound opposite to S₁, so its flux opposes S₁.)

### Step 2 — Differential Output

$$E_{out} = E_{S_1} - E_{S_2} = 3.0 - (-3.0) = 6.0 \text{ V}$$

### Step 3 — Ratiometric Output

$$E_{S_1} + E_{S_2} = 3.0 + (-3.0) = 0 \text{ V at null}$$

Sum at x = 6 mm:

$$E_{S_1} + E_{S_2} = 3.0 + (-3.0) = 0 \text{ V (theoretically)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real LVDT Note:** In practice, secondary sum is *not* exactly zero due to winding imbalance, but the ratio is independent of supply voltage and temperature — making ratiometric reading the gold standard.

### Step 4 — Phase Information
The phase of E_out indicates *direction* of displacement:
- +6 mm: E_out leads primary by some reference angle
- –6 mm: E_out lags by 180°

This is how an LVDT detects *direction* — its key advantage over a single-coil inductive sensor.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Ultrasonic Distance with Temperature Correction

### Statement
An ultrasonic sensor has a measured round-trip time t = 4.2 ms. Ambient temperature = 40 °C. Compute the distance.

### Step 1 — Speed of Sound Correction

$$c = 331.4 + 0.6 T = 331.4 + 0.6 \times 40 = 331.4 + 24 = 355.4 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 2 — Distance Equation

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2} = \frac{355.4 \times 4.2 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

### Step 3 — Numerator

$$355.4 \times 4.2 \times 10^{-3} = 1.4927 \text{ m}$$

### Step 4 — Divide by 2

$$d = 1.4927 / 2 = 0.7463 \text{ m} \approx 74.63 \text{ cm}$$

### Step 5 — Comparison Without Temperature Correction

If we had used c = 343 m/s:

$$d = \frac{343 \times 4.2 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 0.7203 \text{ m}$$

Error:

$$\frac{0.7463 - 0.7203}{0.7463} \times 100 = 3.5 \text{ \%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A 3.5 % error means a 75 cm measurement would read as 72 cm — significant in precision robotics. Always apply temperature compensation for industrial-grade ultrasonic sensing.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Hall Effect Voltage — Numerical

### Statement
A Hall-effect sensor has thickness t = 0.5 mm, I = 5 mA, B = 0.2 T, R_H = 1.2 × 10⁻⁶ m³/C. Find Hall voltage.

### Step 1 — Formula

$$V_H = R_H \cdot \frac{I \cdot B}{t}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute

$$V_H = 1.2 \times 10^{-6} \cdot \frac{5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.2}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

### Step 3 — Numerator of Fraction

$$5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.2 = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$$

### Step 4 — Divide by t

$$\frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.0$$

### Step 5 — Final

$$V_H = 1.2 \times 10^{-6} \times 2.0 = 2.4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V} = 2.4 \text{ µV}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This tiny voltage explains why Hall sensors use **integrated chopper-stabilized amplifiers** to produce usable mV-to-V range outputs.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Comparison Matrix — KTU Favourite

| Property | Thermocouple | RTD | Thermistor | IC Sensor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Active/Passive | Active | Passive | Passive | Active |
| Range | Widest | Wide | Narrow | Narrow |
| Linearity | Fair | Excellent | Poor | Excellent |
| Sensitivity | Low (~µV/°C) | Medium | Very high | High |
| Self-heating | No | Low | High | Low |
| Cost | Low | Medium-High | Low | Lowest |
| Typical app | Furnaces | Lab, process | HVAC | Embedded |

| Property | LVDT | Hall | Eddy | Capacitive | Optical | Ultrasonic |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Contact | No | No | No | No | No | No |
| Target material | Magnetic only | Magnetic | Conductive | Any | Any | Any |
| Range | mm–250 mm | mm–cm | 0.1–80 mm | 0.1–50 mm | mm–100 m | cm–10 m |
| Resolution | µm | mm | µm | sub-µm | µm | mm |
| Cost | High | Very low | Medium | Medium | Medium-high | Low |
