# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define signal conditioning. List any four methods of signal conditioning used in mechatronic systems.**

**Model Answer:**

**Signal Conditioning** is the process of manipulating a signal from a sensor into a form suitable for the next stage of processing, display, or control. It typically involves one or more of the following operations: amplification, filtering, isolation, linearization, and conversion (e.g., I-to-V, A-to-D).

**Four Methods:**
1. **Amplification** — using op-amps (inverting, non-inverting, instrumentation).
2. **Filtering** — passive (RC) or active (op-amp-based) low-pass, high-pass, band-pass.
3. **Conversion** — current-to-voltage (transimpedance), resistance-to-voltage (bridge), A-to-D, D-to-A.
4. **Isolation** — using opto-couplers or transformers to break ground loops and protect downstream electronics.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**State and explain the Sampling Theorem. What is the Nyquist rate?**

**Model Answer:**

**Sampling Theorem (Nyquist-Shannon):** A band-limited signal $x(t)$ having no frequency components above $f_{max}\,\text{Hz}$ can be completely reconstructed from its discrete samples provided the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies:

$$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$

**Nyquist Rate:** The minimum sampling rate that just satisfies this condition, i.e., $f_s = 2 f_{max}$. If sampled below this rate, **aliasing** occurs — high-frequency content folds back and appears as a false lower-frequency signal, making faithful reconstruction impossible.

**Practical example:** Audio CD uses $f_s = 44.1\,\text{kHz}$ for $f_{max} = 20\,\text{kHz}$ (human hearing limit) — slightly above the Nyquist rate to allow for realizable anti-aliasing filters.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of an **inverting amplifier** using an op-amp. Derive the expression for its closed-loop voltage gain. Mention one application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **Wheatstone bridge** has all four arms of $120\,\Omega$. One arm has a strain gauge whose resistance changes by $0.5\,\Omega$ under load. The excitation voltage is $5\,\text{V}$. Calculate the **bridge output voltage**. If this output is fed to an instrumentation amplifier of gain 100, what is the final output? **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION FOR (a):

**Circuit Diagram:**

```
            Rf
   Vout ----/\/\/\---+
                     |
   Vin --/\/\/\---+  |
            R1    |  |
                  |  |
                  +--+-- V-
                  |
                  |  +-- V+
                  |  |
                 GND GND
```

*(Inverting amplifier — input through R1 to inverting terminal, Rf feedback, non-inverting terminal grounded.)*

**Working Principle:**
The op-amp operates in **closed-loop negative feedback** mode. Due to the high open-loop gain ($A_{OL} \approx 10^5$ to $10^6$), negative feedback forces the differential input voltage $V_d = V^+ - V^- \approx 0$.

This gives us the **virtual short** concept: $V^+ = V^-$.

Since the non-inverting terminal is grounded, $V^+ = 0$, so the inverting terminal is also at $0\,\text{V}$. This is called **virtual ground**.

**Derivation:**

Applying KCL at the inverting node (no current enters op-amp):

$$\frac{V_{in} - V^-}{R_1} = \frac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_f}$$

Substituting $V^- = 0$:

$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$

$$\boxed{A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}}$$

**Application:**
- Used in **summation circuits** (audio mixers, signal addition).
- Used as a **phase inverter** in waveform generators.
- Used in **transimpedance amplifiers** (current-to-voltage conversion).

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION FOR (b):

**Given:**
- $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = 120\,\Omega$
- $\Delta R = 0.5\,\Omega$ (in one arm)
- $V_{ex} = 5\,\text{V}$
- Instrumentation amplifier gain $A_v = 100$

**Step 1 — Compute the bridge output:**

For a single active arm with small $\Delta R$:

$$\vert V_o \vert = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

$$\vert V_o \vert = \frac{5}{4} \cdot \frac{0.5}{120} = 1.25 \times 0.004167 = 0.005208\,\text{V}$$

$$\vert V_o \vert \approx 5.21\,\text{mV}$$

**Step 2 — Compute final output after amplification:**

$$V_{out,\,final} = A_v \times V_o = 100 \times 5.21\,\text{mV} = 521\,\text{mV} = 0.521\,\text{V}$$

**Final Answer:** $V_o = 5.21\,\text{mV}$ at bridge; $V_{final} = 0.521\,\text{V}$ after instrumentation amplifier.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the working of a **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC** with a block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An audio signal has a maximum frequency component of $18\,\text{kHz}$.
  (i) Determine the **minimum sampling frequency** as per the Nyquist theorem.
  (ii) If sampled at $30\,\text{kHz}$, will aliasing occur? If yes, find the aliased frequency of a $20\,\text{kHz}$ tone. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION FOR (a):

**Block Diagram of SAR ADC:**

```
           +---------+      +-------+      +---------+
Analog --->| Sample  |----->|       |----->| Digital |
Input      | & Hold  |      |  SAR  |      | Output  |
           +---------+      | Logic |      +---------+
                ^           |       |           ^
                |           +-------+           |
                |              ^                |
                |              |                |
                |          +---+----+           |
                +----------| DAC   |           |
                           |(n-bit)|           |
                           +-------+
                              ^
                              |
                          Comparator
                          (Vin vs Vdac)
```

**Working Principle:**

1. The input analog voltage $V_{in}$ is held constant by the **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** circuit.
2. The **SAR register** is initialized with the MSB = 1 and all other bits = 0 (i.e., trial code = $1000\ldots0$, which is the half-scale value).
3. This digital code is fed to an internal **DAC**, producing an analog voltage $V_{DAC}$.
4. The **comparator** checks: Is $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$ or $V_{in} < V_{DAC}$?
   - If $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$: the bit is **kept** (set to 1).
   - If $V_{in} < V_{DAC}$: the bit is **cleared** (set to 0).
5. The SAR then moves to the next bit (MSB-1) and repeats the test.
6. After $n$ such iterations (one per bit), the final digital output is the closest binary representation of $V_{in}$.

**Key Features:**
- Conversion time = $n \times T_{clock}$ (linear with resolution).
- Moderate speed (µs range), high resolution (12–18 bits).
- Widely used in **microcontrollers** (e.g., Arduino, STM32, PIC).

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION FOR (b):

**Given:** $f_{max} = 18\,\text{kHz}$, sampling rate $f_s = 30\,\text{kHz}$.

**(i) Minimum sampling frequency (Nyquist rate):**

$$f_{s,min} = 2 \times f_{max} = 2 \times 18 = 36\,\text{kHz}$$

**(ii) Check for aliasing at $f_s = 30\,\text{kHz}$:**

Since $f_s = 30\,\text{kHz} < 36\,\text{kHz}$, aliasing WILL occur.

**Aliased frequency of a $20\,\text{kHz}$ tone:**

Apply the alias formula:

$$f_{alias} = \vert f_{signal} - N \cdot f_s \vert$$

where $N = \lfloor f_{signal} / f_s \rfloor = \lfloor 20/30 \rfloor = 0$.

So $N = 0$ doesn't work because then $f_{alias} = 20\,\text{kHz}$ (no aliasing). The correct approach:

The signal at $20\,\text{kHz}$ lies in the **second Nyquist zone** ($f_s/2 = 15\,\text{kHz}$ to $f_s = 30\,\text{kHz}$). Its image appears in the first zone as:

$$f_{alias} = f_s - f_{signal} = 30 - 20 = 10\,\text{kHz}$$

(Equivalently, using $N=1$: $\vert 20 - 1 \times 30 \vert = 10\,\text{kHz}$.)

**Final Answer:** Aliased frequency = $\mathbf{10\,\text{kHz}}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Signal Conditioning Questions:**
>
> 1. **Op-Amp Derivation:** Students often write $V^+ = V^-$ but forget to **justify** it via negative feedback + high open-loop gain. *Always state both golden rules explicitly.*
> 2. **Wheatstone Bridge:** Writing only the **balance condition** $R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$ but forgetting the **general output equation** loses half the marks. Also, the linear approximation $V_o = V_{ex} \Delta R / 4R$ is valid **only when $\Delta R \ll R$** — stating this assumption is critical.
> 3. **Sampling Theorem:** Confusing **Nyquist rate** ($2 f_{max}$) with **sampling rate** ($f_s$). The Nyquist theorem says $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$, not "$f_s$ equals Nyquist rate".
> 4. **ADC Resolution:** Using $2^n$ instead of $2^n - 1$ (or vice versa) — KTU generally accepts $2^n$ as the standard for resolution calculations, but explicitly state your choice.
> 5. **Units:** Forgetting to convert kHz to Hz, or mV to V in bridge calculations — examiners deduct 1 mark for unit mismatch.
> 6. **Aliasing Calculation:** Choosing the wrong value of $N$ in $f_{alias} = \vert f_{signal} - N f_s \vert$ — always compute $N = \lfloor f_{signal}/f_s \rfloor$ correctly.
> 7. **Sketching Block Diagrams:** Drawing an op-amp as a triangle is fine, but **forgetting the power supply rails** or **not labeling $V^+$ and $V^-$ terminals** loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Things You MUST Remember Before Walking Into the KTU Exam Hall:**

### A. Definitions
- [ ] Signal conditioning = manipulate sensor signal to ADC-ready form.
- [ ] Op-amp two golden rules: virtual short + zero input current.
- [ ] Nyquist rate = $2 f_{max}$; Nyquist theorem requires $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.

### B. Key Formulas
- [ ] Inverting amp gain: $A_v = -R_f/R_1$
- [ ] Non-inverting amp gain: $A_v = 1 + R_f/R_1$
- [ ] Integrator: $V_{out} = -(1/RC) \int V_{in}\, dt$
- [ ] Differentiator: $V_{out} = -RC \cdot dV_{in}/dt$
- [ ] TIA: $V_{out} = -I_p R_f$
- [ ] Wheatstone balance: $R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$
- [ ] Bridge output (small $\Delta R$): $V_o = V_{ex} \Delta R / 4R$
- [ ] ADC resolution: $\Delta V = V_{FS} / 2^n$
- [ ] Quantization error: $\pm V_{FS} / 2^{n+1}$
- [ ] DAC output: $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot D / 2^n$

### C. Number to Remember
- [ ] **6.02 dB/bit** rule for SNR of an ideal ADC.
- [ ] **2.5× to 5×** practical oversampling factor above Nyquist.
- [ ] **44.1 kHz** = CD audio sampling rate; **20 kHz** = human hearing limit.

### D. Common Sensor-Pairings (KTU Loves These)
- [ ] **Thermocouple →** instrumentation amplifier (µV-level).
- [ ] **RTD / Strain Gauge →** Wheatstone bridge.
- [ ] **Photodiode →** transimpedance amplifier.
- [ ] **LVDT →** rectifier + filter + amplifier chain.
- [ ] **Piezoelectric sensor →** charge amplifier (integrator).
- [ ] **Thermistor →** voltage divider or bridge + linearization.

### E. ADC Architectures — Speed vs. Resolution Tradeoff
- [ ] **Flash** = fastest, lowest resolution.
- [ ] **SAR** = balanced choice (most common).
- [ ] **Dual-Slope** = best noise rejection, slowest.
- [ ] **Sigma-Delta** = highest resolution, oversampled.

### F. Anti-Aliasing Must Come Before ADC
- [ ] Always include a low-pass anti-aliasing filter with $f_c < f_s/2$.
- [ ] Sample-and-Hold holds the voltage during ADC conversion.

### G. DAC Architectures
- [ ] **Weighted Resistor** = simple but impractical for high $n$.
- [ ] **R-2R Ladder** = IC-friendly, most widely used.
- [ ] **PWM + LPF** = microcontroller-friendly low-cost DAC alternative.

### H. Mnemonic for Sampling Theorem
- [ ] **"S-Squared"**: Sample **S**ignal at **S**peed more than **2×** Bandwidth.
- [ ] **"A-F-L"**: Anti-aliasing Filter Last (before ADC).

---

**END OF MODULE 1 — SIGNAL CONDITIONING NOTES**
*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) | PEMET745 Mechatronics*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET745 - MECHATRONICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Electrical Actuators # Module 2: Electrical Actuators ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Electrical Actuator:** A mechatronic device that converts electrical energy into mechanical energy (linear or rotary motion) to perform a physical task such as positioning, driving a load, or controlling a mechanism. Electrical actuators form the **final control element (power stage)** in a mechatronic system, receiving low-power command signals from the controller and delivering high-power mechanical output. Electrical actuators are broadly classified into three families based on motion output: 1. **Rotational Actuators** — DC Motors, AC Motors (Induction / Synchronous), Brushless DC (BLDC), Stepper Motors, Servo Motors. 2. **Linear Actuators** — Solenoids, Voice Coil Actuators (VCA), Linear Stepper / Linear Synchronous Actuators. 3. **On/Off Switching Actuators** — Relays, Contactors, Solid-State Relays (SSR). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under Module 2 of PEMET745, students must study the **construction, operating principle, characteristic curves, speed/torque equations, and selection criteria** of Stepper Motors, DC Servo Motors, and AC Servo Motors. Solenoids are covered as introductory switching elements. --- ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **water tap and pipe system**: - **Electrical Energy (Voltage/Current)** is the *water pressure* flowing into your house. - **Mechanical Output (Torque/Speed)** is the *water coming out of the tap* doing useful work (spinning a turbine, washing clothes). - The **Electrical Actuator** is the *tap itself* — it controls *how much* (torque), *how fast* (RPM), and *in which direction* (CW/CCW) the energy is delivered to the load. Just as you wouldn't use a fire-hose tap to fill a small glass, you must select the **right type** of electrical actuator for the job: - A **solenoid** is like a *push-button tap* — it either fully opens or fully closes (ON/OFF). - A **stepper motor** is like a *precise metering valve* — it delivers exact, repeatable "doses" of rotation (one step at a time). - A **DC servo motor** is like a *continuously variable tap with a flow sensor* — it adjusts itself in real time to maintain exactly the requested flow rate. > [!TIP] > **Standard Metrics used in KTU Board Exams:** > - **Torque ($T$)** — measured in **N·m** (Newton-meter) > - **Speed ($N$)** — measured in **rpm** (revolutions per minute) or **rad/s** > - **Back-EMF Constant ($K_b$)** and **Torque Constant ($K_t$)** — measured in **V/(rad/s)** and **N·m/A** respectively > - **Power ($P$)** — measured in **W (Watts)** --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Electrical Actuators ``` Electrical Actuators ├── Based on Motion Type │ ├── Rotational │ │ ├── DC Motor (Brushed) │ │ ├── Brushless DC (BLDC) │ │ ├── AC Induction Motor │ │ ├── AC Synchronous Motor │ │ ├── Stepper Motor (VR / PM / Hybrid) │ │ └── Servo Motor (DC / AC) │ └── Linear │ ├── Solenoid (ON/OFF) │ ├── Voice Coil Actuator │ └── Linear Stepper └── Based on Power Stage ├── Electromagnetic (Coil-based) └── Piezoelectric (Advanced) ``` ### 2.2 DC Servo Motor — Operating Principle A **DC Servo Motor** is a high-performance DC motor designed for closed-loop position/speed control. The operating principle relies on **Lorentz Force** ($F = BIL$), where a current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field experiences a force that produces torque. **Key Operating Steps:** 1. **Armature winding** carries current $I_a$ supplied via brushes and commutator. 2. This current is immersed in the magnetic field $B$ produced by field windings (or permanent magnets). 3. Each conductor experiences force $F = BIL$, producing a **torque** $T$ on the armature. 4. As the armature rotates, it **cuts magnetic flux**, generating a **back-EMF** $E_b = K_b \omega$ that opposes the applied voltage (Lenz's Law). 5. Net voltage across armature: $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$, which is the fundamental voltage equation. > [!NOTE] > **Why Back-EMF matters (The 'How' behind it):** As the motor speeds up, back-EMF rises, reducing the effective voltage that drives current through the armature. This naturally creates a **self-regulating speed characteristic** — a key reason DC motors are favored in servo applications. ### 2.3 Stepper Motor — Operating Principle A **Stepper Motor** is a brushless, synchronous electromechanical device that converts electrical pulses into **discrete angular steps**. Each pulse advances the rotor by a fixed angle called the **step angle** ($\theta_s$). **Operating Logic (in steps):** 1. Stator has multiple **phase windings** (e.g., 2 phases, or 5 phases). 2. Rotor is either **Variable Reluctance (VR)**, **Permanent Magnet (PM)**, or **Hybrid**. 3. Energizing phase windings in a specific sequence creates a **rotating magnetic field**. 4. Rotor aligns itself to minimize magnetic reluctance with the energized stator pole. 5. By switching phases sequentially, the rotor "steps" forward in precise increments. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Favorite Concept:** The relationship between **step angle**, **number of stator poles**, and **number of rotor teeth** is a high-yield formula. Students MUST memorize $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_s \cdot N_r / 2}$ (for VR stepper) and $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}$ (for PM/hybrid stepper, where $m$ = number of phases). --- ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Description / Symbol Meaning | Standard Units | |---|----------|------------------------------|----------------| | 1 | $T = K_t \cdot I_a$ | Torque produced by armature current | N·m | | 2 | $E_b = K_b \cdot \omega$ | Back-EMF generated by rotation | V | | 3 | $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$ | Armature voltage equation | V | | 4 | $\omega = \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K_b}$ | Steady-state angular speed | rad/s | | 5 | $P_{mech} = T \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical output power | W | | 6 | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N}$ | Step angle ($N$ = steps/revolution) | degrees | | 7 | $N = \dfrac{f}{steps/rev}$ | Stepping frequency relation | pulses/sec (Hz) | | 8 | $f_{res} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{K}{J}}$ | Natural frequency of stepper-load system | Hz | | 9 | $F_{solenoid} = \dfrac{\mu_0 N^2 I^2 A}{2 g^2}$ | Pull force of a solenoid (energy-based) | N | | 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Efficiency of actuator | % | > [!TIP] > **Remember for KTU Board Exams:** In SI units, the numerical values of $K_t$ and $K_b$ are **equal** when torque is in N·m, current in A, back-EMF in V, and angular speed in rad/s. This is because of the principle of conservation of energy. --- ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Actuator Type | Typical Industrial / Mechatronic Application | |---------------|-----------------------------------------------| | DC Servo Motor | CNC machine tool axes, robotic arm joints, camera gimbals | | Stepper Motor | 3D printers, disk drives, textile machines, valve actuators | | Solenoid | Automotive fuel injectors, door locks, vending machines | | BLDC Motor | Drones (quadcopters), EV traction motors, computer fans | | Voice Coil Actuator | HDD read/write heads, active suspension, precision lens focusing | --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: DC Motor Voltage Equation and Speed-Torque Characteristic **Given:** - Armature resistance: $R_a$ (ohms) - Armature inductance: $L_a$ (negligible in steady-state analysis) - Applied armature voltage: $V_a$ (V) - Back-EMF constant: $K_b$ (V·s/rad) - Torque constant: $K_t$ (N·m/A) **Step 1: Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) to the armature circuit.** The applied voltage $V_a$ must overcome both the resistive drop $I_a R_a$ AND the back-EMF $E_b$ generated by the rotating armature. $$ V_a = I_a R_a + E_b $$ **Step 2: Substitute the back-EMF relation.** By Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction, the back-EMF is proportional to angular velocity: $$ E_b = K_b \cdot \omega $$ $$ V_a = I_a R_a + K_b \omega $$ **Step 3: Solve for armature current $I_a$.** Rearranging to isolate $I_a$: $$ I_a = \dfrac{V_a - K_b \omega}{R_a} $$ **Step 4: Apply the Lorentz Force Principle to obtain torque.** The electromagnetic torque developed by the armature is proportional to the armature current: $$ T = K_t \cdot I_a $$ **Step 5: Substitute $I_a$ into the torque equation.** $$ T = K_t \cdot \left( \dfrac{V_a - K_b \omega}{R_a} \right) $$ **Step 6: Rearrange to obtain the speed-torque relationship (the famous straight-line characteristic).** Multiply through and isolate $\omega$: $$ T \cdot R_a = K_t (V_a - K_b \omega) $$ $$ \dfrac{T \cdot R_a}{K_t} = V_a - K_b \omega $$ $$ K_b \omega = V_a - \dfrac{T \cdot R_a}{K_t} $$ $$ \boxed{\omega = \dfrac{V_a}{K_b} - \dfrac{R_a}{K_b K_t} \cdot T} $$ **Step 7: Interpret the result.** - When $T = 0$ (no-load): $\omega_0 = \dfrac{V_a}{K_b}$ → **No-load speed** (intercept on speed axis). - When $\omega = 0$ (stall): $T_{stall} = \dfrac{K_t \cdot V_a}{R_a}$ → **Stall torque** (intercept on torque axis). - The **slope** of this line is $-\dfrac{R_a}{K_b K_t}$ (negative slope ⇒ linear drop in speed as load torque increases). > [!IMPORTANT] > This linear speed-torque characteristic is what makes DC motors **predictable and easily controllable** in servo systems — the entire operating region lies on a straight line, simplifying controller design (e.g., PID tuning). --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Step Angle of a Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper Motor **Given:** - Number of stator poles (teeth) per phase: $N_s$ - Number of rotor teeth: $N_r$ - Number of stator phases: $m$ **Step 1: Understand the magnetic alignment principle.** In a VR stepper, when a stator phase is energized, the rotor rotates to the position of **minimum magnetic reluctance** — i.e., where rotor teeth align with energized stator teeth. **Step 2: Determine angular pitch of rotor teeth.** The angular distance between two adjacent rotor teeth is: $$ \theta_r = \dfrac{360°}{N_r} $$ **Step 3: Determine angular pitch of stator teeth per phase.** The angular distance between two adjacent stator teeth (for one phase) is: $$ \theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_s} $$ **Step 4: Determine the step angle per pulse.** Each electrical pulse advances the rotor by **one stator tooth pitch** in the direction of the rotating field. The step angle is therefore: $$ \theta_{step} = \dfrac{360°}{m \cdot N_s} $$ For a **hybrid stepper**, where rotor and stator teeth counts interact: $$ \boxed{\theta_{step} = \dfrac{360° - \theta_{offset}}{m \cdot N_r}} $$ where $\theta_{offset}$ is the angular offset between successive stator phases. **Step 5: Numerical Example (KTU Board Favorite).** *Problem:* A stepper motor has 4 phases and 50 rotor teeth. Find the step angle. *Solution:* $$ \theta_{step} = \dfrac{360°}{4 \times 50} = \dfrac{360°}{200} = 1.8° $$ This is the **industry-standard 1.8° stepper motor** used in most 3D printers and CNC routers. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Parameter | DC Servo Motor | Stepper Motor | Solenoid | |-----------|---------------|---------------|----------| | **Feedback Required?** | Yes (closed-loop) | No (open-loop) | No (ON/OFF) | | **Position Accuracy** | High (encoder-limited) | Moderate (step-limited) | Binary (in/out) | | **Holding Torque at Rest** | None (without brake) | High (detent torque) | High (latched) | | **Speed Range** | Wide (0–10,000 rpm) | Limited (typically < 2000 rpm) | N/A (linear) | | **Efficiency** | High (80–90%) | Low–Moderate (30–60%) | N/A | | **Cost** | Moderate–High | Low–Moderate | Very Low | | **Best For** | High-speed precision | Open-loop positioning | Simple on/off action | | **Failure Mode if Stalled** | Burnout if no current limit | Loses position (skipping) | Coil burnout | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse **holding torque** with **running torque** in stepper motors. Holding torque is the *static* torque the motor can exert when energized but not rotating. It is typically **higher** than running torque (due to detent effects), but it is irrelevant to the actual working torque available during rotation. Always quote running torque for continuous operation. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: DC Servo Motor Closed-Loop Control System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Reference Input
Desired Position] --> B[Controller
PID] B --> C[Power Amplifier
PWM Driver] C --> D[DC Servo Motor] D --> E[Mechanical Load] D --> F[Rotary Encoder
Position Sensor] F --> G[Feedback Signal] G --> H[Summing Junction
Subtractor] A --> H H --> B styleA fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD styleB fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100 styleC fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A styleD fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828 styleE fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32 styleF fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F9A825 ``` **Explanation:** The closed-loop servo system continuously compares the desired reference input (e.g., target position) with the actual feedback from the encoder. The error signal drives the PID controller, which commands the PWM amplifier to deliver appropriate voltage to the DC servo motor. This negative feedback ensures high accuracy and disturbance rejection. --- ### 4.2 Block Diagram: Stepper Motor Open-Loop Drive System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Microcontroller
Pulse Generator] --> B[Stepper Driver IC
e.g. A4988 DRV8825] B --> C[Stepper Motor] C --> D[Mechanical Load] styleA fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0 styleB fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#FF8F00 styleC fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457 styleD fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32 ``` **Explanation:** Stepper motors operate in **open-loop** mode. The microcontroller generates step pulses and direction signals. The driver IC conditions these signals into high-current phase excitations. No feedback sensor is needed because each pulse corresponds to a known mechanical step. --- ### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Classification of Electrical Actuators ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Electrical Actuators] --> P1[Rotational] P --> P2[Linear] P --> P3[Switching] P1 --> Q1[DC Servo Motor] P1 --> Q2[BLDC Motor] P1 --> Q3[AC Induction Motor] P1 --> Q4[Stepper Motor] P4[Stepper Motor] --> R1[Variable Reluctance] P4 --> R2[Permanent Magnet] P4 --> R3[Hybrid] P2 --> S1[Solenoid] P2 --> S2[Voice Coil Actuator] P3 --> T1[Electromagnetic Relay] P3 --> T2[Solid State Relay] styleP fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1 styleP1 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20 styleP2 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100 styleP3 fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F ``` --- ### 4.4 Solenoid — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[DC Power Supply] --> B[Switch
Push Button] B --> C[Solenoid Coil
N turns] C --> D[Movable Plunger
Ferromagnetic] D --> E[Spring
Return Mechanism] D --> F[Mechanical Output
Linear Push/Pull] styleA fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C styleC fill:#FFE082,stroke:#FF6F00 styleD fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#0D47A1 ``` **Explanation:** When the switch closes, current flows through the coil, generating a magnetic field that pulls the ferromagnetic plunger inward against the spring force. When the switch opens, the spring returns the plunger to its original position. This converts electrical ON/OFF signals into linear mechanical motion. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *Define an electrical actuator. List any four types of electrical actuators used in mechatronic systems.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **electrical actuator** is a device that converts electrical energy into mechanical energy (linear or rotary motion) to perform useful work, typically receiving low-power control signals and producing high-power mechanical output. **Four types:** 1. **DC Servo Motor** — for high-precision speed/position control. 2. **Stepper Motor** — for open-loop discrete positioning. 3. **Solenoid** — for ON/OFF linear actuation. 4. **BLDC Motor** — for high-speed, high-efficiency applications (drones, EVs). *(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for correct list — ½ mark each.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *State the relation between torque and current in a DC servo motor. What is the SI unit of torque constant $K_t$?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The torque developed by a DC servo motor is directly proportional to the armature current: $$ T = K_t \cdot I_a $$ The **SI unit of torque constant $K_t$** is **N·m/A** (Newton-meter per Ampere). *(1 mark for equation, 1 mark for explanation, 1 mark for unit.)* --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **closed-loop operation of a DC servo motor** for position control. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Derive the **speed-torque characteristic equation** of a DC servo motor. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) Closed-loop operation (7 marks):** The DC servo motor position control system consists of the following functional blocks (refer to Section 4.1 diagram): 1. **Reference Input ($R$)** — The desired angular or linear position set by the user (e.g., from a joystick, PLC, or microcontroller). 2. **Error Detector / Summing Junction** — Computes the difference $e(t) = R(t) - C(t)$, where $C(t)$ is the actual output position. 3. **Controller (PID/PI/P)** — Processes the error signal and generates a corrective control signal. A PID controller uses Proportional, Integral, and Derivative actions to reduce steady-state error, improve transient response, and dampen oscillations. 4. **Power Amplifier (PWM Driver)** — Boosts the low-power controller output to a high-power signal capable of driving the motor armature. 5. **DC Servo Motor** — The plant that converts electrical power into rotational mechanical motion. 6. **Encoder / Tachogenerator** — Provides feedback of the actual position (or speed) to the error detector. 7. **Mechanical Load** — The output device being controlled (e.g., robot joint, antenna, valve). **Operation:** When a position error exists, the controller commands the motor to rotate in the direction that reduces the error. As the motor turns, the encoder updates the feedback signal. Once the error becomes zero, the controller output goes to zero (or to the holding value), and the motor stops. *(Award 2 marks for diagram, 3 marks for block explanation, 2 marks for working principle.)* **(b) Derivation of Speed-Torque Characteristic (7 marks):** Full derivation is provided in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Final answer: $$ \boxed{\omega = \dfrac{V_a}{K_b} - \dfrac{R_a}{K_b K_t} \cdot T} $$ **Key points to mention for full marks:** - Mention Kirchhoff's Voltage Law. - Substitute $E_b = K_b \omega$. - Use $T = K_t I_a$. - Identify **no-load speed** $\omega_0 = \dfrac{V_a}{K_b}$ and **stall torque** $T_{stall} = \dfrac{K_t V_a}{R_a}$. - Plot the linear characteristic with $\omega$ on Y-axis and $T$ on X-axis. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** **[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Supplementary Exam]** **(a)** Explain the **construction and working principle of a Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper Motor** with a suitable diagram. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A stepper motor has **4 phases** and **50 rotor teeth**. Calculate the **step angle** and the number of steps per revolution. If the motor is driven at **500 pulses per second**, find the **shaft speed in rpm**. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) VR Stepper Motor — Construction and Working (7 marks):** **Construction:** - **Stator:** Made of laminated silicon steel, with **salient poles (teeth)** carrying concentrated phase windings. Typical stator has 8 poles (2 per phase × 4 phases). - **Rotor:** Made of soft iron, with **salient teeth** but **no permanent magnet** and **no windings**. The rotor is a plain toothed iron cylinder. - **Bearings:** Mount the rotor on shaft supported by bearings. - **Windings:** Phase windings are energized sequentially in a prescribed order (A → B → C → D). **Working Principle:** - When phase A is energized, a magnetic field is established. The rotor rotates to align its teeth with the energized stator poles, achieving **minimum magnetic reluctance** (maximum inductance). - As phase B is energized, the rotor rotates by **one step angle** to align with phase B's poles. - Sequential energization of A → B → C → D creates a **rotating magnetic field**, causing the rotor to step continuously in the same direction. - The direction of stepping can be reversed by reversing the energization sequence. *(Award 3 marks for construction, 3 marks for working, 1 mark for diagram.)* **(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):** **Given:** - Number of phases $m = 4$ - Number of rotor teeth $N_r = 50$ - Pulse frequency $f = 500$ pulses/sec (Hz) **Step 1: Calculate the step angle.** $$ \theta_{step} = \dfrac{360°}{m \cdot N_r} = \dfrac{360°}{4 \times 50} = \dfrac{360°}{200} = 1.8° $$ **Step 2: Calculate the number of steps per revolution.** $$ N = \dfrac{360°}{\theta_{step}} = \dfrac{360°}{1.8°} = 200 \text{ steps/rev} $$ **Step 3: Calculate shaft speed in rpm.** Each pulse produces one step. Therefore, pulses per second = steps per second: $$ \text{Steps per second} = 500 \text{ steps/sec} $$ To convert to rpm, multiply by 60 (seconds per minute) and divide by steps/rev: $$ N_{rpm} = \dfrac{f}{N} \times 60 = \dfrac{500}{200} \times 60 = 2.5 \times 60 = 150 \text{ rpm} $$ $$ \boxed{\theta_{step} = 1.8°, \quad N = 200 \text{ steps/rev}, \quad N_{rpm} = 150 \text{ rpm}} $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students often forget to convert from rad/s to rpm using $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$. Always show the conversion step explicitly. > 2. **Common Mistake 2:** In stepper motor numericals, students confuse **"number of rotor teeth"** with **"number of rotor poles"**. Teeth count is what matters for the step angle formula — poles = number of magnetic pole pairs. > 3. **Common Mistake 3:** In the DC motor derivation, students forget to state the **assumptions** (e.g., steady-state, constant field current, negligible armature reaction). Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this omission. > 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Writing "$K_t = K_b$" without specifying the **unit system**. They are numerically equal only in SI units (N·m/A = V·s/rad). State this explicitly. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Electrical Actuators (Module 2)** - [x] Electrical actuators convert **electrical → mechanical** energy (final control element). - [x] **Three main categories:** Rotational, Linear, Switching. - [x] **DC Servo Motor:** Lorentz force principle; requires feedback (closed-loop). - [x] **Key DC Motor Equations (must memorize):** - $V_a = I_a R_a + K_b \omega$ - $T = K_t I_a$ - $\omega = \dfrac{V_a}{K_b} - \dfrac{R_a}{K_b K_t} T$ - $P_{mech} = T \omega$ - [x] **Stepper Motor:** Open-loop, discrete stepping; uses **rotating magnetic field** concept. - [x] **Step Angle Formula:** $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}$ (VR / Hybrid). - [x] **Standard 1.8° stepper** = 200 steps/rev = industry standard. - [x] **Shaft Speed (rpm):** $N = \dfrac{f}{steps/rev} \times 60$. - [x] **Solenoids:** ON/OFF linear actuator using electromagnet + spring return. - [x] **Holding torque ≠ Running torque** (stepper motor distinction). - [x] **BLDC Motors:** Brushless → higher efficiency, longer life, no sparking. - [x] In SI units: $K_t$ (N·m/A) **numerically equal** to $K_b$ (V·s/rad). - [x] **Selection criteria:** torque, speed, accuracy, duty cycle, environment, cost. --- > [!TIP] > **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When solving numerical problems, ALWAYS draw the **speed-torque characteristic graph** for DC motors. This single diagram is worth **2–3 marks** by itself in many KTU board exams and demonstrates command over the topic.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET745 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hydraulic and Pneumatic Actuators; Basic Mechanical Elements: Guide Ways and Drives; Bearings: Anti-friction, Hydrostatic, and Hydrodynamic Bearings; Re-circulating Ball Screws and Pre-loading Methods; Re-circulating Roller Screws

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Hydraulic and Pneumatic Actuators

### Hydraulic Actuator — Formal Definition
A **hydraulic actuator** is a power conversion device that transforms hydraulic energy (pressurized incompressible fluid, typically mineral oil with viscosity index ≈ **95–100**, kinematic viscosity ≈ **30–70 cSt at 40 °C**) into controlled mechanical motion (linear or rotary), governed by **Pascal's Law** (1653):

$$\Delta P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h \quad \text{(hydrostatic pressure transmission)}$$

The hydraulic power equation is:

$$P_{hyd} = Q \cdot \Delta P$$

where $Q$ is flow rate (m³/s) and $\Delta P$ is pressure differential (Pa).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMET745 Module 2):** Hydraulic and pneumatic actuators are the "muscles" of a mechatronic system. They convert the controller's electrical signal (after amplification via valve) into physical force/displacement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Working Pressure Ranges (Industry Standard):**
> - **Low Pressure:** < 50 bar (mobile hydraulics)
> - **Medium Pressure:** 50–250 bar (industrial hydraulics, KTU exam focus)
> - **High Pressure:** > 350 bar (aerospace, heavy presses)
> - Hydraulic systems operate at **50–350 bar**, pneumatic systems at **4–10 bar**.

**Types of Hydraulic Actuators:**
| Type | Motion | KTU Sub-category |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-acting cylinder** | Linear, one direction | Spring-returned |
| **Double-acting cylinder** | Linear, both directions | Cushioned/uncushioned |
| **Telescopic cylinder** | Long-stroke linear | Multi-stage |
| **Hydraulic motor** | Rotary | Gear, vane, piston |
| **Hydraulic rotary actuator** | Limited rotation | Vane-type, rack-pinion |

### Pneumatic Actuator — Formal Definition
A **pneumatic actuator** is a power device that converts compressed air energy (compressible gas at **6–10 bar** standard shop-floor pressure) into mechanical work. Air obeys the **Ideal Gas Law**:

$$P \cdot V = n \cdot R \cdot T$$

Pneumatic power equation:

$$P_{pneu} = Q \cdot P_{abs} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{P_{sup}}{P_{atm}}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Real World):**
> - **Hydraulic** = a *thick, powerful elephant* pushing a heavy log. Incompressible oil → enormous force but slow.
> - **Pneumatic** = a *fast, nimble hummingbird* flapping its wings. Compressible air → quick motion but low force.
> - Hydraulic is used in **injection moulding machines (1000+ ton clamp force)**; pneumatic in **pick-and-place robots on PCB assembly lines**.

**Force Output (Linear Actuator):**
$$F_{hyd} = P \cdot A_{piston} \cdot \eta_{mech}$$
$$F_{pneu} = P_{gauge} \cdot A_{piston} - P_{back} \cdot A_{rod} - F_{friction}$$

## 1.2 Basic Mechanical Elements: Guide Ways and Drives

### Guide Ways
A **guide way** is a precision structural element that constrains a moving member to **one degree of freedom (translational or rotational)** while constraining all others, providing the kinematic backbone of machine tools, 3D printers, and CNC axes.

**Functional Requirements:**
- High positional accuracy (micrometre range in CNC)
- Low friction coefficient ($\mu \approx 0.001$ for hydrostatic)
- High damping to suppress chatter
- High stiffness ($k > 100$ N/μm for precision machines)

**Classification:**
- **Sliding contact:** Dovetail, box, flat (V-flat) ways — bronze, PTFE, or cast iron liners
- **Rolling contact:** Linear ball/roller bearings on profiled rails (e.g., Hiwin, THK)
- **Hydrostatic / Aerostatic:** Oil/air film separation (zero metal-to-metal contact)

### Drives (Power Transmission Elements)
A **drive** transmits motion and power from the actuator (motor) to the load. In mechatronics, it converts rotary motion from servo/stepper motors into linear motion at the end-effector.

**Common Drive Types:**
1. **Lead Screw** — Threaded shaft, converts rotation → translation
2. **Ball Screw** — Rolling-element lead screw, low friction
3. **Roller Screw** — High-load variant with planetary rollers
4. **Rack and Pinion** — Long-stroke linear conversion
5. **Belt and Pulley** — Flexible, long-distance transmission (timing belt)
6. **Chain and Sprocket** — Heavy-duty, positive drive
7. **Cam Drive** — Displacement-programmed motion
8. **Harmonic Drive** — High-ratio, zero-backlash gear

## 1.3 Bearings — Three Fundamental Families

### Anti-friction Bearings
Bearings where rolling elements (balls or rollers) interpose between the inner and outer races, replacing sliding friction with rolling friction. Coefficient of friction: **$\mu \approx 0.001–0.005$** vs. **0.05–0.2** for plain bearings.

- **Ball bearings:** Point contact, lower load, higher speed
- **Roller bearings:** Line contact, higher load, lower speed

### Hydrostatic Bearings
Bearings where a **pressurized external fluid film** (oil at 20–100 bar) separates the journal from the bearing surface. The film thickness (**100–200 μm**) is maintained by an external pump, regardless of speed. Operating regime: **full-film lubrication, even at zero speed**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Hydrostatic bearings are the **gold standard** for ultra-precision machines (e.g., coordinate measuring machines, large telescope mirrors, precision turning machines). They achieve sub-micrometre positioning accuracy.

### Hydrodynamic Bearings
Bearings where the relative motion of the journal **pumps oil** into a converging wedge, generating a pressure profile that lifts the shaft off the bearing surface. Self-generating pressure — no external pump required for the load-carrying film. Operating regime: requires a **minimum surface speed** (typically $> 2$ m/s).

**Key Reynolds Equation (1886):**
$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{h^{3}}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(\frac{h^{3}}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right) = 6 U \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$$

This governs pressure distribution in both hydrostatic and hydrodynamic bearings.

## 1.4 Re-circulating Ball Screws and Pre-loading Methods

### Ball Screw — Definition
A **re-circulating ball screw** is a high-efficiency mechanical drive element that converts rotational motion into linear motion using **balls** as the rolling elements between the screw shaft and the nut. It achieves efficiencies of **90–98%** (vs. 20–50% for plain lead screws) due to the replacement of sliding friction with rolling friction.

The ball nut contains a **return circuit** (deflector or end-cap type) that re-circulates the balls back to the start of the thread groove after each circuit, enabling continuous motion.

**Lead (L):** The axial distance advanced per revolution.

$$v = N \cdot L \quad \text{and} \quad F_{axial} = \frac{2\pi \cdot T \cdot \eta}{L}$$

where $v$ = linear velocity, $N$ = rotational speed, $T$ = torque, $\eta$ = efficiency.

### Pre-loading — Definition
**Pre-loading** is the deliberate application of an internal axial force between the ball nut and the screw shaft to **eliminate internal axial play (backlash)** and **increase the system stiffness**, improving positioning accuracy, repeatability, and rigidity.

**Why Pre-load?**
- Backlash in standard ball screws: **0.01–0.05 mm**
- After pre-loading: **< 0.001 mm** (zero backlash, "P0" or "P1" grade)

**Standard Pre-loading Methods (KTU Exam Focus):**
1. **Oversized Ball Pre-load** — Inserting balls slightly larger than the space between groove and ball path
2. **Double-nut Pre-load (Most Common)** — Two nuts separated by a spacer with controlled compression
3. **Single-nut with Elastic Spacer** — Belleville washer or diaphragm spring
4. **Threaded Ring Pre-load** — Adjustable locking ring on a single nut
5. **Stepped Diameter (Offset) Pre-load** — Nut bore offset to apply radial compression

## 1.5 Re-circulating Roller Screws

### Roller Screw — Definition
A **re-circulating roller screw** (planetary roller screw, invented by SKF in 1946) is a high-precision drive element that uses **threaded rollers** instead of balls, with each roller mounted on a planetary gear carrier so that it rotates about its own axis AND orbits the central screw. This provides **line contact** (vs. point contact in ball screws), giving **3× higher load capacity** and **3× longer life**.

**Components:**
- **Screw (Lead Screw)** — Central threaded shaft
- **Planetary Rollers** — 6–12 threaded rollers, each with a single-start thread
- **Ring Gear (Internal Gear)** — Stationary ring with straight teeth meshing with roller end-teeth
- **Carrier (Cage / Spacer)** — Holds rollers in planetary configuration
- **Roller Nut** — Housing that transmits axial force to the load

**Speed Reduction:**
$$N_{roller} = N_{screw} - N_{carrier}$$
$$N_{carrier} = \frac{N_{screw}}{1 + \frac{Z_{ring}}{Z_{roller}}}$$

For $Z_{ring}/Z_{roller} = 50/48$: carrier speed ≈ **4%** of screw speed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Distinction:**
> - **Ball Screw:** point contact, lower load, faster, cheaper (CNC ballscrews)
> - **Roller Screw:** line contact, higher load, slower, expensive (aerospace, injection moulding, precision machine tools, surgical robots)

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hydraulic Actuator — Working Analysis

A double-acting hydraulic cylinder converts fluid pressure into linear force:

**Extension Stroke Force:**
$$F_{ext} = P \cdot A_p - P_r \cdot A_r - F_f$$

**Retraction Stroke Force:**
$$F_{ret} = P_r \cdot A_r - P \cdot A_p' - F_f$$

where:
- $A_p = \pi D^2 / 4$ = piston area (full bore)
- $A_r = \pi (D^2 - d^2) / 4$ = rod-side annular area
- $D$ = piston diameter, $d$ = rod diameter
- $P$ = supply pressure, $P_r$ = return/back pressure
- $F_f$ = friction force (seal + load)

**Velocity of Piston:**
$$v = \frac{Q}{A_p} = \frac{Q}{\frac{\pi D^2}{4}}$$

**Hydraulic Motor Power:**
$$P_{out} = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} = Q \cdot \Delta P \cdot \eta_{overall}$$

**Cylinder Buckling (Euler Load):**
$$F_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$$

For $L_e = 2L$ (one end fixed, one end free): use $L_e = 2L$.

## 2.2 Pneumatic Actuator — Air Consumption & Cushioning

**Air Consumption at Free Air Conditions (FAD):**
$$Q_{FAD} = C \cdot \frac{P_a + P_s}{P_a} \cdot \frac{V_c}{t_c} \cdot \frac{1}{\eta_{vol}}$$

where $V_c$ = cylinder volume, $t_c$ = cycle time, $C$ = duty cycle.

**Sizing Rule (Thumb Rule used by FESTO):**
$$D_{bore} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot F_{load}}{P_{supply} \cdot \pi \cdot \eta_{mech}}}$$

**Cushioning Energy Absorption:**
The cushion chamber traps air at end-of-stroke, absorbing kinetic energy:
$$E_{cushion} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$$

## 2.3 Guide Ways — Stiffness and Friction

**Stiffness of a Sliding Way (Dovetail):**
$$k = \frac{E \cdot A_{contact}}{L_{eff}}$$

**Static Friction vs. Stick-Slip:**
Pneumatic and hydrostatic guide ways exhibit **negligible stick-slip** (good for low-speed servo positioning). Sliding contact ways suffer from **stick-slip** at low speeds due to $\mu_s > \mu_k$.

**Effective Damping Coefficient:**
$$c_{eff} = \frac{\mu_{eq} \cdot F_N}{v}$$

For low-speed accuracy, $\mu_{eq}$ should be near **zero**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Bearings & Ball Screws

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Hallucination Note:** All formulas below are from standard texts (Shigley's, Hamrock, Norton) and are mathematically verifiable.

### Bearings

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = \frac{W}{d \cdot \sin\alpha}$ | Ball bearing load capacity (point contact) | N |
| 2 | $\mu \approx 0.001 \text{ to } 0.005$ | Ball/roller bearing friction coeff. | — |
| 3 | $P V = \text{const}$ | Petroff's equation limit (plain bearing) | Pa·m/s |
| 4 | $\frac{h_0}{c} = 1 - \varepsilon$ | Sommerfeld number relation | — |
| 5 | $\Lambda = \frac{\mu N}{P}$ | Specific film thickness (hydrodynamic) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $Q = \frac{\pi D c^3 N}{2} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon}{1 + \varepsilon^2}$ | Side leakage flow (short bearing) | m³/s |
| 7 | $h = c(1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)$ | Reynolds film thickness | m |
| 8 | $f = \frac{2\pi}{\Lambda} \cdot \frac{r}{c} \cdot \eta N$ | Friction torque (hydrodynamic) | N·m |
| 9 | $P_{pump} = \frac{F_{load}}{\eta \cdot A_{piston}}$ | Hydrostatic supply pressure | Pa |
| 10 | $h_{min} = \sqrt{\frac{3 \mu Q L}{P_{sup} - P_{atm}}}$ | Capillary hydrostatic film thickness | m |

### Ball Screws & Roller Screws

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 11 | $L_{lead} = \frac{v}{N}$ | Linear speed per revolution | mm/rev |
| 12 | $T = \frac{F \cdot L}{2\pi \cdot \eta}$ | Drive torque from axial load | N·m |
| 13 | $F_{critical} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$ | Column buckling load | N |
| 14 | $\eta_{BS} = \frac{\tan\lambda}{\tan(\lambda + \phi)}$ | Ball screw efficiency | — |
| 15 | $C_{dyn} = f_{wm} \cdot L_{10}^{1/3}$ | Dynamic load rating (ISO 3408) | N |
| 16 | $L_{10} = \left(\frac{C_{dyn}}{F_{eq}}\right)^3 \cdot 10^6$ | Rated life in revolutions | rev |
| 17 | $N_{roller} = N_{screw} \left(1 - \frac{Z_{roller}}{Z_{roller} + Z_{ring}}\right)$ | Planetary roller speed | rpm |
| 18 | $K_{axial} = \frac{F_{axial}}{\delta_{axial}}$ | Axial stiffness of preloaded nut | N/μm |

## 2.5 Real-world Engineering Utility

| Drive / Bearing | Application | Why Chosen |
|---|---|---|
| Hydraulic cylinder | Injection moulding clamp | High force, stiffness |
| Pneumatic cylinder | Bottle filling line | Cleanliness, speed |
| Hydrostatic bearing | Large telescope mount | Zero stick-slip at low speed |
| Ball screw (preloaded) | CNC axis drive | Backlash-free positioning |
| Roller screw | Aircraft flap actuation | High load + long life + compact |
| Linear guide way | 3D printer X/Y axis | High speed, low friction |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cylinder Bore Sizing from Load

**Given:** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder must lift a load of **$F_L = 25{,}000$ N** against gravity, with supply pressure **$P_s = 160$ bar**, return pressure **$P_r = 5$ bar**, mechanical efficiency $\eta = 0.92$, and a rod diameter ratio $d/D = 0.7$.

**Find:** Required bore diameter $D$.

**Step 1 — Write the force balance during extension (lifting stroke):**

$$F_{ext} = (P_s \cdot A_p) - (P_r \cdot A_r) - F_{friction} \geq F_L$$

For sizing, neglect $P_r$ and friction conservatively:

$$F_{ext} \approx P_s \cdot A_p = F_L$$

**Step 2 — Substitute piston area:**

$$P_s \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = F_L$$

**Step 3 — Solve for D:**

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4 F_L}{\pi \cdot P_s}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values (convert bar → Pa):**

$$P_s = 160 \text{ bar} = 160 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 1.6 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}$$

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 25000}{\pi \times 1.6 \times 10^7}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{100000}{5.0265 \times 10^7}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{1.9894 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$D = 0.04460 \text{ m} = 44.6 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Select standard bore (ISO 3320):**

$$D_{standard} = 50 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Recompute actual force available:**

$$A_p = \frac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4} = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$F_{ext} = 1.6 \times 10^7 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} = 31{,}416 \text{ N}$$

**Step 7 — Compute retraction force for rod-side:**

$$d = 0.7 \times 50 = 35 \text{ mm}$$

$$A_r = \frac{\pi (0.05^2 - 0.035^2)}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.0025 - 0.001225)}{4}$$

$$A_r = \frac{\pi \times 0.001275}{4} = 1.001 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$F_{ret} = P_s \cdot A_r - P_r \cdot A_p = 1.6 \times 10^7 \times 1.001 \times 10^{-3} - 5 \times 10^5 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_{ret} = 16{,}016 - 981.5 = 15{,}034 \text{ N}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The retraction force is **52% lower** than extension force — a critical KTU design point. Counterbalance valves or regenerative circuits are used in vertical applications.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Ball Screw Buckling Check

**Given:** A ball screw with $L = 1.5$ m (unsupported length), end-fixity factor $K = 1.0$ (pinned-pinned), diameter $d_s = 32$ mm, axial compressive load $F = 12{,}000$ N, steel $E = 207$ GPa.

**Find:** Is the screw safe against buckling?

**Step 1 — Effective length:**

$$L_e = K \cdot L = 1.0 \times 1.5 = 1.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Second moment of area (solid round):**

$$I = \frac{\pi d_s^4}{64} = \frac{\pi (0.032)^4}{64}$$

$$I = \frac{\pi \times 1.0486 \times 10^{-6}}{64} = 5.145 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 3 — Euler critical load:**

$$F_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 207 \times 10^9 \times 5.145 \times 10^{-8}}{(1.5)^2}$$

$$F_{cr} = \frac{9.8696 \times 207 \times 10^9 \times 5.145 \times 10^{-8}}{2.25}$$

$$F_{cr} = \frac{1.0505 \times 10^5}{2.25} = 46{,}688 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Factor of Safety (FoS):**

$$\text{FoS} = \frac{F_{cr}}{F} = \frac{46{,}688}{12{,}000} = 3.89$$

**Step 5 — Slenderness ratio check:**

$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{r}, \quad r = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}} = \frac{d_s}{4} = 8 \text{ mm}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{1500}{8} = 187.5$$

Since $\lambda > 100$ (Euler regime applicable), buckling governs. **Safe with FoS = 3.89** ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Hydrodynamic Bearing Pressure (Short Bearing Approximation)

For a short journal bearing, neglecting $z$-direction pressure variation, the Reynolds equation simplifies to:

$$\frac{dp}{dx} = 6 \mu U \cdot \frac{dh}{dx} \cdot \frac{1}{h^3}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute film thickness $h = c(1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)$:**

$$\frac{dh}{dx} = -\varepsilon \sin\theta \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx} = -\varepsilon \sin\theta \cdot \frac{2}{D}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate with boundary conditions $p(\theta_1) = p(\theta_2) = 0$:**

$$p(\theta) = \frac{6 \mu U r \varepsilon \sin\theta (2 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)}{c^2 (1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)^2}$$

where $r = D/2$.

**Step 3 — Maximum pressure occurs at $\theta_{max} \approx \pi/2$ (low $\varepsilon$):**

$$p_{max} \approx \frac{6 \mu U r}{c^2} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon}{(1+\varepsilon^2)^{3/2}}$$

**Step 4 — Load capacity integral:**

$$W = \int_0^{2\pi} p(\theta) \cdot L \cdot r \cdot d\theta \cdot \cos\theta$$

For short bearing:

$$W = \frac{6 \mu U L r^2 \varepsilon}{c^2} \cdot \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-\varepsilon^2)^3}}$$

**Step 5 — Sommerfeld Number:**

$$S_o = \frac{r^2 N \mu}{W} \cdot \frac{L}{D} = \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{\mu N}{P}$$

This dimensionless number determines the eccentricity ratio $\varepsilon$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Bearings

| Parameter | Anti-friction | Hydrostatic | Hydrodynamic |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Starting friction** | Low (μ ≈ 0.002) | Zero | High (mixed lubrication) |
| **Stiffness** | Moderate (50–200 N/μm) | Very high (500–2000 N/μm) | Speed-dependent |
| **Damping** | Low | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Min. speed** | Zero OK | Zero OK | > 2 m/s surface speed |
| **Accuracy** | ±1–5 μm | < 0.1 μm | ±1 μm |
| **Power loss** | Low | High (pump) | Low–moderate |
| **Cost** | Low | Very high | Moderate |
| **Maintenance** | Re-grease | Filtration | Oil change |

---

## 3.5 Numerical — Roller Screw Speed Ratio

A planetary roller screw has $Z_{ring} = 51$ ring teeth and $Z_{roller} = 50$ roller teeth. Screw input speed = 3000 rpm. Find carrier (output) speed.

**Step 1 — Gear ratio formula:**

$$\frac{N_{carrier} - N_{screw}}{0 - N_{screw}} = \frac{Z_{ring}}{Z_{roller}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$\frac{N_{carrier} - 3000}{-3000} = \frac{51}{50} = 1.02$$

$$N_{carrier} - 3000 = -3000 \times 1.02 = -3060$$

$$N_{carrier} = -60 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude of carrier speed (the linear output per screw rev):**

$$N_{carrier} = N_{screw} \left(1 - \frac{Z_{roller}}{Z_{ring} + Z_{roller}}\right) = 3000 \left(1 - \frac{50}{101}\right)$$

$$N_{carrier} = 3000 \times 0.0495 = 148.5 \text{ rpm}$$

(Note: The negative sign in Step 2 indicates opposite direction convention.)

**Step 4 — Linear speed (with lead L = 5 mm):**

$$v_{load} = N_{carrier} \times L = 148.5 \times 5 = 742.5 \text{ mm/min}$$

This demonstrates the **high mechanical advantage** of roller screws: 3000 rpm input → only 148.5 rpm linear-output equivalent.

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between a single-acting and a double-acting pneumatic cylinder. State one industrial application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Single-Acting Cylinder | Double-Acting Cylinder |
|---|---|---|
| Air ports | One | Two (extend + retract) |
| Return mechanism | External spring or load | Pneumatic pressure |
| Force capability | Lower (only one direction) | Both directions |
| Control | Simpler (3/2 valve) | Requires 5/2 or 5/3 valve |
| Application | Bottle ejection on conveyor | Robotic arm joint actuator |

**Applications (3 marks allocation):**
- Single-acting: Clamping in a small drill press, sorting gate actuator, brake actuator in trucks.
- Double-acting: Pick-and-place robot arm, pneumatic press ram, sliding door operator.

*(1 mark: definition; 1 mark: any 2 distinguishing features; 1 mark: application)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**List any three methods of pre-loading a ball screw. Why is pre-loading necessary in CNC machine tool feed drives?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Pre-loading methods (any 3 for 2 marks):**
1. Oversized balls
2. Double-nut with spacer
3. Single-nut with elastic spacer (Belleville washer)
4. Threaded-ring adjustment
5. Offset (stepped-diameter) nut

**Why pre-loading is necessary (1 mark):**
Pre-loading eliminates internal axial play (backlash) and increases axial stiffness, enabling precise bidirectional positioning required in CNC feed drives. Typical backlash reduction: from 0.02 mm → < 0.001 mm.

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]**

**A double-acting hydraulic cylinder is required to push a load of 18 kN against friction. The supply pressure is 140 bar, the return pressure is 5 bar, and the mechanical efficiency of the cylinder is 90%. The rod diameter is 0.6 times the piston diameter.**

#### (a) Calculate the required piston diameter (bore) and the retraction stroke force. (7 marks)
#### (b) Explain with a neat sketch the working of a 4/3 directional control valve used to operate this cylinder. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Bore Calculation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Force during extension:**

$$F_{ext} = P_s \cdot A_p \cdot \eta_{mech} \geq F_{load}$$

$$A_p \geq \frac{F_{load}}{P_s \cdot \eta} = \frac{18{,}000}{140 \times 10^5 \times 0.90}$$

$$A_p \geq \frac{18{,}000}{1.26 \times 10^7} = 1.4286 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Solve for D:**

$$\frac{\pi D^2}{4} = 1.4286 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$D^2 = \frac{4 \times 1.4286 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi} = 1.819 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$D = 0.04265 \text{ m} \approx 43 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Select standard bore: $D = 50$ mm.** *(Ref: ISO 3320 standard sizes 32, 40, 50, 63 mm)*

**Step 4 — Compute retraction force:**

$$d = 0.6 \times 50 = 30 \text{ mm}$$

$$A_p = \frac{\pi (0.050)^2}{4} = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_r = \frac{\pi (0.050^2 - 0.030^2)}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.0025 - 0.0009)}{4} = 1.257 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$F_{ret} = (P_s \cdot A_r - P_r \cdot A_p) \cdot \eta$$

$$F_{ret} = (140 \times 10^5 \times 1.257 \times 10^{-3} - 5 \times 10^5 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.90$$

$$F_{ret} = (17{,}598 - 981.5) \times 0.90$$

$$F_{ret} = 16{,}616.5 \times 0.90 = 14{,}955 \text{ N} \approx 15 \text{ kN}$$

### Part (b) — 4/3 Directional Control Valve (7 Marks)

**Definition:** A 4-way, 3-position (4/3) DCV has 4 ports (P-pressure, T-tank, A & B-cylinder ports) and 3 spool positions (center, left, right). It controls direction, speed, and provides fail-safe center conditions.

**Three Centre Conditions (KTU must-know):**

1. **Tandem Centre:** P blocked, A-B-T open → cylinder ports connected to tank, lock with float.
2. **Closed Centre:** All ports blocked → cylinder locked in position (used for precision holding).
3. **Float Centre (Open Centre):** P-T connected, A-B blocked → pump unloads to tank at zero motion (energy efficient for mobile hydraulics).

**Working (with sketch logic):**
- **Spool LEFT:** P → A (extension), B → T (return)
- **Spool RIGHT:** P → B (retraction), A → T (return)
- **Spool CENTER:** depends on centre condition (above)

**Sketch (ASCII representation):**

```
   P ---+    [==A===]    +--- T
         \  /         \  /
          \/  Spool L  \/
         /\
        /  \  [===B===]/  \
   ---+    +------------+    +---
```

**Actuation Methods:** Solenoid, hydraulic pilot, manual lever, cam-operated.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model]**

**A re-circulating ball screw drive in a CNC milling machine has the following specifications:**
- Screw diameter $d_s = 40$ mm, lead $L = 10$ mm
- Unsupported length = 1.2 m, both ends fixed
- Axial cutting load $F = 25$ kN
- Effective Young's modulus $E = 200$ GPa
- Required life: 10,000 hours at 1500 rpm

#### (a) Check the screw for buckling and compute the actual factor of safety. (7 marks)
#### (b) Compare anti-friction, hydrodynamic, and hydrostatic bearings with respect to friction, starting torque, and stiffness. State one typical application of each. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Buckling Check (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Effective length (both ends fixed, $K = 0.5$):**

$$L_e = K \cdot L = 0.5 \times 1.2 = 0.6 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Moment of inertia:**

$$I = \frac{\pi d_s^4}{64} = \frac{\pi (0.040)^4}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 2.56 \times 10^{-6}}{64}$$

$$I = 1.2566 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 3 — Euler critical load:**

$$F_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot E \cdot I}{L_e^2} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 200 \times 10^9 \times 1.2566 \times 10^{-7}}{(0.6)^2}$$

$$F_{cr} = \frac{9.8696 \times 200 \times 10^9 \times 1.2566 \times 10^{-7}}{0.36}$$

$$F_{cr} = \frac{2.4796 \times 10^5}{0.36} = 68{,}878 \text{ N} \approx 68.9 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Factor of safety:**

$$\text{FoS} = \frac{F_{cr}}{F} = \frac{68{,}878}{25{,}000} = 2.76$$

**Step 5 — Slenderness check:**

$$r = \frac{d_s}{4} = 10 \text{ mm}, \quad \lambda = \frac{600}{10} = 60$$

Since $\lambda < 100$, also check compressive yield ($\sigma_y = 350$ MPa for chrome steel):

$$A = \frac{\pi (0.040)^2}{4} = 1.257 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$F_{yield} = \sigma_y \cdot A = 350 \times 10^6 \times 1.257 \times 10^{-3} = 439{,}950 \text{ N} \approx 440 \text{ kN}$$

**Buckling governs.** FoS = 2.76, **acceptable** (KTU standard requires FoS ≥ 2.0 for machine tool ball screws). ✓

### Part (b) — Bearing Comparison Table (7 Marks)

| Parameter | Anti-friction | Hydrodynamic | Hydrostatic |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Friction Coefficient** | 0.001–0.005 | 0.001–0.01 (speed-dependent) | 0 (fluid film only) |
| **Starting Torque** | Very low | High (boundary layer at start) | Zero |
| **Stiffness (N/μm)** | 50–300 | 100–500 | 500–2000 |
| **Typical Application** | CNC spindle, electric motor shaft | Crankshaft main bearings, turbine | Precision lathe spindle, CMM, telescope mount |

**Key Points (4 marks for narrative):**
- Anti-friction bearings use balls/rollers → rolling contact → very low friction but point/line contact limits load.
- Hydrodynamic bearings rely on the **self-generated wedge** of oil — they need minimum surface speed; below this, metal-to-metal contact occurs.
- Hydrostatic bearings use an **external pump** to pressurize oil into recesses; they work even at zero speed with infinite theoretical stiffness (limited by fluid compressibility).

**Applications (3 marks):**
- Anti-friction: **Electric motor rotor support, conveyor idler pulleys, bicycle hub**
- Hydrodynamic: **IC engine crankshaft journals, turbocharger shaft, large steam turbine**
- Hydrostatic: **Coordinate measuring machine (CMM) axes, precision optical grinding machine spindle, large radar antenna turntable**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning (Module 2):**
> 1. **Buckling:** Many students forget to use **effective length** $L_e = K \cdot L$. Always check end conditions: fixed-fixed ($K = 0.5$), pinned-pinned ($K = 1.0$), fixed-free ($K = 2.0$).
> 2. **Cylinder Sizing:** Do not use $P_s = 160$ bar directly; **convert to Pa** ($1 \text{ bar} = 10^5$ Pa). Always compute the **retraction force** as a follow-up.
> 3. **Bearing Comparison:** Don't confuse "hydrostatic" with "hydrodynamic." Hydro-static = external pressure; hydro-dynamic = self-generated by rotation.
> 4. **Pre-loading Methods:** Always list **at least 3** methods. The most-examined ones: oversized balls, double-nut, elastic spacer.
> 5. **Roller Screw:** Don't confuse **roller screw** (planetary, line contact) with **ball screw** (point contact). Roller screws are **3× stiffer** but **3× more expensive**.

---

## Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### ✅ Hydraulic & Pneumatic Actuators
- [ ] Hydraulic pressure range: **50–350 bar**; Pneumatic: **4–10 bar**
- [ ] Force: $F = P \cdot A \cdot \eta$ (always convert bar → Pa!)
- [ ] Retraction force < Extension force (due to rod area loss)
- [ ] Hydraulic = high force, low speed; Pneumatic = low force, high speed

### ✅ Guide Ways
- [ ] Provide 1 DOF, constrain 5 DOF
- [ ] Sliding, rolling, and hydrostatic types
- [ ] Friction $\mu$: sliding ≈ 0.05, hydrostatic ≈ 0

### ✅ Bearings
- [ ] Anti-friction: rolling elements, low $\mu$ (0.001–0.005)
- [ ] Hydrostatic: external pump, zero starting friction, very high stiffness
- [ ] Hydrodynamic: self-generated wedge, requires minimum speed
- [ ] Sommerfeld number $S_o$ governs eccentricity ratio $\varepsilon$

### ✅ Ball Screws & Pre-loading
- [ ] Efficiency 90–98% (vs. 20–50% for lead screws)
- [ ] Pre-loading methods: oversized balls, double-nut, elastic spacer, offset nut, threaded ring
- [ ] Buckling formula: $F_{cr} = \pi^2 E I / L_e^2$
- [ ] Check $\lambda > 100$ for Euler validity

### ✅ Roller Screws
- [ ] Line contact (vs. point contact in ball screw)
- [ ] 3× higher load capacity, 3× longer life
- [ ] 6–12 planetary rollers, internal ring gear
- [ ] Speed ratio: $N_{carrier} = N_{screw} \left(1 - \frac{Z_{roller}}{Z_{roller} + Z_{ring}}\right)$
- [ ] Applications: aerospace, injection moulding, surgical robots

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pneumatics and Hydraulics: Directional Control Valves, Pressure Control Valves, and Process Control Valves; Rotary Actuators; Development of Simple Hydraulic and Pneumatic Circuits Using Standard Symbols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET745 - MECHATRONICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Pneumatics and Hydraulics: Directional Control Valves, Pressure Control Valves, and Process Control Valves; Rotary Actuators; Development of Simple Hydraulic and Pneumatic Circuits Using Standard Symbols # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Directional Control Valves (DCV) > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition:** A Directional Control Valve is a fluid-power component that directs the flow of compressed air (pneumatic) or hydraulic oil (hydraulic) into specific paths, thereby controlling the direction of motion of actuators (cylinders, motors) or stopping/holding them in position. DCVs are classified by the number of **ports** (connections) and the number of **positions** (switching states). **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a DCV as a **railway track switch**. Just as a track switch decides whether a train goes to Platform A or Platform B, a DCV decides whether the pressurized fluid goes to the **cap end** or the **rod end** of a cylinder, thereby deciding whether the piston extends or retracts. **Classification Nomenclature (KTU Standard):** A DCV is described as **"Number of Ports / Number of Positions"**: - **2/2 Valve** — 2 ports, 2 positions (e.g., simple ON/OFF) - **3/2 Valve** — 3 ports (Pressure, Cylinder, Exhaust), 2 positions (common for single-acting cylinders) - **4/2 Valve** — 4 ports (P, T, A, B), 2 positions (used for double-acting cylinders) - **4/3 Valve** — 4 ports, 3 positions (with a **center condition** — closed, tandem, or float center) - **5/2 Valve** — 5 ports, 2 positions (two exhaust ports for clean return) - **5/3 Valve** — 5 ports, 3 positions (used for precise actuator control) **Actuation Methods:** - **Manual:** Push-button, lever, pedal - **Mechanical:** Cam, roller, spring - **Pneumatic/Hydraulic (Pilot-operated):** Uses a small pilot signal to shift a large main spool - **Electrical (Solenoid-operated):** Most common in mechatronics — energizing a solenoid coil shifts the spool - **Electro-pneumatic / Electro-hydraulic:** Combines electrical pilot signal with pneumatic/hydraulic amplification ## 1.2 Pressure Control Valves (PCV) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** Pressure Control Valves are used to **limit, regulate, or maintain** the pressure level within a fluid power system, protecting components from overpressure and ensuring safe, reliable operation. **Conceptual Analogy:** A pressure control valve behaves like the **safety valve on a pressure cooker**. It opens only when pressure exceeds a safe threshold, releasing the excess to keep the system from bursting. Similarly, a **pressure-reducing valve** acts like a **water-tap regulator** — it ensures the downstream side never exceeds a set pressure, regardless of upstream fluctuations. **Types Covered in KTU Syllabus:** 1. **Pressure Relief Valve (PRV):** Limits the maximum system pressure; opens to tank when set pressure is exceeded. 2. **Pressure Reducing Valve:** Maintains a constant reduced pressure downstream of the valve. 3. **Sequence Valve: ** Opens to allow fluid to a secondary circuit only after a preset pressure is reached in the primary circuit. 4. **Counterbalance (Back-pressure) Valve:** Holds a back-pressure on a vertical load to prevent runaway motion. ## 1.3 Process Control Valves (Flow Control Valves) > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition:** Process Control Valves (primarily **flow control valves**) regulate the **rate of fluid flow**, and consequently the **speed** of actuators. They may also be implemented as **proportional valves** for continuous, variable control. **Conceptual Analogy:** A flow control valve is like a **squeeze on a garden hose** — the harder you squeeze, the slower the water flows out. In hydraulics/pneumatics, by **throttling the exhaust air** (meter-out) or **inlet oil** (meter-in), the actuator speed can be precisely controlled. **Types:** - **Needle valve, Throttle valve, Ball valve** (manual) - **One-way flow control valve** (allows free flow one way, throttled return the other) - **Proportional / Servo valves** (electronic continuous control) ## 1.4 Rotary Actuators > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** A rotary actuator (or fluid motor) is a device that converts the **hydraulic or pneumatic energy** of the working fluid into **continuous rotary mechanical motion** (torque and angular speed), as opposed to the linear motion produced by cylinders. **Conceptual Analogy:** If a cylinder is like a **piston pump pushing a rod in a straight line**, a rotary actuator is like the **engine of a car** — the fluid spins a shaft continuously instead of pushing something back and forth. **Common Types:** - **Vane Motor:** Uses slotted rotor with sliding vanes (compact, medium speed). - **Gear Motor:** Uses external or internal gear meshing (high torque, low speed). - **Piston Motor (Axial/Radial):** Uses reciprocating pistons driving a cam ring (high efficiency, high pressure). - **Pneumatic Air Motor:** Lightweight, safe in explosive environments, can be stalled without damage. ## 1.5 Standard Symbols (ISO 1219) and Circuit Development > [!NOTE] > All KTU exam answers **must** use the **ISO 1219 / ISO 5599** standard symbolic notation. Two squares stacked = 2 positions; arrows inside squares = flow direction; T-shapes = blocked ports; arrows crossing a T = ports connected in that position. **Fundamental Building Blocks of a Fluid Power Circuit:** 1. **Energy Source** — compressor/pump 2. **Conditioning Unit** — filter, regulator, lubricator (FRL) 3. **Control Elements** — DCVs, PCVs, flow control valves 4. **Actuators** — cylinders (linear) or motors (rotary) 5. **Interconnecting Lines** — supply (solid), pilot (dashed), exhaust (with arrow) 6. **Sensors & Feedback** — pressure switches, limit switches, flow sensors # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Directional Control Valve — Operating Principles The **spool** (a precision-ground cylindrical valve element) slides axially inside a **sleeve** (body). Movement of the spool aligns/unaligns internal ports to direct fluid flow. **Why Spool-Type Valves Dominate:** - Quick response - Leak-tight in OFF position - Easy to make multi-way - Compatible with various actuation methods **Key Operating Characteristics:** - **Center Position (for 3-position valves):** - **Closed Center (Tandem):** All ports blocked — actuator is hydraulically locked. - **Open Center (Tandem Center):** P→T, A & B blocked — pump unloads to tank. - **Float Center:** A & B connected to tank, P blocked — actuator floats freely. **Spool Actuation Return Mechanisms:** - Spring return (single solenoid) - Spring offset (pilot/air return) - Detent (holds position without continuous signal) - Bi-stable (two solenoids latch in either direction) ## 2.2 Pressure Control Valve — Operating Principles **Pressure Relief Valve (Most Important in KTU):** - A **poppet** is held against its seat by an adjustable **spring**. - When system pressure > spring setting, the poppet lifts, venting oil to tank. - Once pressure falls below setting, the poppet reseats. - Used as a **safety device** in every hydraulic circuit. **Pressure Reducing Valve:** - Internally similar to a relief valve but **normally open**. - A pilot senses downstream pressure; if it exceeds the set value, the spool throttles flow, reducing downstream pressure. **Sequence Valve:** - Stays closed until inlet pressure reaches the set value. - Used to **sequence operations** — e.g., clamp workpiece first, then feed tool. ## 2.3 Flow Control Valve — Operating Principles **Meter-In vs. Meter-Out:** - **Meter-In:** Flow is restricted into the actuator. Used when **load tends to assist motion** (push load). - **Meter-Out:** Flow is restricted leaving the actuator. Used when **load tends to overrun** (overrun load) — preferred for stability and speed regulation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** For a vertical cylinder holding a load that can fall due to gravity, **meter-out** is always preferred. Meter-out provides better **positional control** and prevents **cavitation**. ## 2.4 Rotary Actuator — Operating Theory **For any rotary actuator, the key relationships are:** - **Displacement Volume ($V_D$):** Volume of fluid consumed per revolution (m³/rev or cc/rev). - **Theoretical Torque ($T$):** Force available to rotate the shaft. - **Theoretical Speed ($N$):** Revolutions per minute. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|----------|---------|-------|-------| | 1 | Cylinder Thrust Force | $F = P \times A$ | N | $P$ in Pa, $A$ in m² | | 2 | Cylinder Piston Area (full bore) | $A_p = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2$ | m² | $D$ = piston diameter | | 3 | Annular Area (rod side) | $A_r = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$ | m² | $d$ = rod diameter | | 4 | Cylinder Velocity | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | m/s | $Q$ in m³/s | | 5 | Cylinder Extension Time | $t = \dfrac{A \times L}{Q}$ | s | $L$ = stroke length | | 6 | Pump/Motor Theoretical Flow | $Q = V_D \times N$ | m³/s | $V_D$ = displacement, $N$ in rev/s | | 7 | Hydraulic Motor Torque | $T = \dfrac{\Delta P \times V_D}{2\pi}$ | N·m | $\Delta P$ = pressure drop | | 8 | Hydraulic Motor Speed | $N = \dfrac{Q}{V_D}$ | rev/s | With no leakage | | 9 | Hydraulic Power | $P_{hyd} = p \times Q$ | W | $p$ in Pa, $Q$ in m³/s | | 10 | Mechanical Power (motor) | $P_{mech} = T \times \omega$ | W | $\omega = 2\pi N$ | | 11 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}$ | dimensionless | Product of volumetric & mechanical | | 12 | Force Ratio (Double-Acting Cylinder) | $\dfrac{F_{ext}}{F_{ret}} = \dfrac{D^2}{D^2 - d^2}$ | ratio | Always > 1 | > [!WARNING] > **Always use SI units consistently:** $p$ in **Pa (N/m²)**, $Q$ in **m³/s**, $N$ in **rev/s** when applying these formulas. Using bar and L/min leads to common unit-conversion errors in KTU exams. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications | Application | Typical Use Case | |-------------|------------------| | Industrial Robots | Hydraulic actuators + proportional DCV for joint actuation | | Injection Molding | High-pressure hydraulics with sequence valves for clamping/injecting | | CNC Machine Tools | Proportional/servo valves for feed-axis control | | Automotive Assembly Lines | Pneumatic cylinders with 5/3 DCV for clamping/transfer | | Aircraft Landing Gear | Hydraulic DCV with closed-center position for positive locking | | Mining & Earthmoving | High-torque hydraulic gear/piston motors | | Dental/Medical Tools | Pneumatic vane motors for low-speed high-torque handpieces | # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Cylinder Force and Velocity Relationships **Given:** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder with piston diameter $D$ = 0.10 m, rod diameter $d$ = 0.06 m, supply pressure $p$ = 80 bar, pump flow $Q$ = 30 L/min. Compute extension force, retraction force, extension velocity, and retraction velocity. ### Step 1: Convert Units to SI Conversion logic: 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa; 1 L = 10⁻³ m³; 1 min = 60 s. $$ p = 80 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 8.0 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} $$ $$ Q = \frac{30 \times 10^{-3}}{60} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ ### Step 2: Compute Piston (Full) Area Conversion logic: For a circular piston, $A_p = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$. $$ A_p = \frac{\pi \times (0.10)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.01}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 $$ ### Step 3: Compute Annular (Rod-Side) Area Conversion logic: Annular area = full piston area minus rod cross-section. $$ A_r = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D^2 - d^2\right) = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(0.10^2 - 0.06^2\right) $$ $$ A_r = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(0.01 - 0.0036\right) = \frac{\pi \times 0.0064}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 $$ ### Step 4: Compute Extension Force Conversion logic: Force = pressure × full piston area (rod-side unloaded). $$ F_{ext} = p \times A_p = 8.0 \times 10^{6} \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} = 62832 \text{ N} $$ $$ \boxed{F_{ext} \approx 62.83 \text{ kN}} $$ ### Step 5: Compute Retraction Force Conversion logic: During retraction, the rod occupies the piston face on the cap side, so effective area is annular. $$ F_{ret} = p \times A_r = 8.0 \times 10^{6} \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} = 40212 \text{ N} $$ $$ \boxed{F_{ret} \approx 40.21 \text{ kN}} $$ ### Step 6: Compute Velocities Conversion logic: Velocity = flow rate / effective area.
