Extension velocity: $$ v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_p} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-4}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0637 \text{ m/s} $$ Retraction velocity: $$ v_{ret} = \frac{Q}{A_r} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-4}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0995 \text{ m/s} $$ ### Step 7: Compute Force Ratio $$ \frac{F_{ext}}{F_{ret}} = \frac{D^2}{D^2 - d^2} = \frac{0.01}{0.0064} = 1.5625 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** A hydraulic cylinder extends with **more force** but **slower speed**, and retracts with **less force** but **faster speed**. This is the classic **force–velocity trade-off** in hydraulic actuators. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Hydraulic Motor Power Transmission **Given:** A hydraulic motor has displacement $V_D = 100 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$, pressure drop across it $\Delta P = 140 \text{ bar}$, volumetric flow rate $Q = 60 \text{ L/min}$, mechanical efficiency $\eta_m = 0.90$, volumetric efficiency $\eta_v = 0.95$. Compute torque, speed, and output power. ### Step 1: Convert Units Conversion logic: 1 cm³ = 10⁻⁶ m³; 1 L = 10⁻³ m³; 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa. $$ V_D = 100 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev} $$ $$ \Delta P = 140 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 1.4 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} $$ $$ Q = \frac{60 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ ### Step 2: Compute Theoretical Speed Conversion logic: $N = Q / V_D$ (ignoring leakage). $$ N_{th} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{1.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 10 \text{ rev/s} = 600 \text{ rpm} $$ ### Step 3: Compute Actual Speed (with Volumetric Efficiency) Conversion logic: Volumetric efficiency accounts for internal leakage — actual flow delivered is less than theoretical. $$ N_{actual} = N_{th} \times \eta_v = 10 \times 0.95 = 9.5 \text{ rev/s} = 570 \text{ rpm} $$ ### Step 4: Compute Theoretical Torque $$ T_{th} = \frac{\Delta P \times V_D}{2\pi} = \frac{1.4 \times 10^{7} \times 1.0 \times 10^{-4}}{2\pi} $$ $$ T_{th} = \frac{1400}{6.2832} = 222.8 \text{ N·m} $$ ### Step 5: Compute Actual Torque $$ T_{actual} = T_{th} \times \eta_m = 222.8 \times 0.90 = 200.5 \text{ N·m} $$ ### Step 6: Compute Output Power $$ P_{out} = T_{actual} \times \omega = 200.5 \times 2\pi \times 9.5 $$ $$ P_{out} = 200.5 \times 59.69 = 11967 \text{ W} \approx 12.0 \text{ kW} $$ ### Step 7: Verify with Input Power $$ P_{in} = \Delta P \times Q = 1.4 \times 10^{7} \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3} = 14000 \text{ W} = 14.0 \text{ kW} $$ $$ \eta_{overall} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{12000}{14000} = 0.857 \approx 85.7\% $$ > [!NOTE] > This matches: $\eta_{overall} = \eta_v \times \eta_m = 0.95 \times 0.90 = 0.855$ ✓ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — DCV Symbol Quick-Reference | Valve Type | Symbol (Position 1) | Symbol (Position 2) | Application | |------------|---------------------|---------------------|-------------| | 2/2 NC | Blocked P→A | Connected P→A | ON/OFF air supply | | 3/2 NC (button) | A→T, P blocked | P→A, T blocked | Single-acting cylinder | | 4/2 DCV | P→A, B→T | P→B, A→T | Double-acting cylinder | | 4/3 Closed-Center | All blocked (center) | Two distinct flow paths | Locked actuator | | 4/3 Tandem-Center | P→T (center), A,B blocked | Two distinct flow paths | Pump unloads | | 5/2 DCV | P→A, B→R→T, S→T | P→B, A→R→T, S→T | Double-acting (clean exhaust) | | 5/3 Closed-Center | All blocked | Two distinct flow paths | Position holding | > [!TIP] > **Memorize the symbols:** Two adjacent **filled triangles** = pressurized supply; **empty triangle** = exhaust (to tank); **T-intersection** = ports connected; **perpendicular bar across T** = ports blocked. --- ## 3.4 Numerical Problem Bank (for self-practice) | # | Problem Statement | Key Formulae Used | |---|-------------------|-------------------| | 1 | A 5/2 DCV controls a cylinder. Draw the circuit with FRL and a meter-out flow control. | ISO 1219 symbols | | 2 | A hydraulic motor with $V_D$ = 50 cc/rev at $\Delta P$ = 100 bar, $Q$ = 25 L/min. Find $T$, $N$. | $T = \Delta P \cdot V_D / 2\pi$; $N = Q / V_D$ | | 3 | Cylinder with $D$ = 80 mm, $d$ = 45 mm, $p$ = 60 bar. Find extension/retraction forces. | $F = pA$ | | 4 | Determine pump size for cylinder with $A$ = 20 cm², stroke = 0.5 m, cycle time = 3 s. | $Q = A \cdot L / t$ | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!IMPORTANT] > The following diagrams use the **ISO 1219 symbolic notation** which is the KTU-mandated standard for fluid power circuit representation. Each diagram maps a specific physical component to its standardized graphic symbol. ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic Fluid Power System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Power Source
Compressor or Pump] --> B1[Conditioning Unit
Filter Regulator Lubricator] B1 --> C1[Pressure Control Valve
Relief Reducing Sequence] C1 --> D1[Directional Control Valve
DCV 5 by 2 or 4 by 2] D1 --> E1[Flow Control Valve
Meter in or Meter out] E1 --> F1[Actuator
Cylinder or Rotary Motor] F1 --> G1[Feedback Sensors
Pressure Switch Limit Switch] G1 --> D1 A1 --> C1 F1 --> H1[Tank or Reservoir] D1 --> H1 C1 --> H1 ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: 5/2 DCV Operating Two Positions with Double-Acting Cylinder ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Pos1["Position 1: Solenoid A Energized"] P1[Pressure Supply] -->|P to A| CV1[Cap End of Cylinder] RV1[Rod End of Cylinder] -->|B to T| EX1[Exhaust to Tank] end subgraph Pos2["Position 2: Solenoid B Energized"] P2[Pressure Supply] -->|P to B| RV2[Rod End of Cylinder] CV2[Cap End of Cylinder] -->|A to T| EX2[Exhaust to Tank] end subgraph CTRL["Control Logic"] SOLA[Solenoid Coil A] -.-> Pos1 SOLB[Solenoid Coil B] -.-> Pos2 end ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Pressure Relief Valve Operation Logic ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[System Pressure p] -->|Force on Poppet| P1{Pressure Check} P1 -->|p less than set| SB1[Spring Holds Poppet Closed] P1 -->|p greater than set| OP1[Poppet Lifts Open] OP1 --> V1[Vent Oil to Tank] SB1 --> CY1[System Pressurized Normally] V1 --> UN1[Pressure Capped at Set Value] CY1 --> UN1 ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram: Sequence Valve application in Two-Stage Clamping Circuit ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Pump Supply] --> DCV[4 by 2 DCV] DCV -->|Output A| CL1[Clamp Cylinder 1] CL1 -->|Reaches End| SQ1[Pressure Rises to Set Value] SQ1 -->|Triggers| SEQV[Sequence Valve Opens] SEQV --> CL2[Clamp Cylinder 2 Activates] DCV -->|Return Path| T1[Tank] CL1 --> T1 CL2 --> T1 ``` ## 4.5 Block Diagram: Hydraulic Rotary Motor System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Hydraulic Pump] --> PC1[Pressure Control Valve] PC1 --> DCV1[Directional Control Valve] DCV1 --> M1[Hydraulic Motor] M1 -->|Mechanical Output| SH1[Output Shaft with Torque T] M1 -->|Return Flow| DCV1 DCV1 --> T1[Reservoir] P1 --> RV1[Relief Valve Connected to Tank] M1 -.->|Speed and Torque Feedback| CTL1[Electronic Controller] CTL1 -.-> DCV1 ``` ## 4.6 Block Diagram: Pneumatic Speed Control Circuit (Meter-Out) Logic ```mermaid flowchart LR PS1[Compressed Air Supply] --> FRL1[Filter Regulator Lubricator] FRL1 --> DCV2[3 by 2 DCV Normally Closed] DCV2 -->|Extends| CYL1[Single Acting Cylinder] CYL1 --> FC1[One Way Flow Control Valve] FC1 --> EX2[Exhaust Throttled to Control Speed] DCV2 --> EP1[Exhaust Port Quick Venting] CYL1 -.->|Spring Return| DCV2 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Key Learning from Diagrams:** In the **meter-out speed control** above, the **exhaust air** is throttled, not the inlet air. This gives more **stable speed control** against varying loads, which is the KTU-preferred configuration for most applications. ## 4.7 Process Control Valve Architecture in a Closed-Loop System ```mermaid flowchart TB REF1[Reference Input Setpoint] --> COMP[Comparator] FBK1[Feedback from Process Sensor] --> COMP COMP -->|Error Signal| CTRL[Controller PID] CTRL --> AMP[Amplifier] AMP --> PRV[Proportional Pressure or Flow Valve] PRV --> ACT[Hydraulic or Pneumatic Actuator] ACT --> PROC[Industrial Process Load] PROC --> FBK1 ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Q: Differentiate between a 3/2 directional control valve and a 5/2 directional control valve. State one application of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | 3/2 DCV | 5/2 DCV | |--------|---------|---------| | **Number of Ports** | 3 (Pressure, Cylinder, Exhaust) | 5 (Pressure, 2 cylinder ports, 2 exhausts) | | **Number of Positions** | 2 | 2 | | **Cylinder Type** | Single-acting | Double-acting | | **Exhaust Path** | Single exhaust port | Separate exhausts for cap and rod sides | | **Symbol** | Two square boxes with 3 connection points | Two square boxes with 5 connection points | | **Application** | Operating a small clamping pin (single-acting) | Controlling a double-acting cylinder in an assembly line transfer arm | A 3/2 DCV is **simpler, cheaper, and used for low-power single-acting actuators**, whereas a 5/2 DCV provides **independent exhaust paths** giving cleaner, faster operation of double-acting cylinders. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Q: Define a pressure relief valve. Explain with a sketch how it operates as a safety device in a hydraulic system.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > A **pressure relief valve** is a normally-closed pressure control valve that opens automatically when the system pressure exceeds a preset safe value, venting excess fluid to the tank. **Operation:** 1. A spring-loaded **poppet** is held firmly against its **valve seat** by an adjustable spring. 2. System pressure acts on the underside of the poppet, generating an upward force. 3. As long as the hydraulic force is **less than** the spring force, the valve remains closed and the system is pressurized normally. 4. When system pressure **exceeds** the spring setting (e.g., due to a blockage or overload), the hydraulic force overcomes the spring force, the poppet lifts, and excess oil escapes to the tank. 5. Once pressure drops below the set value, the spring reseats the poppet, and the system returns to normal operation. **Role as Safety Device:** It **protects the pump, actuators, and piping** from catastrophic failure due to over-pressure conditions, which is mandatory in every industrial hydraulic circuit per safety standards (ISO 4413). --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions with Internal Choice (Module Choice Pattern) > [!IMPORTANT] > Per KTU 2024 Scheme, each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must attempt **either Question A or Question B** (internal choice between two 14-mark options). --- ### **Question A (14 marks) — Track A: Design and Analysis** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]** **Part (a) [7 marks]:** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a piston diameter of 100 mm and a rod diameter of 60 mm. It is supplied by a pump delivering 40 L/min at a pressure of 100 bar. Calculate: - (i) Extension and retraction forces - (ii) Extension and retraction velocities - (iii) Time required for a stroke of 300 mm during extension **Part (b) [7 marks]:** With the help of a labeled ISO 1219 circuit diagram, describe the operation of a **sequencing circuit** using a 4/2 DCV and a sequence valve to operate two double-acting cylinders in a definite order (cylinder A extends fully, retracts; then cylinder B extends fully, retracts). #### **Model Solution to Part (a):** **Step 1: Convert Units** $$ D = 0.100 \text{ m}, \quad d = 0.060 \text{ m}, \quad p = 100 \times 10^5 = 1.0 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} $$ $$ Q = \frac{40 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ **Step 2: Compute Areas** $$ A_p = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.10)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 $$ $$ A_r = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.10^2 - 0.06^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.01 - 0.0036) = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 $$ **Step 3: Compute Forces** $$ F_{ext} = p \cdot A_p = 1.0 \times 10^7 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} = 78540 \text{ N} = 78.54 \text{ kN} $$ $$ F_{ret} = p \cdot A_r = 1.0 \times 10^7 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} = 50270 \text{ N} = 50.27 \text{ kN} $$ **Step 4: Compute Velocities** $$ v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_p} = \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0849 \text{ m/s} $$ $$ v_{ret} = \frac{Q}{A_r} = \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1326 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step 5: Compute Stroke Time** $$ t = \frac{L}{v_{ext}} = \frac{0.300}{0.0849} = 3.534 \text{ s} $$ #### **Model Solution to Part (b):** **Sequence Circuit Description (using ISO 1219 symbols):** **Stage 1 — Cylinder A extends:** - Solenoid of 4/2 DCV is energized; pressure flows to cap end of Cylinder A. - Cylinder A's rod end is connected to tank via DCV; Cylinder A extends. - Pressure builds up only after the load is met; the sequence valve is set to a higher pressure. **Stage 2 — Cylinder A retracts, Cylinder B extends:** - A second 4/2 DCV (or pilot-operated stage) is triggered; Cylinder A retracts. - After Cylinder A is fully retracted, pressure rises to the sequence valve setting. - The sequence valve opens, allowing flow to Cylinder B's DCV, which extends Cylinder B. **Stage 3 — Cylinder B retracts:** - The DCV for Cylinder B is reversed; Cylinder B retracts to its home position. **Application Example:** Such a circuit is used in a **mold-clamping and injection** process where: - The **clamp** (Cylinder A) must close fully before **injection** (Cylinder B) starts. - The **injection ram** (Cylinder B) must retract before the **clamp** can open. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to label the **pilot line** (dashed) in a pilot-operated sequence circuit, costing 2 marks. Also, **port numbering (P, T, A, B)** must be included on the DCV symbol for full credit. --- ### **Question B (14 marks) — Track B: Component Knowledge and Applications** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]** **Part (a) [7 marks]:** Explain with neat ISO 1219 standard symbols, the construction and working of: - (i) 4/3 closed-center DCV - (ii) One-way flow control valve - (iii) Counterbalance valve **Part (b) [7 marks]:** Compare **pneumatic and hydraulic rotary actuators** (vane, gear, piston types). State the preferred application area for each. #### **Model Solution to Part (a):** **(i) 4/3 Closed-Center DCV:** - Has 4 ports: **P** (pressure), **T** (tank), **A** and **B** (actuator). - Three positions: Left, Center, Right. - In the **center position**, **all four ports are blocked** — symbolized by four T-shapes with perpendicular bars. - The actuator (cylinder or motor) is **hydraulically locked** in the center — useful for **vertical load holding** in machine tools and presses. - Spool is spring-centered; pilot pressure on either end shifts the spool to the respective end position. **(ii) One-Way Flow Control Valve:** - A **throttle valve** placed in parallel with a **check valve**. - Flow in one direction passes **freely** through the check valve (full flow, no throttling). - Flow in the opposite direction is **forced through the throttle** (restricted flow, controlled speed). - Common use: **Meter-out speed control of double-acting cylinders** where the rod-side return is throttled. - Symbol: A throttle symbol (constricted line) parallel to a check valve symbol (ball-and-seat) in a single block. **(iii) Counterbalance Valve:** - A **pilot-assisted pressure control valve** mounted on the return line of a vertical cylinder. - Maintains a **back-pressure** sufficient to support the weight of the load against gravity. - When the cylinder's downward motion is initiated, the pilot pressure drops, opening the valve to allow flow out — but the back-pressure prevents runaway. - Symbol: A pressure relief valve symbol with a **pilot line connection from the inlet side** of the cylinder. - Application: Holds a vertical load (e.g., a die on a press) safely during a power failure. #### **Model Solution to Part (b):** | Feature | Pneumatic Rotary Actuator | Hydraulic Rotary Actuator | |---------|---------------------------|---------------------------| | **Working Fluid** | Compressed air (~6-8 bar) | Hydraulic oil (~100-350 bar) | | **Power Density** | Low (0.5-1 kW/kg) | High (3-5 kW/kg) | | **Speed Range** | Very high (up to 30,000 rpm) | Moderate (up to 5000 rpm) | | **Torque Capability** | Low to medium | Very high | | **Overload Behavior** | Stalls safely without damage | Stalling causes overheating/damage | | **Leakage & Contamination** | Air leaks harmless | Oil leaks = fire/slip hazard | | **Cost** | Lower initial cost | Higher (precision components) | | **Maintenance** | Simpler, drier system | Requires filtration, coolers | **Type-wise Application:** | Type | Pneumatic Use | Hydraulic Use | |------|---------------|---------------| | **Vane Motor** | Pneumatic hand tools, dental drills, air turbines | Low-pressure machine tool spindles | | **Gear Motor** | High-speed paint sprayers, mixers | Agricultural machinery, conveyor drives | | **Piston Motor (Axial/Radial)** | Less common | Excavators, winches, injection molding, marine drives | | **Turbine Motor** | Pneumatic high-speed spindles | Not common | > [!NOTE] > **Key Selection Rule:** Pneumatic motors are preferred for **lightweight, high-speed, intrinsically safe** (explosive environments) applications. Hydraulic motors are preferred where **high torque, high power density, and precise speed control** are required. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in This Module:** > 1. **Forgetting to use ISO 1219 standard symbols** → Up to 3 marks lost in circuit diagrams. > 2. **Mixing up "meter-in" and "meter-out"** terminology → 1-2 marks lost on speed control questions. > 3. **Not labeling the center condition of 3-position valves** (closed/tandem/float) → 2 marks lost. > 4. **Wrong units in numerical problems** (using bar directly with cm² instead of Pa with m²) → 1-2 marks lost. > 5. **Missing the dashed pilot line** in pilot-operated DCV/sequence valve circuits → 1 mark lost. > 6. **Failing to mention safety devices** (relief valve, FRL) in any drawn circuit → 1-2 marks lost. > 7. **Confusing "ports" with "positions"** in DCV nomenclature → Conceptual marks lost. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Read this checklist 3 times before entering the KTU exam hall.** ### ⭐ Directional Control Valves (DCV) - ☐ DCV nomenclature = **Ports / Positions** (e.g., 4/2, 5/3). - ☐ 3/2 → single-acting cylinder; 4/2 or 5/2 → double-acting cylinder. - ☐ 3-position valves have a **center condition**: closed, tandem, or float. - ☐ Solenoid-operated DCVs are **most common in mechatronics**. - ☐ Symbol: 2 squares (positions) with arrows showing flow paths. - ☐ Spring return → single solenoid; bi-stable → two solenoids latch. ### ⭐ Pressure Control Valves (PCV) - ☐ **Relief valve** = safety device, normally closed, opens at set pressure. - ☐ **Reducing valve** = maintains constant downstream pressure, normally open. - ☐ **Sequence valve** = opens at preset pressure to activate secondary circuit. - ☐ **Counterbalance valve** = holds vertical load against gravity. ### ⭐ Process / Flow Control Valves - ☐ Throttle/needle valve = manual speed control. - ☐ **One-way flow control** = free flow one way, throttled return. - ☐ **Meter-in** = throttle inlet flow; **Meter-out** = throttle exhaust flow. - ☐ Meter-out is preferred for **overrun loads** (vertical, inertial). - ☐ **Proportional/servo valves** allow continuous, electronic flow/pressure control. ### ⭐ Rotary Actuators - ☐ Convert fluid energy → **continuous rotary motion** (torque + speed). - ☐ Key formulas: $T = \Delta P \cdot V_D / 2\pi$; $N = Q / V_D$. - ☐ Vane → compact, medium pressure; Gear → high torque low speed; Piston → high efficiency, high pressure. - ☐ Pneumatic motors **stall safely**; hydraulic motors **overheat if stalled**. ### ⭐ Circuit Design Conventions - ☐ Always use **ISO 1219** standard symbols in KTU answers. - ☐ Every circuit must show: **Pump/Compressor → FRL → DCV → Actuator → Tank**. - ☐ **Relief valve** must be present and connected back to tank for safety. - ☐ **Solid lines** = main/signal flow; **Dashed lines** = pilot/control flow. - ☐ **Arrows in symbols** = direction of flow; **T-bars** = blocked ports. - ☐ Number sub-components and provide a **working description** of each stage. ### ⭐ Critical Formulas to Memorize - ☐ $F = p \cdot A$ (cylinder force) - ☐ $v = Q / A$ (cylinder velocity) - ☐ $T = \Delta P \cdot V_D / 2\pi$ (motor torque) - ☐ $N = Q / V_D$ (motor speed) - ☐ $P = p \cdot Q$ (hydraulic power) - ☐ $\eta = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$ (overall efficiency) ### ⭐ Real-World KTU-Favorite Examples - ☐ **Mold clamping** in injection molding = sequence valve application. - ☐ **Vertical load holding** = counterbalance valve. - ☐ **Punch press safety** = 4/3 closed-center DCV. - ☐ **Robot arm actuation** = proportional valve + double-acting cylinder. - ☐ **Conveyor drive** = hydraulic gear motor. - ☐ **Pneumatic assembly clamp** = 3/2 NC push-button valve + spring-return cylinder. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** Always draw a **labeled circuit diagram** (with component names, port letters, and pilot lines) **before** writing the descriptive theory. This guarantees you capture the **7–8 marks** awarded for diagrams in any Part B question. Then support the diagram with a clear, step-by-step **operational description** of the fluid flow in each stage.

topics.title: Electric Motors: BLDC and Stepper Motors; Torque-Speed Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET745 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electric Motors: BLDC and Stepper Motors; Torque-Speed Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Brushless DC (BLDC) Motor — Formal KTU Definition

A **Brushless DC (BLDC) Motor** is a synchronous electric motor powered by direct-current electricity, in which the rotor is a permanent magnet and the stator carries the polyphase armature winding. Commutation is achieved *electronically* by an inverter/controller that switches stator phase currents in synchronism with rotor position feedback (Hall sensors, encoders, or sensorless back-EMF sensing). The ideal back-EMF waveform is **trapezoidal**, giving the motor its DC-like V–I characteristics despite being inherently an AC machine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Definition to Memorise:**
> *"A BLDC motor is a self-controlled, electronically commutated synchronous motor with permanent-magnet rotor and trapezoidal back-EMF, where the inverter switching is derived from rotor-position sensing."*

**Conceptual Analogy — The Spinning Compass with a Choreographer:**
Imagine a magnetic compass needle (the rotor magnet) sitting on a turntable. A conductor (choreographer) standing beside it senses where the needle points and — without ever touching it — pushes magnetic fields in the stator windings to gently pull the needle forward. There is no rubbing contact (no brushes), so there is no friction or sparking. That non-touching "invisible hand" is the electronic controller of a BLDC motor.

### 1.2 Stepper Motor — Formal KTU Definition

A **Stepper Motor** is a brushless, synchronous electro-mechanical actuator that converts a discrete train of input electrical pulses into precisely defined angular increments (*steps*). The shaft rotates in fixed angular displacements (typical step angles: **1.8°** and **0.9°**) without requiring a position feedback sensor in open-loop configuration. Stepper motors are commonly classified into **Variable Reluctance (VR)**, **Permanent Magnet (PM)**, and **Hybrid (HB)** types.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Term — "Step Angle":** The angular displacement produced per input pulse, given by $\theta_s = \dfrac{360^\circ}{N_r \cdot N_{ph}}$, where $N_r$ is the number of rotor teeth and $N_{ph}$ the number of stator phases pulsed per step.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Stair-Climber:**
Picture a person climbing a spiral staircase (the rotor teeth) whose hand is always placed on the next higher step by a guide (the stator). Each time the guide says "step," the person moves up by exactly one stair height. Whether the person is told to take 1 step or 1000 steps, they reach a precise location. That deterministic "one-pulse-one-step" nature is the defining feature of a stepper motor.

### 1.3 Standardised Performance Metrics (Syllabus Constants)

The following physical constants and performance indices are routinely tested in KTU examinations:

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $K_e$ | Back-EMF constant | **V·s/rad** |
| $K_t$ | Torque constant | **N·m/A** |
| $J$ | Rotor moment of inertia | **kg·m²** |
| $B$ | Viscous friction coefficient | **N·m·s/rad** |
| $\theta_s$ | Step angle | **degrees** or **radians** |
| $T_{hold}$ | Holding torque | **N·m** |
| $T_{pull\text{-}out}$ | Maximum load torque at given speed | **N·m** |
| $\omega_{max}$ | Maximum no-load speed | **rad/s** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Knee-Point Fact (BLDC):** For a BLDC motor, $K_e$ (in V/rad/s) equals $K_t$ (in N·m/A) in SI units. This is a direct consequence of energy conservation in the electromechanical conversion process and is a frequently asked 2-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 BLDC Motor — Working Principle (Structured Logic)

- **Construction**: A BLDC has a permanent-magnet rotor (Surface-mounted or Interior) and a 3-phase star-connected (Y) or delta-connected stator winding. Hall-effect sensors (H1, H2, H3) are mounted on the stator at 120° electrical spacing.
- **Rotor Position Sensing**: Three Hall sensors detect the rotor's angular position every 60° electrical. The sensor outputs are decoded (typically 6 valid states out of $2^3 = 8$ combinations) to generate the 6-step commutation sequence.
- **Electronic Commutation**: A 3-phase bridge inverter (6 MOSFETs/IGBTs) energises two of the three phases at any instant. The energized phases produce a magnetic field vector that "chases" the rotor, producing continuous torque.
- **Trapezoidal vs. Sinusoidal Drive**: BLDCs fed with rectangular current pulses (matching trapezoidal back-EMF) are called *trapezoidal* or *six-step* motors. When fed with sinusoidal currents to a motor with sinusoidal back-EMF, it is operated as a **Permanent-Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM)** — a related but distinct category.
- **Sensorless Operation**: At higher speeds, back-EMF zero-crossings can replace Hall sensors; at very low speeds, this fails, which is a frequent viva question.

### 2.2 BLDC Motor — Torque Production Equation

The instantaneous electromagnetic torque developed by a BLDC is given by:

$$
T_e = \frac{(e_a i_a + e_b i_b + e_c i_c)}{\omega_m}
$$

where $e_a, e_b, e_c$ are the phase back-EMFs and $i_a, i_b, i_c$ the phase currents.

For two phases conducting at any instant (six-step mode) with the third phase idle:

$$
T_e = \frac{2 E_{\text{peak}} I_{\text{peak}}}{\omega_m} = 2 K_t I_{\text{peak}}
$$

This is because during the *flat-top* region of the trapezoidal back-EMF, $e_a - e_b = 2E_{\text{peak}}$, and the equivalent DC link current $I_{\text{dc}} = I_{\text{peak}}$.

### 2.3 BLDC Motor — Equivalent Circuit & Voltage Equation

The per-phase equivalent circuit of a BLDC motor in the DC-link domain yields the well-known "DC-motor-like" equation:

$$
V_s = I \cdot R + L\,\frac{dI}{dt} + E_b
$$

where:
- $V_s$ = DC supply voltage (**V**)
- $I$ = DC link current (**A**)
- $R$ = phase-to-phase equivalent resistance (**Ω**)
- $L$ = phase-to-phase equivalent inductance (**H**)
- $E_b = K_e \cdot \omega_m$ = back-EMF (**V**)

### 2.4 BLDC — Torque-Speed Characteristic (Region of Operation)

The BLDC torque-speed curve is essentially a **straight line with negative slope**, valid in the **continuous-conduction region** (CCR):

$$
\omega_m = \frac{V_s}{K_e} - \frac{R}{(K_e)^2}\, T_e
$$

Three operating regions can be identified:

- **Region I — Constant Torque (Below Base Speed)**: Current-limited region; $T_e$ is held constant by current-control loop; speed varies up to $\omega_{base}$.
- **Region II — Constant Power (Above Base Speed)**: Voltage-limited region achieved by *field-weakening* (advancing commutation angle); torque falls as $1/\omega$.
- **Region III — High-speed Series Region**: Dominated by $I^2R$ losses; practical operating limit.

### 2.5 Stepper Motor — Working Principle

- **Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper**: Rotor is a soft-iron toothed cylinder (no magnet). Stator poles are also toothed. When a phase is energised, the rotor moves to the position of **minimum reluctance** (maximum inductance). Sequential pulsing causes stepping.
- **Permanent Magnet (PM) Stepper**: Rotor carries axially magnetised poles. Energising stator coils reverses the polarity seen by the rotor, causing it to align with the new stator field — step motion. Step angles are typically **45° or 90°**.
- **Hybrid (HB) Stepper**: Combines PM rotor with toothed stator/rotor geometry, yielding small step angles (**0.9°, 1.8°**) with high holding torque. Most common industrial type.

### 2.6 Stepper Motor — Key Formulae

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360^\circ}{m \cdot N_r} \quad \text{(for PM and HB multi-stack types)}
$$

$$
f_{step} = \frac{n}{60} \cdot N_r \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_s = \frac{2\pi}{N_r}
$$

where $m$ = phases pulsed per step, $N_r$ = total number of rotor pole-pairs pulsed per revolution, $n$ = rotor rpm, $f_{step}$ = pulse rate.

**Pull-out torque** (maximum load torque before losing synchronism) decreases approximately with:

$$
T_{pull\text{-}out}(\omega) \approx \frac{T_0}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega/\omega_n)^2}}
$$

where $\omega_n$ is the natural mechanical frequency of the rotor-load system.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Equation | Meaning | Units | Valid For |
|---|---|---|---|
| $E_b = K_e \omega_m$ | Back-EMF relation | V, rad/s | All PM motors |
| $T_e = K_t I$ | Torque-current relation | N·m, A | BLDC, PM DC |
| $V_s = IR + L\,dI/dt + E_b$ | Voltage equation | V | DC & BLDC |
| $\omega_m = \dfrac{V_s}{K_e} - \dfrac{R}{K_e^2}T_e$ | Torque-speed line | rad/s | BLDC (CCR) |
| $\theta_s = \dfrac{360^\circ}{m N_r}$ | Step angle | degrees | Stepper |
| $f_{res} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{K \cdot N_r^2}{J}}$ | Resonance freq. | Hz | Stepper |
| $T_{hold} = 2 \cdot T_{pull\text{-}in}$ | Approximate empirical rule | N·m | Stepper |
| $K_e \text{(SI)} = K_t \text{(SI)}$ | SI-unit equivalence | — | All rotary PM motors |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Utility — Why KTU Tests This:**
> BLDC motors dominate **CNC spindle drives, EV traction, hard-disk drives, drone propulsion, and aerospace actuators** because they offer high power density with zero brush wear. Stepper motors dominate **3-D printers, CNC X/Y/Z axes, telescope tracking, and valve actuation** where open-loop precision and holding torque at zero speed are mandatory. The torque-speed characteristic dictates *controller design choices* (current loop bandwidth, field-weakening capability, gearhead selection).

### 2.8 Comparison Table — BLDC vs. Stepper

| Parameter | BLDC Motor | Stepper Motor |
|---|---|---|
| Commutation | Electronic (continuous) | Electronic (pulsed) |
| Feedback | Required (Hall/encoder/sensorless) | Not required (open loop) |
| Holding torque at zero speed | Zero (free rotation) | High (detent + holding) |
| Speed range | Up to **100,000 rpm** | Up to **~3,000 rpm** |
| Efficiency | High (**85–95 %**) | Low to moderate (**30–70 %**) |
| Position accuracy | Encoder-dependent | Step-resolution-limited |
| Resonance | No significant issue | Major issue at low speeds |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Back-EMF Constant from Faraday's Law

For a single conductor of length $\ell$ moving at velocity $v$ perpendicular to a magnetic field of density $B$:

$$
e = B \ell v
$$

For a multi-turn coil with $N$ turns rotating at angular velocity $\omega_m$ at radius $r$:

$$
v = \omega_m r
$$

$$
e_{peak} = B \ell N (\omega_m r) = (B \ell N r)\, \omega_m
$$

Therefore, by definition:

$$
K_e = B \ell N r \quad \text{(units: V·s/rad)}
$$

**Energy equivalence reasoning for $K_t = K_e$:**

In SI units, the input electrical power converted to mechanical form is:

$$
P_{conv} = e \cdot i = K_e \omega_m \cdot i
$$

Equating this to mechanical output power $T_e \omega_m$:

$$
T_e \omega_m = K_e \omega_m i \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_e = K_e i
$$

Hence, in SI units:

$$
\boxed{K_t \;[\text{N·m/A}] = K_e \;[\text{V·s/rad}]}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: BLDC Steady-State Torque-Speed Equation

Starting from the DC-link side voltage equation (assuming quasi-steady state, $dI/dt = 0$):

$$
V_s = I R + E_b
$$

Substitute $E_b = K_e \omega_m$ and $T_e = K_t I = K_e I$:

$$
V_s = \frac{T_e}{K_t} R + K_e \omega_m
$$

Solve for $\omega_m$:

$$
K_e \omega_m = V_s - \frac{T_e R}{K_t}
$$

$$
\omega_m = \frac{V_s}{K_e} - \frac{R}{K_e K_t}\, T_e
$$

Using $K_e = K_t$ in SI units:

$$
\boxed{\omega_m = \frac{V_s}{K_e} - \frac{R}{K_e^{\,2}}\, T_e}
$$

This is a **linear equation** in $T_e$ with:
- **No-load speed** ($\omega_0$): $T_e = 0 \Rightarrow \omega_0 = V_s / K_e$
- **Stall torque** ($T_{stall}$): $\omega_m = 0 \Rightarrow T_{stall} = V_s K_e / R$

### 3.3 Derivation: Step Angle of a Hybrid Stepper

A hybrid stepper has two rotor cups, each with $N_r$ teeth, displaced axially by half a tooth pitch. The stator has $N_s$ teeth per pole. The full stepping motion results from the combined detent effects.

For a hybrid stepper with $N_r$ rotor teeth (one cup considered) and stator phase excitation:

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360^\circ}{2 \cdot N_r \cdot m}
$$

where $m$ = number of phases (typically 2 for HB).

For the **most common HB stepper** ($m = 2$, two phases):

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360^\circ}{4 \cdot N_r}
$$

**Numerical example:** A 50-tooth hybrid rotor:

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360^\circ}{4 \times 50} = \frac{360^\circ}{200} = 1.8^\circ
$$

This is the canonical **1.8° stepper** used in 3-D printers — a KTU viva favourite.

### 3.4 Derivation: Stepper Pull-In and Pull-Out Torque Characteristics

Consider a stepper driving a load with total inertia $J$ and friction $B$, under viscous-damped behaviour. The transfer function from phase current to rotor position exhibits mechanical resonance at:

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K_t K_{\theta}}{J}}
$$

where $K_{\theta}$ is the torque-angle stiffness (N·m/rad). The maximum starting (pull-in) pulse rate before losing synchronism is given by:

$$
f_{pull\text{-}in} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{K_{\theta}}{J}} - \text{(friction term)}
$$

At steady-state running, the **pull-out torque** as a function of stepping frequency $f$ follows approximately:

$$
T_{pull\text{-}out}(f) = \frac{T_0}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{f}{f_n}\right)^2}}
$$

with $f_n = \omega_n / (2\pi)$ being the natural stepping frequency.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Selecting Motor for Application

| Application | Torque Req. | Speed Req. | Position Req. | Recommended Motor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CNC spindle (high speed) | Medium | **Very high** | Encoder-based | **BLDC / PMSM** |
| 3-D printer X/Y axis | Medium | Low | Open-loop OK | **Stepper (1.8°)** |
| Robotic arm joint | High | Medium | Closed-loop | **BLDC + harmonic drive** |
| Drone propeller | Low | **Very high** | — | **Outrunner BLDC** |
| Telescope RA drive | Low | Very low | High precision | **Stepper with gearing** |
| HDD spindle | Low | Very high | Speed stability | **BLDC** |
| Pick-and-place Z-axis | High at standstill | Low | Holding force | **Stepper with brake** |

### 3.6 Numerical Worked Example — BLDC Torque-Speed

**Given:** A BLDC motor with $V_s = 48$ V, $R = 0.5\ \Omega$, $K_e = 0.05$ V·s/rad.

**Find:** (a) No-load speed, (b) Stall torque, (c) Speed at $T_e = 4$ N·m.

**Solution:**

(a) No-load speed:

$$
\omega_0 = \frac{V_s}{K_e} = \frac{48}{0.05} = 960 \text{ rad/s}
$$

Convert to rpm:

$$
n_0 = \omega_0 \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = 960 \times 9.5493 \approx 9{,}167 \text{ rpm}
$$

(b) Stall torque:

$$
T_{stall} = \frac{V_s K_e}{R} = \frac{48 \times 0.05}{0.5} = 4.8 \text{ N·m}
$$

(c) Speed at $T_e = 4$ N·m:

$$
\omega = \frac{48}{0.05} - \frac{0.5}{(0.05)^2} \times 4 = 960 - 800 = 160 \text{ rad/s}
$$

In rpm:

$$
n = 160 \times 9.5493 \approx 1{,}528 \text{ rpm}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 BLDC Motor — Functional Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["DC SUPPLY V_S"] --> nodeB["3 PHASE INVERTER BRIDGE"]
    nodeC["HALL SENSORS H1 H2 H3"] --> nodeD["DECODER LOGIC"]
    nodeD --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeE["3 PHASE STATOR WINDING"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["PERMANENT MAGNET ROTOR"]
    nodeF -.feedback.-> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeG["CURRENT SENSOR"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["CURRENT CONTROLLER PI"]
    nodeH --> nodeB
    nodeC --> nodeI["SPEED AND POSITION ESTIMATOR"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["SPEED CONTROLLER PI"]
    nodeJ --> nodeH
```

**Block-level interpretation:**

- The **Hall sensors** detect rotor position every 60° electrical and feed the **decoder**, which fires the appropriate pair of inverter switches.
- The **current sensor** measures DC-link current; the inner-loop **current controller** ensures $T_e = K_t I$ is tracked.
- The outer-loop **speed controller** commands a torque/current reference to the inner loop — a classical cascade control structure typical of KTU Module 2 syllabus.

### 4.2 Stepper Motor — Microstepping Excitation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["PULSE TRAIN f_STEP"] --> nodeB["MICROSTEPPING CONTROLLER"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["DAC OR PWM STAGE"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["PHASE A DRIVER"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["PHASE B DRIVER"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["PHASE A WINDING"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["PHASE B WINDING"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["STEPPER ROTOR"]
    nodeG --> nodeH
```

**Interpretation:**

- The microstepping controller generates two sinusoidal current references with a 90° phase shift (for a 2-phase stepper).
- DAC/PWM stages produce amplitude-modulated currents — enabling fractional step angles (e.g., **1/16 microstep of a 1.8° motor = 0.1125°**).

### 4.3 BLDC — Six-Step Commutation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["HALL CODE 101"] --> nodeB["Q1 ON Q6 ON A POS B NEG"]
    nodeC["HALL CODE 100"] --> nodeD["Q1 ON Q2 ON A POS C NEG"]
    nodeE["HALL CODE 110"] --> nodeF["Q3 ON Q2 ON B POS C NEG"]
    nodeG["HALL CODE 010"] --> nodeH["Q3 ON Q4 ON B POS A NEG"]
    nodeI["HALL CODE 011"] --> nodeJ["Q5 ON Q4 ON C POS A NEG"]
    nodeK["HALL CODE 001"] --> nodeL["Q5 ON Q6 ON C POS B NEG"]
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeL
    nodeL -.returns.-> nodeB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each Hall code (3-bit binary from sensors H1, H2, H3) corresponds to one of six valid switching states. Codes 000 and 111 are invalid and used for fault detection — a question that KTU frequently sets for 3 marks.

### 4.4 Comparative Torque-Speed Curve (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["BLDC TORQUE SPEED"] --> nodeB["CONSTANT TORQUE REGION I"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["CONSTANT POWER REGION II"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["SERIES REGION III"]
    nodeE["STEPPER TORQUE SPEED"] --> nodeF["HOLDING TORQUE AT ZERO"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["PULL OUT DECAYS WITH FREQ"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["RESONANCE DIPS AT LOW F"]
```

**Reading note:** Unlike the BLDC, a stepper's *holding* torque at standstill is a key advantage; however, it loses torque rapidly at higher stepping rates due to winding inductance and mechanical resonance.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Explain the construction and working of a Brushless DC motor. Why is electronic commutation preferred over mechanical commutation?

**Model Answer:**

A BLDC motor consists of a permanent-magnet rotor and a 3-phase stator winding. Rotor position is sensed using **Hall-effect sensors** placed 120° apart on the stator. The Hall outputs are decoded to determine which pair of stator phases should be energised at each 60° electrical interval, and a 3-phase inverter switches the corresponding MOSFETs/IGBTs to drive the current.

Electronic commutation is preferred because:

1. There is **no brush friction**, hence no wear and tear and no sparking.
2. The motor has a **higher efficiency** and longer life.
3. **Higher speeds** (tens of thousands of rpm) are attainable since brushes limit speed.
4. **Better heat dissipation** as heat is on the stator (outside), where cooling is easier.
5. **Reduced EMI** and maintenance.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Differentiate between Variable Reluctance, Permanent Magnet, and Hybrid stepper motors.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | VR Stepper | PM Stepper | Hybrid Stepper |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rotor | Soft-iron toothed | Axially magnetised | Toothed + axially magnetised |
| Magnet | None | Yes (ferrite / NdFeB) | Yes (NdFeB ring) |
| Step angle | 5°–15° | 45°–90° | **0.9°–1.8°** (smallest) |
| Holding torque | Low | Moderate | **Highest** |
| Detent torque | None | Yes | Yes (high) |
| Applications | Light-duty timers | Toys, low-cost drives | **CNC, robotics, printers** |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Internal Choice Format:** Each Part-B question is presented as Question A or Question B. Choose any **ONE**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the closed-loop speed control of a BLDC motor. Discuss the role of Hall sensors and current-control loop. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Derive the torque-speed characteristic equation of a BLDC motor. A 48 V, 3-phase BLDC motor has a back-EMF constant of 0.05 V·s/rad and a phase resistance of 0.5 Ω. Calculate the no-load speed, stall torque, and the speed at which the motor develops a torque of 4 N·m. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Closed-Loop Speed Control Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["SPEED REFERENCE N_REF"] --> nodeB["COMPARATOR"]
    nodeC["TACHOMETER OR HALL FREQ"] --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeD["SPEED PI CONTROLLER"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["CURRENT REFERENCE I_REF"]
    nodeF["ACTUAL CURRENT"] --> nodeG["CURRENT PI"]
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH["PWM GENERATOR"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["3 PHASE INVERTER"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["BLDC MOTOR"]
    nodeJ --> nodeF
    nodeJ --> nodeC
```

**Explanation (for 7 marks):**

- **Outer Loop (Speed Control):** Speed reference $\omega_{ref}$ is compared with measured speed (from Hall-sensor frequency or back-EMF). The error is processed by a **PI controller** which outputs the current reference $I_{ref}$ — this is the torque command because $T_e = K_t I$.
- **Inner Loop (Current Control):** The actual phase current measured by a shunt resistor / current transducer is compared with $I_{ref}$. The error drives a second PI controller that outputs voltage commands to the **PWM generator**.
- **PWM → Inverter:** The PWM signals switch the 6 IGBTs of the 3-phase inverter at high frequency (~20 kHz), modulating the DC bus voltage to regulate current.
- **Role of Hall Sensors:** They provide rotor-position information for *commutation* (which phases to energise) and *speed feedback* (via frequency of Hall transitions).
- **Decoupling:** Speed loop bandwidth is set ~10× slower than current loop to ensure stability (cascaded-loop design rule).

---

**(b) Derivation and Numerical Calculation (for 7 marks):**

**Derivation** (as shown in §3.2 of these notes):

$$
\omega_m = \frac{V_s}{K_e} - \frac{R}{K_e^{\,2}}\, T_e
$$

**Given:**
- $V_s = 48$ V
- $K_e = 0.05$ V·s/rad
- $R = 0.5\ \Omega$
- $T_e = 4$ N·m

**Calculations:**

(i) **No-load speed** ($T_e = 0$):

$$
\omega_0 = \frac{V_s}{K_e} = \frac{48}{0.05} = 960 \text{ rad/s}
$$

In rpm:

$$
n_0 = 960 \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = 9{,}167 \text{ rpm}
$$

(ii) **Stall torque** ($\omega_m = 0$):

$$
T_{stall} = \frac{V_s K_e}{R} = \frac{48 \times 0.05}{0.5} = 4.8 \text{ N·m}
$$

(iii) **Speed at $T_e = 4$ N·m:**

$$
\omega = 960 - \frac{0.5}{(0.05)^2} \times 4 = 960 - 800 = 160 \text{ rad/s}
$$

In rpm:

$$
n = 160 \times 9.5493 \approx 1{,}528 \text{ rpm}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a diagram, explain the construction and working of a hybrid stepper motor. Derive the expression for its step angle. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A hybrid stepper motor has 50 rotor teeth and is excited by a two-phase stator winding. Calculate the step angle and the number of pulses required to rotate the shaft through (i) one complete revolution, (ii) 90°. If the motor runs at 600 pulses per second, find the shaft speed in rpm. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working (for 7 marks):**

A **hybrid stepper motor** combines features of VR and PM stepper motors.

- **Rotor:** Two soft-iron end-caps, each with $N_r$ teeth (typically 50), are mounted on a common shaft and axially displaced by **half a tooth pitch**. A radially magnetised (or axially magnetised) permanent magnet sits between them, magnetising the two end-caps with opposite polarity (one North, one South).
- **Stator:** Has **two phases (A, B)**, each with $N_s$ toothed poles. The teeth of Phase-A and Phase-B are physically displaced by **¼ of a tooth pitch**.
- **Working:** When Phase A is energised, the rotor's North-pole teeth align with Phase-A stator teeth; South-pole teeth align with the diametrically opposite Phase-A teeth. When Phase A is switched OFF and Phase B is energised, the rotor advances by ¼ tooth pitch to align with Phase-B teeth. Continuous alternating excitation produces continuous stepping motion.

**Step Angle Derivation:**

Each full step (one complete excitation cycle) advances the rotor by **¼ rotor-tooth pitch**:

$$
\theta_s = \frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{360^\circ}{N_r} = \frac{360^\circ}{4 N_r}
$$

For a 2-phase HB stepper with $N_r$ rotor teeth on each cup:

$$
\boxed{\theta_s = \frac{90^\circ}{N_r}}
$$

---

**(b) Numerical (for 7 marks):**

**Given:** $N_r = 50$, two-phase hybrid stepper.

**(i) Step angle:**

$$
\theta_s = \frac{90^\circ}{50} = 1.8^\circ
$$

**(ii) Pulses for one revolution:**

$$
N_{360} = \frac{360^\circ}{\theta_s} = \frac{360}{1.8} = 200 \text{ pulses}
$$

**(iii) Pulses for 90° rotation:**

$$
N_{90} = \frac{90^\circ}{1.8^\circ} = 50 \text{ pulses}
$$

**(iv) Shaft speed at 600 pulses/s:**

The shaft completes $600 / 200 = 3$ revolutions per second:

$$
n = 3 \times 60 = 180 \text{ rpm}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert $\omega$ (rad/s) to $n$ (rpm) using $n = 60\omega/2\pi$. Always show both.
> 2. **Mis-stating $K_e = K_t$ (–1 mark):** This is valid only in **SI units**. In CGS or with mixed units, the conversion factor $K_t \approx 1.355\, K_e$ must be applied.
> 3. **Forgetting $\frac{1}{4}$ factor in hybrid stepper (–2 marks):** A common error is to use $\theta_s = 360^\circ/N_r$ (which gives the *tooth pitch*, not the *step angle*).
> 4. **Ignoring Hall-sensor invalid codes (–1 mark):** Codes 000 and 111 are fault states; mentioning this fetches an extra mark.
> 5. **Confusing pull-in with pull-out torque (–2 marks):** Pull-in is the **maximum starting pulse rate** (motor starts from rest). Pull-out is the **maximum load torque at a given running speed** (motor loses synchronism above this curve).

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Electric Motors:**

- [x] BLDC motor is a **synchronous, electronically commutated** motor with **permanent-magnet rotor** and **trapezoidal back-EMF**.
- [x] BLDC eliminates brushes → **higher efficiency, longer life, higher speeds**, better thermal management.
- [x] Six-step commutation uses **Hall sensors** (3 of them, 120° apart) and **6 switching states** of a 3-phase inverter.
- [x] BLDC voltage equation (DC-link equivalent): $V_s = IR + L\,\frac{dI}{dt} + E_b$.
- [x] BLDC torque-speed line: $\omega_m = \dfrac{V_s}{K_e} - \dfrac{R}{K_e^{\,2}} T_e$ — linear with slope $-R/K_e^{\,2}$.
- [x] Two regions: **constant torque** (below base speed) and **constant power** (field-weakening, above base speed).
- [x] **Stepper motors** convert discrete pulses into precise angular increments — used in **open-loop position control**.
- [x] Three types: **Variable Reluctance (VR)**, **Permanent Magnet (PM)**, **Hybrid (HB)** — HB has the smallest step angles.
- [x] Hybrid stepper step angle: $\theta_s = 90^\circ/N_r$; e.g., $N_r = 50 \Rightarrow \theta_s = 1.8^\circ$.
- [x] PM stepper step angle: $\theta_s = 360^\circ / (m \cdot N_r)$.
- [x] **Holding torque** = torque required to displace rotor from rest; **detent torque** = residual torque when unenergised (PM & HB only).
- [x] **Pull-out torque** decreases with stepping frequency; **resonance** at low speeds can cause missed steps — often mitigated by **microstepping** or **damping**.
- [x] In SI units, $K_t \;[\text{N·m/A}] = K_e \;[\text{V·s/rad}]$ — frequently asked in 2-mark questions.
- [x] BLDC vs. Stepper: BLDC → high-speed, high-efficiency, requires feedback. Stepper → open-loop precision, high holding torque, susceptible to resonance.
- [x] **Microstepping** (1/2, 1/4, 1/8, 1/16 …) subdivides a step by modulating phase currents sinusoidally — improves smoothness and reduces resonance.
- [x] **Hall sensor codes 000 and 111** are invalid (fault conditions); only 6 valid codes → 6-step commutation.
- [x] Practical formulae: $n_0 = 60 V_s / (2\pi K_e)$ rpm; $T_{stall} = V_s K_e / R$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Modeling: Mathematical Models and Basic Building Blocks of General Mechanical, Electrical, Fluid, and Thermal Systems; Typical Elements of Open and Closed Loop Control Systems; Adaptive Controllers for Machine Tools
Microcontrollers: Architecture and Use for Mechatronic Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET745 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Microcontrollers: Architecture and Use for Mechatronic Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Microcontroller (µC):** A single-chip, highly integrated microcomputer that contains a **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**, **Read-Only Memory (ROM/Flash)**, **Random-Access Memory (RAM)**, **Input/Output (I/O) ports**, **timers/counters**, **interrupt controller**, and **analog/digital converter (ADC/DAC)** subsystems all embedded on a single integrated circuit (IC). It is purpose-built for **dedicated, real-time, embedded control** tasks rather than general-purpose computing.

In the KTU 2024 Mechatronics syllabus context, a microcontroller is the **"decision-making brain"** of any mechatronic system — it accepts signals from sensors, executes a control algorithm, and drives actuators.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of a microcontroller as the **central nervous system of an insect**.
> - The **CPU** is the *brain* (decides what to do).
> - **Sensors** (eyes, skin) feed inputs to the brain.
> - **Actuators** (legs, wings) perform physical actions commanded by the brain.
> - **Memory** is the insect's *learned behavior and muscle memory*.
> - **I/O ports** are the *nerves* connecting everything.
> - **Power supply** is the *blood/food* that keeps it alive.
> - **Timers/Interrupts** are its *reflexes* — automatic, ultra-fast responses to danger.

Just as an insect's tiny brain controls flight, walking, and sensing simultaneously, a tiny microcontroller chip controls motors, reads sensors, and communicates — all in **real time**.

### 1.3 Microprocessor vs. Microcontroller

| Feature | Microprocessor (MPU) | Microcontroller (MCU) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | CPU-only chip | CPU + Memory + I/O on **one chip** |
| Memory | External (off-chip) | **Internal (on-chip)** |
| I/O Ports | External peripherals needed | **Built-in** |
| Cost | Higher (system cost) | **Low** (single chip) |
| Power Consumption | Higher | **Very low** |
| Application | PC, Laptop, Server | **Embedded / Mechatronic** systems |
| Bit-widths (typical) | 32 / 64 bit | 8 / 16 / 32 bit |
| Real-time Response | Not guaranteed | **Deterministic & real-time** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** In mechatronics, the **microcontroller is preferred** over the microprocessor because most mechatronic tasks (motor control, sensor reading, PID loop) are **deterministic and embedded** — exactly what an MCU is designed for.

### 1.4 Why Microcontrollers Are Central to Mechatronics

- **Compactness:** One IC replaces many discrete components.
- **Reliability:** Fewer interconnections → fewer failure points.
- **Real-Time Control:** Deterministic execution cycle (suitable for PID, PWM, feedback loops).
- **Low Cost:** Enables mass production of consumer mechatronic products.
- **Low Power:** Critical for battery-operated robots and portable systems.
- **Easy Interfacing:** Built-in ADC, PWM, UART, I²C, SPI for sensor/actuator integration.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Internal Architecture of a Typical Microcontroller

A generic 8-bit microcontroller (representative of 8051 / PIC / AVR families) contains the following functional blocks:

1. **Central Processing Unit (CPU / ALU + Control Unit)**
   - Performs arithmetic, logic, and control decisions.
   - **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU):** executes +, −, ×, ÷, AND, OR, XOR, shifts, rotates.
   - **Control Unit:** fetches, decodes, and executes instructions.

2. **Program Memory (ROM / Flash)**
   - Stores the **firmware** (control algorithm) permanently.
   - Non-volatile — retains data on power-off.
   - Typical size: **4 KB – 256 KB**.

3. **Data Memory (RAM)**
   - Volatile — temporary storage of variables, stack, registers.
   - Typical size: **128 B – 64 KB**.

4. **I/O Ports**
   - Bidirectional pins (P0, P1, P2, P3 in 8051) for digital interfacing.
   - Each port is **8-bit wide**, individually programmable as input or output.

5. **Timers / Counters**
   - Generate precise time delays, count external events, and produce **PWM** signals.
   - Formula for Timer Delay is critical — see Section 3.

6. **Interrupt Controller**
   - Handles asynchronous events (sensor trigger, emergency stop).
   - 8051 supports **5 interrupt sources**: Reset, Two external (INT0, INT1), Two timer overflows (TF0, TF1), Serial port (TI/RI).

7. **Serial Communication Interface (UART / SCI)**
   - Asynchronous serial data exchange with PC or other MCUs.
   - Baud rate programmable.

8. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**
   - Converts analog sensor signals (temperature, pressure) to digital numbers.
   - Resolution typically **8-bit, 10-bit, or 12-bit**.

9. **Pulse-Width Modulation (PWM) Channels**
   - Drives DC motor speed, servo angle, LED brightness.

10. **Clock Oscillator Circuit**
    - Provides timing reference. Typical crystal: **11.0592 MHz** (for 8051 — yields exact 9600 baud).

### 2.2 Architectural Models

#### 2.2.1 Von Neumann Architecture

- **Single shared bus** for instructions and data.
- CPU fetches instruction and data **one at a time** (Fetch–Execute overlap impossible).
- Simpler hardware, but **bottleneck (Von Neumann bottleneck)**.

#### 2.2.2 Harvard Architecture

- **Separate buses** for program memory and data memory.
- **Simultaneous** instruction fetch + data access.
- **Faster throughput**, used by **8051, PIC, AVR, ARM Cortex-M**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Most mechatronic microcontrollers (8051, PIC16F, ATmega328) use **Modified Harvard Architecture** — separate buses for performance, but unified address space for flexibility.

### 2.3 Popular Microcontroller Families for Mechatronics

| Family | Manufacturer | Architecture | Key Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **8051** | Intel (and many clones) | 8-bit Harvard | Legacy industrial control, learning |
| **AVR (ATmega328)** | Microchip (Atmel) | 8-bit RISC | Arduino-based robotics |
| **PIC (PIC16F, PIC18F)** | Microchip | 8-bit Harvard | Automotive, appliances |
| **MSP430** | Texas Instruments | 16-bit | Low-power wearables, sensors |
| **ARM Cortex-M (STM32)** | ST Microelectronics | 32-bit RISC | Advanced robotics, drones, IoT |
| **ESP32** | Espressif | 32-bit + Wi-Fi/BT | IoT-enabled mechatronics |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Timer Delay (Mode 1, 16-bit, 8051) | $T_{delay} = (65536 - X) \times T_{machine}$ | $X$ = Timer initial value |
| Machine Cycle Period | $T_{machine} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | $f_{osc}$ in **Hz** |
| PWM Duty Cycle | $D = \dfrac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}} \times 100\%$ | Expressed in **%** |
| Average DC Motor Voltage | $V_{avg} = D \times V_{supply}$ | With PWM drive |
| ADC Resolution | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | $n$ = ADC bit-width |
| Baud Rate (8051 UART) | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$ | For Mode 1 |
| Binary-to-Decimal (ADC) | $V_{in} = \dfrac{\text{ADC code}}{2^n - 1} \times V_{ref}$ | — |
| Instruction Cycle | $\text{Cycles} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12}$ | Per second |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas appear in KTU exam numericals on **timer programming, PWM motor control, and ADC-based sensor reading** — practice them thoroughly.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Timer Delay Calculation (8051, Mode 1)

**Goal:** Compute the timer initial value $X$ for a desired time delay.

**Given:**
- Oscillator frequency: $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$
- Desired delay: $t = 50 \text{ ms}$
- Timer mode: **Mode 1** (16-bit timer)

**Step 1 — Compute Machine Cycle Period:**

$$T_{machine} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values:**

$$T_{machine} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 1.085 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1.085 \text{ µs}$$

**Step 3 — Total Timer Counts Required:**

$$N = \frac{t}{T_{machine}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 46082 \text{ counts}$$

**Step 4 — Initial Timer Value (16-bit Timer Max = 65536):**

$$X = 65536 - N = 65536 - 46082 = 19454$$

**Step 5 — Split into High and Low Bytes:**

$$X = 19454 = 0x4BFE$$

$$TH0 = 0x4B, \quad TL0 = 0xFE$$

**Step 6 — Verify:**

$$T_{delay} = (65536 - 19454) \times 1.085 \text{ µs} = 46082 \times 1.085 \text{ µs} \approx 50 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** For a **50 ms delay** on 8051 with 11.0592 MHz crystal, the timer should be loaded with `TH0 = 0x4B` and `TL0 = 0xFE`.

### 3.2 Derivation: ADC Resolution for Sensor Interfacing

**Goal:** Find the smallest measurable voltage (step size) of an ADC.

**Given:**
- ADC bit-width: $n = 10$ bits
- Reference voltage: $V_{ref} = 5 \text{ V}$

**Step 1 — Apply the Resolution Formula:**

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$\Delta V = \frac{5}{2^{10}} = \frac{5}{1024} \approx 4.88 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 3 — Practical Interpretation:**

A 10-bit ADC with 5 V reference can detect voltage changes as small as **4.88 mV**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** If a temperature sensor outputs **10 mV/°C**, this ADC can resolve **~0.488 °C** steps. To improve resolution, increase bit-width (12-bit ADC → ~1.22 mV step) or reduce $V_{ref}$.

### 3.3 Derivation: PWM Duty Cycle for DC Motor Speed Control

**Goal:** Find the average armature voltage for a given duty cycle.

**Given:**
- Supply voltage: $V_s = 24 \text{ V}$
- PWM duty cycle: $D = 60\% = 0.6$

**Step 1 — Apply the Average Voltage Formula:**

$$V_{avg} = D \times V_s$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$V_{avg} = 0.6 \times 24 = 14.4 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Motor Speed Relation (linear approximation):**

$$\omega \approx \omega_{max} \times D$$

So at 60% duty cycle, motor runs at approximately **60% of full speed**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Sensor-to-Actuator Signal Chain in a Mechatronic System

| Stage | Component | Input | Output | MCU Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sensor (e.g., LM35) | Temperature | Analog voltage (10 mV/°C) | Read via **ADC** |
| 2 | Signal Conditioning | Analog voltage | Filtered, scaled voltage | Optional op-amp |
| 3 | MCU (e.g., ATmega328) | Digital ADC code | Control decision (PID) | **Algorithm execution** |
| 4 | PWM Generator | Digital command | Variable duty cycle pulse | **Timer in PWM mode** |
| 5 | Driver (e.g., L298N) | PWM signal | High-current drive | H-bridge to motor |
| 6 | Actuator (DC Motor) | Electrical power | Mechanical rotation | Speed/torque output |
| 7 | Feedback | Encoder / Tachogenerator | Pulse train | **Counter input** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** This **closed-loop signal chain** is the heart of any mechatronic system — the MCU closes the loop by comparing sensor feedback with the desired set-point.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Internal Block Diagram of a Generic 8-bit Microcontroller (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU[CPU and ALU]:::core
    CU[Control Unit]:::core
    ROM[Program Memory ROM or Flash]:::mem
    RAM[Data Memory RAM]:::mem
    PORTA[I or O Port A]:::io
    PORTB[I or O Port B]:::io
    PORTC[I or O Port C]:::io
    TIMER0[Timer Counter 0]:::periph
    TIMER1[Timer Counter 1]:::periph
    UART[UART Serial Port]:::periph
    ADC[ADC Module]:::periph
    PWM[PWM Channels]:::periph
    IRQ[Interrupt Controller]:::periph
    OSC[Crystal Oscillator]:::clk
    BUS[Internal System Bus]:::bus

    OSC --> CU
    BUS --- CPU
    BUS --- ROM
    BUS --- RAM
    BUS --- PORTA
    BUS --- PORTB
    BUS --- PORTC
    BUS --- TIMER0
    BUS --- TIMER1
    BUS --- UART
    BUS --- ADC
    BUS --- PWM
    BUS --- IRQ
    IRQ --> CPU

    classDef core fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mem fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: MCU in a Mechatronic System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENSORSET[Desired Set Point]:::input
    SENSE[Physical Sensors Temperature Pressure Position]:::input
    SIGNAL[Signal Conditioning Amplifier Filter]:::proc
    MCU[Microcontroller ADC to PID to PWM]:::core
    DRIVER[Power Driver H Bridge]:::proc
    ACTUATOR[Actuator DC Motor Solenoid]:::output
    PLANT[Mechanical Plant]:::output
    FB[Feedback Encoder]:::input

    SENSORSET --> MCU
    SENSE --> SIGNAL
    SIGNAL --> MCU

    DRIVER --> ACTUATOR
    ACTUATOR --> PLANT

    FB --> MCU

    classDef input fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

```

### 4.3 Harvard vs. Von Neumann Architecture Comparison

```mermaid

    end

```
