<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between a microprocessor and a microcontroller. Which one is preferred for mechatronic applications and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Microprocessor (MPU) | Microcontroller (MCU) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | CPU-only chip | CPU + Memory + I/O integrated |
| Memory & I/O | External | **Internal, on-chip** |
| Cost & Size | High (system-level) | **Low (single-chip)** |
| Real-time response | Not guaranteed | **Deterministic** |

**Preferred for Mechatronics: Microcontroller** — because mechatronic systems are **embedded, real-time, low-power, cost-sensitive**, and require **on-chip peripherals** (ADC, PWM, timers) for sensor-actuator interfacing. **(1 mark for preference + valid reason)**

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the Harvard architecture used in microcontrollers. How is it different from Von Neumann architecture?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Harvard Architecture:** Has **separate memory spaces and separate buses** for *program instructions* and *data*. CPU can fetch an instruction while simultaneously reading/writing data — enables **pipelining** and faster execution. Used by 8051, PIC, AVR, ARM. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Von Neumann Architecture:** A **single shared bus** is used for both instructions and data, leading to the **Von Neumann bottleneck** (cannot fetch instruction and data at the same time). Used in older PCs. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the internal architecture of the 8051 microcontroller with a neat block diagram.**

**Model Solution:**

The 8051 is an **8-bit, single-chip, Harvard-architecture microcontroller** introduced by Intel in 1980. Its architecture consists of:

1. **CPU (Central Processing Unit):**
   - Includes an **8-bit ALU** for arithmetic and logic operations.
   - Contains a **Control Unit** that decodes and executes instructions.
   - Has an **Accumulator (A)**, **B register**, **Stack Pointer (SP)**, **Program Counter (PC)**, and **PSW (Program Status Word)**.

2. **Program Memory (ROM — 4 KB Internal):**
   - Stores the program code permanently.
   - Addressed by 16-bit **Program Counter (PC)** → up to **64 KB** external.
   - Signal **PSEN̄** is used to read external program memory.

3. **Data Memory (RAM — 128 Bytes Internal):**
   - 32 working registers arranged in **4 banks** of 8 registers (R0–R7 each).
   - **16 bytes bit-addressable** area (20H–2FH).
   - **80 bytes general-purpose RAM** (30H–7FH).
   - SFRs (Special Function Registers) from **80H–FFH**.

4. **I/O Ports:**
   - **4 ports** (P0, P1, P2, P3), each 8-bit.
   - P0: open-drain (needs external pull-up).
   - P3 has **alternate functions** (RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR̄, RD̄).

5. **Timers/Counters:**
   - **Two 16-bit timers** (T0, T1) that can operate in **4 modes**.

6. **Serial Port (UART):**
   - Full-duplex, programmable baud rate.

7. **Interrupt Controller:**
   - **5 interrupt sources**, **2 priority levels**.

8. **Oscillator Circuit:**
   - Crystal connected between **X1 and X2** (typical 11.0592 MHz or 12 MHz).

**Block Diagram (descriptive):** CPU ↔ System Bus ↔ {Program Memory, Data Memory, I/O Ports, Timers, Serial Port, Interrupt Controller}. **(Full diagram: 3 marks, explanation: 4 marks)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain with examples the various applications of microcontrollers in mechatronic systems.**

**Model Solution:**

Microcontrollers are the **embedded intelligence** of mechatronic systems. Major application areas include:

1. **Robotics:**
   - MCU controls motor drivers (L298N) via PWM.
   - Reads encoder feedback for closed-loop position/velocity control.
   - Example: Pick-and-place robotic arm using ATmega328.

2. **Automotive Mechatronics:**
   - **ABS, Airbag control, Fuel injection, Engine ECU, Climate control.**
   - Example: 32-bit ARM Cortex-M MCUs in modern Engine Control Units (ECUs).

3. **Industrial Automation:**
   - PLC replacement, conveyor belt control, pick-and-place machines.
   - MCU interfaces with sensors (LVDT, strain gauge) and actuators (solenoids, stepper motors).

4. **Consumer Electronics / Home Appliances:**
   - Washing machines (water level, motor speed via PWM).
   - Microwave ovens, air conditioners, smart refrigerators.
   - Example: PIC16F877A in washing machine controllers.

5. **Medical Mechatronics:**
   - Infusion pumps, digital BP monitors, prosthetics, insulin pumps.
   - Example: MSP430 in low-power wearable heart-rate monitors.

6. **Process Control & Instrumentation:**
   - PID control of temperature, pressure, flow, level.
   - Example: ATmega-based PID temperature controller for a furnace.

7. **IoT and Smart Systems:**
   - ESP32 MCU with Wi-Fi/Bluetooth in smart agriculture (soil moisture, weather monitoring).
   - Example: IoT-based automatic irrigation system.

8. **Aerospace & Drones:**
   - STM32 in flight controllers of quadcopters (sensors: IMU, GPS, barometer; control: BLDC motor ESC via PWM).

**Conclusion:** Microcontrollers unify sensing → computation → actuation in a compact, deterministic, low-power package — the essence of mechatronics. **(7 marks = 7 distinct application areas × 1 mark each)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the memory organization of the 8051 microcontroller.**

**Model Solution:**

The 8051 has a **Harvard architecture** with **separate program and data memory spaces**.

**1. Program Memory (Code Memory):**
- 4 KB **on-chip ROM** (address 0000H–0FFFH).
- Can be expanded externally up to **64 KB** (0000H–FFFFH) using **PSEN̄** signal.
- 16-bit **Program Counter (PC)** for addressing.
- On reset, execution begins at **address 0000H**.
- Vector addresses: 0003H (INT0), 000BH (T0), 0013H (INT1), 001BH (T1), 0023H (Serial).

**2. Data Memory (RAM) — 128 bytes internal (00H–7FH):**
- **00H–1FH:** 32 bytes divided into **4 register banks** (Bank 0–3), each with 8 registers R0–R7. Bank selected by **PSW bits RS0 and RS1**.
- **20H–2FH:** **16 bytes bit-addressable** area (128 individually addressable bits).
- **30H–7FH:** **80 bytes general-purpose scratch-pad RAM**.
- **80H–FFH:** **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** — control registers for ports, timers, serial port, interrupt, etc. (e.g., P0 at 80H, SP at 81H, TCON at 88H, SCON at 98H).

**External Data Memory (XRAM):**
- Up to **64 KB** external (0000H–FFFFH), accessed via **MOVX** instruction and signals **RD̄/WR̄**.

**Key Memory-Mapped SFRs:**

| SFR | Address | Function |
|---|---|---|
| ACC | E0H | Accumulator |
| B | F0H | B register (multiplication/division) |
| PSW | D0H | Program Status Word |
| SP | 81H | Stack Pointer (resets to 07H) |
| DPTR | 82H-83H | Data Pointer (16-bit) |
| P0/P1/P2/P3 | 80H/90H/A0H/B0H | I/O ports |
| TCON/TMOD | 88H/89H | Timer control/mode |
| SCON/SBUF | 98H/99H | Serial control/buffer |
| IE/IP | A8H/B8H | Interrupt enable/priority |

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain the interrupt system of the 8051 microcontroller with a neat vector table.**

**Model Solution:**

An **interrupt** is an **asynchronous event** that temporarily halts the main program to execute a special routine called **ISR (Interrupt Service Routine)**.

**1. Interrupt Sources of 8051 (5 total):**
- **Reset** (highest priority, non-maskable).
- **Two External Hardware Interrupts:** INT0̄ (P3.2), INT1̄ (P3.3).
- **Two Timer Interrupts:** TF0, TF1 (timer overflow).
- **Serial Port Interrupt:** TI/RI (transmit/receive).

**2. Interrupt Vector Table (addresses in program memory):**

| Interrupt | Flag | Vector Address |
|---|---|---|
| Reset | — | **0000H** |
| External 0 (INT0̄) | IE0 | **0003H** |
| Timer 0 Overflow | TF0 | **000BH** |
| External 1 (INT1̄) | IE1 | **0013H** |
| Timer 1 Overflow | TF1 | **001BH** |
| Serial Port (TI/RI) | TI/RI | **0023H** |

**3. SFRs Controlling Interrupts:**
- **IE (Interrupt Enable) — A8H:** Bit-wise enable (EA, EX0, ET0, EX1, ET1, ES).
- **IP (Interrupt Priority) — B8H:** Two levels — **High (1)** or **Low (0)**. Default = all low.
- **TCON (Timer/Control) — 88H:** Contains interrupt flags (IT0, IE0, IT1, IE1) and level/edge trigger selection.

**4. Interrupt Handling Sequence:**
1. Interrupt request raised (flag set).
2. CPU finishes current instruction.
3. PC is pushed onto **stack**.
4. PC loaded with **vector address** of the ISR.
5. ISR executes.
6. **RETI** instruction pops PC and resumes main program.

**5. Level vs. Edge Triggering:**
- **IT0/IT1 = 0:** Level-triggered (low level).
- **IT0/IT1 = 1:** Edge-triggered (falling edge) — most common in mechatronics.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often confuse **IE0/IE1 (interrupt flags)** with the **IT0/IT1 (trigger type)** bits in TCON — remember TCON is a **dual-purpose** register.
> - **RETI** (not RET) must be used at end of ISR — RETI also re-enables the interrupt logic.
> - Always enable **EA (global enable)** along with the specific interrupt bit, otherwise nothing works.
> - Each ISR is **only 8 bytes apart** in vector table — keep ISRs short or use a jump table.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **MCU = CPU + Memory + I/O + Peripherals on a single chip.**
- **Microprocessor** is CPU only — needs external memory/I/O.
- **Harvard** = separate buses (8051, PIC, AVR). **Von Neumann** = shared bus.
- 8051 has **4 I/O ports** (P0–P3), **2 timers**, **1 UART**, **5 interrupts**, **128 B RAM**, **4 KB ROM**.
- Timer formula: $T_{delay} = (65536 - X) \times \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ (Mode 1, 8051).
- Machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods.
- ADC step size: $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ — higher $n$ → better resolution.
- PWM controls motor speed: $V_{avg} = D \times V_{supply}$.
- Interrupt vector addresses: **0003H, 000BH, 0013H, 001BH, 0023H**.
- SFRs for control: **TCON, TMOD, SCON, IE, IP, T2CON**.
- **RETI** (not RET) ends an ISR.
- Popular mechatronic MCUs: **8051, ATmega328 (Arduino), PIC16F877, MSP430, STM32, ESP32**.
- **Edge-triggered** interrupts are preferred over level-triggered in noisy mechatronic environments.
- **Watchdog Timer (WDT)** is critical for mechatronic reliability — auto-resets MCU on software hang.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programmable Logic Controllers (PLC): Basic Structure, Input/Output Processing; Programming: Timers, Internal Relays, Counters, and Shift Registers; Development of Ladder Programs for Specific Purposes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET745 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Programmable Logic Controllers (PLC): Basic Structure, Input/Output Processing; Programming: Timers, Internal Relays, Counters, and Shift Registers; Development of Ladder Programs for Specific Purposes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (NEP 2020 Aligned)**
> A **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** is a solid-state, microprocessor-based, ruggedized industrial computer that continuously monitors the state of input devices and makes decisions based upon a custom program to control the state of output devices, thereby automating industrial processes with high reliability in harsh environments.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Industrial Brain"

Imagine a **factory supervisor (the PLC)** standing in the middle of a noisy production hall.
- His **eyes and ears** are the **Input Modules** (limit switches, sensors, push buttons) — they constantly feed him real-world signals.
- His **hands and voice** are the **Output Modules** (motors, solenoids, relays, lamps) — they physically act on the machines based on his decisions.
- His **rulebook** (ladder program stored in memory) is a list of IF–THEN conditions he follows.
- His **thinking process** (the CPU cycle) repeats hundreds of times per second — reading inputs → solving logic → updating outputs.

This endless repetition is called the **Scan Cycle**, and it is the heartbeat of every PLC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight — Why PLC over Relays/Microcontrollers?**
> - **Ruggedness:** Designed for **electrical noise, vibration, temperature (-10 °C to +60 °C), and humidity**.
> - **Modularity:** Input/Output cards can be hot-swapped.
> - **Ease of programming:** Ladder logic mimics hard-wired relay diagrams.
> - **Reliability:** Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) often exceeds **100,000 hours**.

### 1.2 Basic Block Structure of a PLC

A PLC consists of **six fundamental sub-systems**:

| Block | Function | Typical Components |
|---|---|---|
| **CPU** | Executes the user program & manages memory | Microprocessor, RAM, ROM, System Clock |
| **Memory** | Stores user program, data, status | RAM (volatile), EPROM/Flash (non-volatile) |
| **Input Module** | Converts field signals to logic-level signals | Optical isolators, RC filters, ADC |
| **Output Module** | Converts logic signals to field signals | Relays, Transistors, Triacs |
| **Power Supply** | Provides regulated DC to internal electronics | 24 V DC / 120–230 V AC |
| **Programming Device** | Used to write, edit & upload programs | Hand-held console or PC with software |

---

### 1.3 Input/Output (I/O) Processing — The Cornerstone Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *I/O Processing* refers to the mechanism by which the CPU reads the physical state of input terminals, processes the logic, and latches the resulting output states during each scan.

#### 1.3.1 Types of I/O Signals
- **Discrete (Digital) I/O:** ON/OFF — 24 V DC or 110/230 V AC.
- **Analog I/O:** Continuous voltage (0–10 V) or current (4–20 mA).

#### 1.3.2 Signal Conditioning at the Input Module
Every input module performs **three critical tasks**:
1. **Optical Isolation** — protects the CPU from voltage spikes using opto-couplers.
2. **Debounce Filtering** — eliminates contact bounce of mechanical switches using an **RC time constant**.
3. **Signal Normalization** — converts AC to DC logic levels for the processor.

#### 1.3.3 Output Module Variants
- **Relay Output:** Universal (AC/DC), slow (~10 ms), bounce-free, **rated typically up to 2 A per contact**.
- **Transistor Output:** DC only, very fast (~0.5 ms), long life, used for high-speed switching.
- **Triac Output:** AC only, moderate speed, silent operation, used for solenoid valves & heaters.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always specify the **switching speed** and **current rating** when comparing output module types in the KTU exam.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The PLC Scan Cycle — Operational Breakdown

Every PLC executes a strict, sequential loop called the **Scan Cycle**. The cycle time is the most important performance metric in any PLC problem.

The four phases of a complete scan cycle are:

1. **Input Scan (Read Phase):** The CPU reads the *current* ON/OFF status of every input terminal and writes this snapshot into a dedicated memory area called the **Input Image Table (IIT)**. Sensors are *not* read again until the next scan.
2. **Program Scan (Logic Phase):** The CPU executes the user program **rung-by-rung, top-to-bottom, left-to-right**, using only the data in the IIT (not the live inputs). The results are placed in the **Output Image Table (OIT)**.
3. **Output Scan (Write Phase):** The contents of the OIT are latched onto the physical output terminals simultaneously, energizing motors, lamps, solenoids, etc.
4. **Housekeeping (Overhead Phase):** Internal diagnostics, communication servicing, and timer/counter updates occur.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Implication — Input–Output Latency:** A button pressed at time *t* will be physically responded to by an output only at *t + T_scan*. For high-speed applications, **T_scan** must be minimized.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **T_scan** | $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{house}$ | ms | Total scan time per cycle |
| **T_in** | $\dfrac{N_{in}}{R_{proc}}$ | ms | Input scan time (N_in = number of inputs) |
| **T_prog** | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_{i}$ | ms | Sum of execution times of all instructions |
| **T_out** | $\dfrac{N_{out}}{R_{proc}}$ | ms | Output scan time |
| **T_total (N cycles)** | $T_{total} = N \cdot T_{scan}$ | s | Time for N complete cycles |
| **Timer (TON)** | $t_{elapsed} = n \cdot T_{cycle}$ | s | Counts elapsed scans until preset |
| **Counter (CTU)** | $CV = PV$ triggers output | counts | Counts up to preset value |
| **Shift Register** | $D_{new} = B_{input}$ ; $D_i = D_{i-1}$ | bits | Each clock pulse shifts data one position |
| **RC Debounce** | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | seconds | Time constant of input filter |
| **Max Throughput** | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{scan}}$ | Hz | Max number of full scans per second |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Tip:** If the average instruction execution time is **8 µs** and the program contains **500 instructions**, then $T_{prog} = 500 \times 8 \,\mu s = 4 \,ms$. Add I/O and housekeeping to estimate full $T_{scan}$.

### 2.3 Programming Elements — Detailed Analysis

#### 2.3.1 Timers
A Timer is essentially a **software-based counter of scan cycles**. There are three primary types:

| Type | Symbol | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **TON — On-Delay Timer** | `TON` | Output turns ON *after* a preset time *elapses* following a TRUE input. |
| **TOF — Off-Delay Timer** | `TOF` | Output stays ON for a preset time *after* the input goes FALSE. |
| **TP — Pulse Timer** | `TP` | Produces a fixed-width pulse on the rising edge. |

**Timing Equation:**
$$PV = \dfrac{T_{required}}{T_{timebase}}$$

where **PV (Preset Value)** is the number the timer counts to, and **T_timebase** is the resolution (e.g., **0.01 s, 0.1 s, 1 s**).

> [!EXAMPLE]
> To get a **10 s** delay using a **0.1 s** base: $PV = 10 / 0.1 = 100$.

#### 2.3.2 Counters
Counters are **edge-triggered memory elements** that increment or decrement on each input pulse.

| Type | Function |
|---|---|
| **CTU (Count Up)** | Increments CV by 1 on every rising edge of CU input; sets output when CV ≥ PV. |
| **CTD (Count Down)** | Decrements CV by 1 on each CD pulse; sets output when CV = 0. |
| **CTUD (Up-Down)** | Two inputs — counts up on one, down on the other. |

#### 2.3.3 Internal Relays (Marker/Flag Coils)
These are **virtual relays** that exist only in memory — they have no physical I/O. Used for:
- **Memory bits** (holding intermediate logic states).
- **Latching circuits** (Set/Reset behavior).
- Example addressing: `M0.0`, `M100.5` (Siemens convention) or `B3:1/0` (Allen-Bradley).

#### 2.3.4 Shift Registers
A Shift Register is a chain of internal relays (bits) that **moves data one position to the left or right** on each clock pulse. Used for:
- **Conveyor sequencing** (parts tracking).
- **Sequential process control** (e.g., bottle filling lines).
- **Pattern generation** (traffic light sequences).

**Shift Equation (Right Shift):**
$$Q_{n}^{new} = Q_{n-1}^{old}, \quad Q_{0}^{new} = B_{input}$$

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

| Industry | PLC Application |
|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Robotic welding cell sequencing, paint shop conveyor control |
| **Food & Beverage** | Bottle filling, capping, labeling, pasteurization |
| **Water Treatment** | Pump alternation, level control, chemical dosing |
| **Packaging** | Wrapping, boxing, palletizing |
| **Power Plants** | Coal handling, ash removal, turbine lube sequencing |
| **Elevators** | Floor call logic, door interlocks, dispatch scheduling |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Industrial Context (2024 Scheme):** With Industry 4.0, PLCs now integrate with **SCADA**, **HMI**, and **IIoT gateways** via protocols like **Modbus TCP**, **Profinet**, and **EtherNet/IP**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Scan Cycle Time

The total scan time of a PLC is the sum of four independent timing phases. We derive each in turn.

**Given:**
- Number of inputs: $N_{in} = 64$
- Number of outputs: $N_{out} = 32$
- Number of program instructions: $N_{prog} = 1200$
- Average execution time per instruction: $t_{i} = 7.5 \,\mu s$
- Housekeeping time: $T_{house} = 1.2 \,ms$
- Processor data bus speed: $R_{proc} = 8 \,MHz$

---

**Step 1 — Input Scan Time (T_in):**

The CPU reads one input word per I/O scan cycle. Each word contains 16 bits. For 64 inputs, we need 4 words.

$$T_{in} = \dfrac{N_{in}}{16 \times R_{proc}} = \dfrac{64}{16 \times 8 \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{64}{128 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$T_{in} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \,s = 0.5 \,\mu s$$

**Step 2 — Program Execution Time (T_prog):**

$$T_{prog} = N_{prog} \times t_i = 1200 \times 7.5 \,\mu s$$

$$T_{prog} = 9000 \,\mu s = 9 \,ms$$

**Step 3 — Output Scan Time (T_out):**

$$T_{out} = \dfrac{N_{out}}{16 \times R_{proc}} = \dfrac{32}{128 \times 10^{6}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \,s = 0.25 \,\mu s$$

**Step 4 — Housekeeping Time (T_house):** Given directly.

$$T_{house} = 1.2 \,ms$$

**Step 5 — Total Scan Time (T_scan):**

$$T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{house}$$

$$T_{scan} = 0.5 \,\mu s + 9 \,ms + 0.25 \,\mu s + 1.2 \,ms$$

$$T_{scan} = 0.0005 \,ms + 9 \,ms + 0.00025 \,ms + 1.2 \,ms$$

$$\boxed{T_{scan} \approx 10.2 \,ms}$$

**Step 6 — Maximum Scan Frequency:**

$$f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{scan}} = \dfrac{1}{10.2 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 98 \,Hz$$

**Interpretation:** The PLC completes **98 full logic cycles per second**, meaning any input change is acted upon within **~10 ms**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Timer Preset Value

**Problem:** A conveyor belt must start 15 seconds after a push button is pressed. The PLC uses a 0.01 s time base. Calculate the preset value (PV).

**Step 1 — Identify desired delay:**
$$T_{delay} = 15 \,s$$

**Step 2 — Identify time base:**
$$t_b = 0.01 \,s$$

**Step 3 — Apply preset equation:**
$$PV = \dfrac{T_{delay}}{t_b} = \dfrac{15}{0.01}$$

$$\boxed{PV = 1500}$$

**Step 4 — Verification:** Number of scans needed = 1500 × (T_scan / t_b) — but the timer hardware internally divides the CPU clock into the time base automatically.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Counter Increment Rate

**Problem:** A proximity sensor produces 4 pulses per second. The PLC counter is to trigger a sorting gate after 100 parts pass. How long until the gate triggers?

**Step 1 — Pulse frequency:**
$$f_p = 4 \,Hz$$

**Step 2 — Total pulses required:**
$$N_{p} = 100$$

**Step 3 — Time required:**
$$T = \dfrac{N_p}{f_p} = \dfrac{100}{4}$$

$$\boxed{T = 25 \,seconds}$$

---

### 3.4 KTU Analytical Decision Matrix — Timer/Counter Selection

| Application | Recommended Element | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Delayed motor start after 5 s | **TON Timer** | Time-dependent, input-driven delay. |
| Holding output for 8 s after input goes OFF | **TOF Timer** | Off-delay preserves output. |
| Counting parts on a conveyor | **CTU Counter** | Edge-triggered, accumulates discrete events. |
| Tracking 8 parts through a 4-stage washer | **Shift Register** | Tracks position of each part along the line. |
| Latching a fault alarm | **Internal Relay (Set/Reset)** | Memory retention, bistable behavior. |
| Generating a 2-second pulse on a button press | **TP Timer** | Fixed-width pulse generation. |

### 3.5 Ladder Logic Boolean Equation Extraction

A ladder rung can be translated directly into a Boolean equation. Consider a rung with contacts **A**, **B** (normally open, in series) and **C** (normally closed, in parallel):

The corresponding equation is:

$$Output = (A \cdot B) + \overline{C}$$

This rule (left-to-right = series = AND; vertical = parallel = OR; bar = NC contact = NOT) is the foundation of all ladder analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Expectation:** Always derive the Boolean equation *before* drawing the timing diagram. This shows logical rigor.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Architecture of a PLC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Field_Inputs
        PS[Push Button]
        LS[Limit Switch]
        SS[Sensor 4-20mA]
    end
    PS --> IM[Input Module\nOptical Isolator]
    LS --> IM
    SS --> AM[Analog Input Module]
    AM --> CPU[CPU + Memory]
    IM --> CPU
    CPU --> OM[Output Module\nRelay Transistor Triac]
    OM --> SOL[Solenoid]
    OM --> MOT[Motor Contactor]
    OM --> LAMP[Indicator Lamp]
    CPU <--> PG[Programming Device\nPC or Handheld Console]
    PS[Power Supply 24V DC] --> CPU
    PS --> IM
    PS --> OM
```

**Description of Flow:**
- Field signals enter via the **Input Module**, which conditions them.
- The **CPU** executes the user program stored in memory.
- Resultant decisions are sent to the **Output Module** which switches field actuators.
- The **Programming Device** allows the engineer to write/modify logic.
- The **Power Supply** powers all internal electronics.

---

### 4.2 PLC Scan Cycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Power ON]) --> INIT[Initialization & Self-Test]
    INIT --> READ[Input Scan\nRead All Inputs into IIT]
    READ --> EXEC[Program Scan\nExecute Ladder Logic\nTop-to-Bottom, Left-to-Right]
    EXEC --> UPDATE[Update Output Image Table]
    UPDATE --> WRITE[Output Scan\nLatch Outputs to Physical Terminals]
    WRITE --> HOUSE[Housekeeping\nDiagnostics & Communication]
    HOUSE --> READ
```

**Explanation:** This loop runs continuously. Each loop is one **scan**. Note that inputs are sampled only at the **start** of the scan — therefore changes that occur *during* the program scan are not seen until the *next* iteration.

---

### 4.3 TON Timer — Ladder Logic Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A([Start Input]) --> X{Timer Input\nEnable}
    X -- TRUE --> T[TON Timer\nPreset = 1500\nBase = 0.01s]
    T -- Done Bit --> Y([Motor Contactor])
    T -.-> D[Display ET\nElapsed Time]
```

**Operation:**
- When Start Input turns ON, the **TON** begins timing.
- After **1500 × 0.01 s = 15 s**, the timer's Done Bit (DN) becomes TRUE.
- This energizes the **Motor Contactor** output coil.

---

### 4.4 Shift Register — Conveyor Sequencing Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Station 1\nFilling] --> S2[Station 2\nCapping]
    S2 --> S3[Station 3\nLabeling]
    S3 --> S4[Station 4\nInspection]
    CONV[Conveyor Motor] --- S1
    CONV --- S2
    CONV --- S3
    CONV --- S4
    CTRL[PLC Shift Register\nTracks Each Bottle] --> S1
    CTRL --> S2
    CTRL --> S3
    CTRL --> S4
```

**Logic:** As each bottle moves through the stations, the shift register's bits move one position per clock pulse. Only the bottle at a given station gets the appropriate process action — illustrating **batch/sequence tracking**.

---

### 4.5 I/O Signal Conditioning Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FIELD[Field Signal\nAC 230V or DC 24V] --> RC[RC Filter\nDebounce]
    RC --> OC[Opto-Coupler\nIsolation]
    OC --> INV[Schmitt Trigger\nWave Shaping]
    INV --> IIT[Input Image Table\nMemory Register]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters:** Without proper conditioning, the CPU could see a switch bounce as multiple ON/OFF transitions, causing erratic output behavior. The **RC filter** alone has a time constant $\tau = RC$ — typically **5–15 ms** for mechanical switch debounce.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"List any six advantages of PLC over conventional relay-based control systems."**

**Model Answer (6 points, any 6 fetch full marks):**

1. **Programmable Flexibility:** Logic changes are done via software, no rewiring.
2. **Compactness:** A single PLC replaces hundreds of relays → reduced panel size.
3. **High Reliability:** Solid-state, no moving contacts (except relays) → MTBF > 100,000 hrs.
4. **Easy Troubleshooting:** Built-in diagnostics and LED indicators on each I/O.
5. **Communication:** Native support for SCADA, HMI, and industrial networks.
6. **Lower Long-Term Cost:** Reduced wiring and faster commissioning.
7. **Documentation:** The program *is* the documentation — easy to maintain.
8. **Modularity:** I/O cards can be added/removed without redesign.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Differentiate between TON and TOF timers with the help of a timing diagram."**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | TON (On-Delay) | TOF (Off-Delay) |
|---|---|---|
| Activates when input | Turns ON | Turns OFF |
| Output turns ON | After preset time elapses | Immediately (when input goes ON) |
| Output turns OFF | When input goes OFF | After preset time elapses (post input OFF) |
| Typical Use | Delayed motor start | Fan cool-down after machine stop |
| Equation | $PV = T_d / t_b$ | $PV = T_d / t_b$ |

**Timing Diagram (ASCII representation):**
```
Input (Start):    |‾‾‾‾‾‾|__________|‾‾‾‾|___
TON Output:       |____|‾‾‾‾|_______|‾|________
TOF Output:       |‾‾‾‾‾‾|________|‾‾‾‾‾‾|____
                  ↑      ↑       ↑   ↑       ↑
                Input   Done   Input Done
                ON      (after  Done (after
                        T_d)   OFF  T_d)
```

---

### **Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students often forget to **convert units** between time bases (e.g., 0.1 s vs 0.01 s).
> - In ladder logic, **internal relays** are sometimes wrongly drawn as physical outputs — you lose 2 marks.
> - Always **label every contact and coil** in your ladder diagram with addresses (e.g., I0.0, Q0.1).
> - For shift registers, mention the **direction** (left/right shift) explicitly.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **basic architecture of a PLC** and describe the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A water tank has two level sensors — **L (Low)** and **H (High)** — and a pump **P**. Design a ladder logic program to:
- Start the pump when level falls below L.
- Stop the pump when level reaches H.
- Include a **START/STOP pushbutton** station with **latching** behavior.
- Add an **indicator lamp** that glows whenever the pump is running.

Write the corresponding **Boolean equation** and explain the operation. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

A PLC consists of the following blocks:

1. **CPU:** The brain. Executes the user program using the microprocessor. Includes the ALU, registers, and system clock.
2. **Memory:** Two types — System ROM (firmware) and User RAM (ladder program + data tables). Non-volatile flash holds the program during power-off.
3. **Input Module:** Receives signals from field devices. Provides **isolation (opto-coupler)**, **debounce (RC filter)**, and **signal conditioning**.
4. **Output Module:** Switches field loads. Available as relay, transistor, or triac types.
5. **Power Supply:** Converts 230 V AC to **regulated 24 V DC / 5 V DC** for internal electronics.
6. **Programming Device:** PC with software (e.g., TIA Portal, RSLogix) used to write, upload, and debug the program.

**Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**Boolean Equation Derivation:**

Let:
- `START = I0.0`
- `STOP = I0.1` (NC contact)
- `L_Sensor = I0.2` (NC — true when level below L)
- `H_Sensor = I0.3` (NO — true when level reaches H)
- `Pump = Q0.0`
- `Lamp = Q0.1`
- `RUN` = internal memory bit M0.0

**Latching Equation (RUN bit):**
$$RUN = (START + RUN) \cdot \overline{STOP}$$

**Pump Start/Stop Equation:**
$$Pump = (RUN \cdot L\_Sensor) \cdot \overline{H\_Sensor}$$

**Indicator Lamp:**
$$Lamp = Pump$$

**Ladder Diagram (ASCII representation):**

```
         START        STOP
          |            |\
          |     RUN    | \
          |------|/|---+--( RUN )---
          |             |
          |     RUN     |
          |------| |----|

         RUN      L_Sensor        H_Sensor
          |          |                |/
          |          |                |
          |---( PUMP )---( LAMP )----|

                  M0.0                Q0.0          Q0.1
```

**Operation Explanation:**
- The first rung is a **latching circuit**. Pressing START sets RUN = 1. RUN holds itself ON until STOP is pressed (which breaks the latch).
- The second rung **ANDs** RUN with the low-level sensor (active when tank is low). The high-level sensor is used as an NC contact — when tank is full, this contact opens and stops the pump.
- The lamp coil is in parallel with the pump coil, so both energize together.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **four phases of the PLC scan cycle** with a timing diagram. If a PLC has **256 digital inputs**, **128 digital outputs**, a program of **2000 instructions**, and an average instruction execution time of **6 µs**, calculate the **total scan time**. Assume housekeeping takes **1.5 ms** and the I/O scan rate is **8 µs per word**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a ladder program using a **CTU counter** to count **50 products** on a conveyor. When the count reaches 50, a solenoid should activate for **5 seconds** to divert the batch to a packing section. After 5 s, the counter must **automatically reset** and begin counting the next batch. Draw the ladder logic, write the Boolean equations, and explain. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

**Four Scan Cycle Phases:**

1. **Input Scan:** Read all inputs into IIT.
2. **Program Scan:** Execute ladder logic.
3. **Output Scan:** Update physical outputs.
4. **Housekeeping:** Diagnostics, comms, timer updates.

**Timing Diagram (ASCII):**
```
|<-- Input Scan -->|<-- Program Scan -->|<-- Output Scan -->|<- Housekeeping ->|
|<------------------- One Complete Scan Cycle (T_scan) -------------------->|
```

**Calculation:**

Given:
- $N_{in} = 256$, $N_{out} = 128$
- $N_{prog} = 2000$, $t_i = 6 \,\mu s$
- $T_{house} = 1.5 \,ms$
- I/O rate = $8 \,\mu s$ per word

**Input Scan:**
$$T_{in} = \dfrac{256}{16} \times 8 \,\mu s = 16 \times 8 \,\mu s = 128 \,\mu s = 0.128 \,ms$$

**Program Scan:**
$$T_{prog} = 2000 \times 6 \,\mu s = 12000 \,\mu s = 12 \,ms$$

**Output Scan:**
$$T_{out} = \dfrac{128}{16} \times 8 \,\mu s = 8 \times 8 \,\mu s = 64 \,\mu s = 0.064 \,ms$$

**Total:**
$$T_{scan} = 0.128 + 12 + 0.064 + 1.5$$

$$\boxed{T_{scan} = 13.692 \,ms}$$

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Boolean Equations:**

- `Sensor = I0.0` (proximity sensor, gives one pulse per product)
- `Reset_Btn = I0.1` (manual reset, optional)
- `Solenoid = Q0.0`
- `Timer_Done = T37` (TON, preset = 5 s with 0.1 s base → PV = 50)
- `Counter_Done = C0` (CTU, PV = 50)
- `Counter_Reset = M0.0`

**Counter Equation:**
$$C0.DN = TRUE \;\; \text{when} \;\; CV \geq 50$$

**Timer Activation (after counter done):**
$$T37 = C0.DN$$

**Solenoid Output:**
$$Solenoid = T37.Done$$

**Auto-Reset Logic:**
$$Counter\_Reset = T37.Done$$
$$C0.Reset = Counter\_Reset$$

**Ladder Diagram (ASCII):**

```
   Sensor         Counter_Done
     |                |
     |    C0          |
     |--|CU|---(RESET?)---| 
     |  CTU              |
     |  PV = 50          |

   Sensor       (manual reset)
     |              |
     |   C0         |
     |---(RESET)----|

   C0.DN         T37
     |             |
     |  T37        |
     |--|TON|--(T37_Done)---
     |  PV=50
     |  Base=0.1s

   T37_Done
     |
     |---(SOLENOID)---
     |
     |---(C0_RESET)---
```

**Operation Explanation:**
- Each product passing the sensor pulses CU input of counter C0.
- When CV reaches 50, the **DN bit** goes high.
- The **TON timer** starts timing for 5 s.
- During these 5 s, the **solenoid** is energized (diverting the batch).
- When the timer finishes, its Done bit triggers the **counter reset**, starting the next batch.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** ⚡

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU Exam**

- ✅ **PLC = Industrial Computer** — solid-state, ruggedized, programmable.
- ✅ **Six basic blocks:** CPU, Memory, Input, Output, Power Supply, Programming Device.
- ✅ **Scan Cycle = 4 phases:** Input Read → Program Execute → Output Write → Housekeeping.
- ✅ **I/O Image Tables** decouple physical inputs from program execution — guaranteeing deterministic behavior.
- ✅ **Timer Preset:** $PV = T_{delay} / t_b$ (always include units in your answer).
- ✅ **Counter Types:** CTU (up), CTD (down), CTUD (both).
- ✅ **Internal Relays** are memory-only — used for latching, sequencing, intermediate logic.
- ✅ **Shift Registers** = sequence tracking; bit shifts on every clock pulse.
- ✅ **Ladder Logic Reading Order:** Top-to-bottom, left-to-right, rung-by-rung.
- ✅ **Contacts:** NO = `| |`, NC = `|/|`. Coils: `-( )-`.
- ✅ **Relay vs Transistor vs Triac Output:** Know speed, voltage, current ratings.
- ✅ **Optical Isolation** is mandatory in every input module for noise immunity.
- ✅ **Boolean Equation Extraction** is expected in every KTU ladder question — always derive it!
- ✅ **MTBF** for industrial PLCs typically exceeds **100,000 hours**.
- ✅ **Common Protocols:** Modbus RTU/TCP, Profibus, Profinet, EtherNet/IP.

> **Final Mantra for KTU 2024 Exam:** *"Read Inputs → Solve Logic → Update Outputs → Repeat!"* 🔁
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET745 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** MEMS Devices: Principle, Fabrication, and Working of MEMS-Based Pressure Sensor, Accelerometer, and Gyroscope; MEMS Devices for Biomedical Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of MEMS

**Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS)** are miniaturized integrated devices or systems that combine mechanical and electrical components fabricated using microfabrication techniques borrowed from integrated circuit (IC) manufacturing. The structural dimensions typically range from **1 micrometer (1 µm)** to **1 millimeter (1 mm)**, and they integrate sensors, actuators, and electronics on a common silicon substrate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** MEMS are microscale devices that convert a physical measurand (pressure, acceleration, angular rate) into a usable electrical signal by exploiting the mechanical response of microfabricated suspended structures (beams, diaphragms, proof masses, combs, tethers) coupled to a transduction mechanism (piezoresistive, capacitive, piezoelectric, or resonant).

The functional hierarchy of any MEMS device consists of three layers:

| Layer | Function | Typical Elements |
|------|---------|------------------|
| **Mechanical / Structural Layer** | Sense or respond to physical stimulus | Diaphragm, proof mass, suspended beam, comb drive, gyroscopic rotor |
| **Transduction Layer** | Convert mechanical energy into electrical | Piezoresistor, capacitor plates, piezoelectric film, tunneling tip |
| **Signal Conditioning Layer** | Amplify, filter, and digitize | CMOS ASIC, op-amp, ADC, temperature compensation |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mechanical IC"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students:** Think of MEMS as a "mechanical circuit on a chip." Just as a transistor uses electrical charge flowing through doped silicon to perform logic, a MEMS device uses a moving silicon structure (a beam, plate, or rotor) whose displacement, strain, or vibration is converted into a proportional voltage or capacitance change. The fabrication is essentially the same photolithographic process used to build microprocessors — only the "device" being built is mechanical rather than purely electronic.

A simple real-world analogy: A MEMS accelerometer works just like a ball inside a sealed box. If the box accelerates forward, the ball appears to roll backward relative to the box wall. In the MEMS version, the "ball" is a tiny silicon proof mass suspended by flexible springs, and its displacement relative to the silicon frame is measured capacitively or piezoresistively.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

The following constants and performance metrics are fundamental to MEMS design and frequently appear in KTU numerical problems:

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / Unit |
|--------|----------|----------------------|
| **E (Young's Modulus of Silicon)** | Stiffness of single-crystal silicon | **169 GPa** (along <110>) |
| **ρ (Density of Silicon)** | Mass per unit volume | **2330 kg/m³** |
| **ν (Poisson's Ratio of Silicon)** | Lateral strain ratio | **0.28** |
| **ε₀ (Vacuum Permittivity)** | Electric constant | **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m** |
| **εᵣ (Relative Permittivity, SiO₂)** | For capacitive gaps | **3.9** |
| **Gauge Factor (GF)** | Piezoresistive sensitivity | **100 to 200** for silicon |
| **Die Size Range** | Typical MEMS chip footprint | **1 mm × 1 mm** to **10 mm × 10 mm** |
| **Feature Resolution** | Modern lithography limit | **< 100 nm** (deep UV) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Always quote Young's Modulus of silicon as **E = 169 GPa** for beams oriented along the <110> crystal direction (the standard orientation used in surface micromachining). Using the <100> value (130 GPa) will be marked as incorrect by examiners.

## 1.4 Why MEMS? The Driving Advantages

> [!NOTE]
> The transition from macroscopic sensors to MEMS is driven by the **"SMALL"** acronym that appears in virtually every KTU module:
> - **S**mall size and weight (mg-level)
> - **M**inimal power consumption (µW to mW range)
> - **A**ccuracy and repeatability (batch-fabricated, hence uniform)
> - **L**ow cost in volume (wafer-level batch processing)
> - **L**ong life and high reliability (no wear-prone macro joints)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Principles of MEMS Operation

All MEMS devices, regardless of the specific measurand, follow the same generic signal chain:

1. **Physical Stimulus Acts on a Compliant Micro-Structure** — pressure bends a diaphragm, acceleration displaces a proof mass, rotation drives a vibrating mass via the Coriolis effect.
2. **Mechanical Response is Transduced into an Electrical Signal** — through piezoresistive (resistance change with strain), capacitive (gap change with displacement), piezoelectric (charge generation with stress), or resonant (frequency shift with mass/stiffness change) mechanisms.
3. **Signal is Conditioned** — bridge completion, amplification, and temperature compensation are performed either on-chip or in an adjacent ASIC.
4. **Calibrated Output is Delivered** — typically a ratiometric analog voltage, a digital I²C/SPI word, or a frequency output.

## 2.2 MEMS Fabrication Technologies — The Four Pillars

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Focus:** Module 4 explicitly tests the four major MEMS fabrication routes. Students must know the process sequence, the role of the **sacrificial layer**, and the difference between **bulk** and **surface** micromachining.

### 2.2.1 Bulk Micromachining

The silicon substrate itself is selectively etched to form the mechanical structure. The etch is typically **anisotropic** (etches preferentially along crystal planes) using wet chemicals such as **KOH (Potassium Hydroxide)** or **TMAH (Tetramethylammonium Hydroxide)**, or **anisotropic dry etching** such as **DRIE (Deep Reactive Ion Etching)** with the Bosch process.

**Key feature:** Produces thick, robust structures (typical diaphragm thickness: **5 µm to 500 µm**), ideal for pressure sensors that must withstand high overpressure.

### 2.2.2 Surface Micromachining

A **sacrificial layer** (typically silicon dioxide or phosphosilicate glass, PSG) is deposited first, patterned, and then covered by a **structural layer** (typically polysilicon). The sacrificial layer is subsequently dissolved in a release etch (e.g., **HF vapor or liquid**), leaving the structural layer freestanding, anchored only at support posts.

**Key feature:** Produces thin, low-mass structures (typical thickness: **1 µm to 5 µm**) ideal for accelerometers and gyroscopes requiring low inertial mass.

### 2.2.3 LIGA (Lithographie, Galvanoformung, Abformung)

Combines **X-ray lithography**, **electroplating** (typically nickel or gold), and **molding** to produce very tall, high-aspect-ratio metallic structures (**100 µm to 1000 µm**). Used for optical MEMS, inertial sensors, and microfluidic channels.

### 2.2.4 SOI (Silicon-On-Insulator) Micromachining

Uses a bonded SOI wafer with a buried oxide (BOX) layer as an etch stop. The device silicon layer (typically **10 µm to 100 µm** thick) is patterned by DRIE, then released by HF etching of the BOX. Yields excellent thickness uniformity and is the modern industry standard for high-performance inertial sensors.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Parallel-plate capacitance (initial gap d, area A) | F |
| 2 | $\dfrac{\Delta C}{C} = \dfrac{\Delta d}{d}$ | Small-signal capacitance change for displacement | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon$ | Piezoresistive gauge equation; $\varepsilon$ = longitudinal strain | dimensionless |
| 4 | $D = \dfrac{E\,t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ | Flexural rigidity of a diaphragm of thickness t | N·m |
| 5 | $w_{\max} = \dfrac{P\,a^4}{64\,D}$ | Maximum center deflection of a clamped circular diaphragm under uniform pressure P (radius a) | m |
| 6 | $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{3\,P\,a^2}{4\,t^2}$ | Maximum bending stress at the clamped edge of a circular diaphragm | Pa |
| 7 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Natural angular frequency of a spring–mass system | rad/s |
| 8 | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Natural frequency in Hz | Hz |
| 9 | $a = \dfrac{k\,x}{m} = \omega_n^2 \, x$ | Steady-state displacement of proof mass under constant acceleration a | m |
| 10 | $F_C = 2\,m\,(\Omega \times v)$ | Coriolis force on a mass m moving with velocity v in a rotating frame of rate $\Omega$ | N |
| 11 | $F_C = 2\,m\,\Omega\,v$ | Scalar Coriolis force when $\Omega \perp v$ | N |
| 12 | $S = \dfrac{\Delta C / C}{a}$ | Accelerometer sensitivity (capacitance ratio per g) | /g |
| 13 | $\Delta C_{gyro} = \dfrac{2\,\varepsilon_0\,A\,\Omega\,v_{drv}}{d^2}$ | Gyroscope Coriolis-induced differential capacitance change | F |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** These equations are used by design engineers at Bosch, STMicroelectronics, Honeywell, and Analog Devices to size diaphragms, choose beam stiffness, and predict sensor sensitivity long before fabrication. A 1 µm error in the gap distance in a capacitive accelerometer can shift sensitivity by **more than 30 %**.

## 2.4 Working Principle of Each MEMS Device (Detailed Theory)

### 2.4.1 MEMS Pressure Sensor — Piezoresistive Type

A thin silicon diaphragm (typically **20 µm to 50 µm** thick, **1 mm to 5 mm** in diameter) is etched from a silicon wafer using anisotropic KOH etching, leaving a square or circular bossed membrane. Four piezoresistors are ion-implanted at the edge of the diaphragm where maximum tensile and compressive stresses occur (two radial, two tangential). When external pressure deflects the diaphragm downward, the piezoresistors experience longitudinal and transverse strains, changing their resistances by amounts governed by the gauge factor. The four resistors are connected in a **Wheatstone bridge** configuration; differential output voltage is directly proportional to applied pressure.

**Key Design Trade-offs:**
- Thinner diaphragm → higher sensitivity but lower burst pressure.
- Larger diameter → higher deflection but larger die size.
- Bossed (thickened center) diaphragm → linear response over wider pressure range.

### 2.4.2 MEMS Accelerometer — Capacitive Type

A silicon proof mass (typically **0.1 µg to 10 µg**) is suspended by four polysilicon flexure beams. The proof mass carries a set of movable capacitive fingers that interdigitate with fixed stator fingers anchored to the substrate. Under acceleration along the sensitive axis, inertial force displaces the proof mass, changing the overlap area (and hence capacitance) of each finger pair. A differential capacitance readout (C₁ − C₂)/(C₁ + C₂) rejects common-mode errors.

**Modes of Operation:**
- **Open-loop (analog output):** direct capacitance-to-voltage conversion.
- **Closed-loop (force-balanced):** electrostatic feedback re-centers the proof mass; the feedback voltage is the output — provides excellent linearity and bandwidth up to **> 10 kHz**.

### 2.4.3 MEMS Gyroscope — Vibratory (Coriolis) Type

A proof mass is driven into resonant oscillation along the **drive axis** (X) at frequency $\omega_d$ and amplitude $x_0$. When the device rotates about the **input axis** (Z) at angular rate $\Omega$, the proof mass experiences a Coriolis force along the **sense axis** (Y), perpendicular to both the drive velocity and the rotation vector. This force drives a secondary out-of-plane (or in-plane) oscillation whose amplitude is proportional to $\Omega$. The motion along Y is sensed capacitively by sense electrodes.

**Critical Design Constraint:** The drive mode and sense mode must have **matched frequencies** to maximize sensitivity. In practice, a slight deliberate offset (typically **few hundred Hz**) is introduced to broaden the bandwidth.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Deflection of a Clamped Circular Diaphragm (Pressure Sensor)

This derivation is the most frequently tested 14-mark problem in KTU Module 4.

**Statement:** A circular silicon diaphragm of radius *a*, thickness *t*, made of material with Young's modulus *E* and Poisson's ratio *ν*, is rigidly clamped at its edge and subjected to a uniform pressure *P* on one face. Derive the expression for the maximum center deflection *w_max*.

### Step 1 — Define the Flexural Rigidity of a Thin Plate

For a plate of thickness *t*, the bending stiffness per unit width is captured by the **flexural rigidity**:

$$D = \frac{E\,t^{3}}{12\,(1-\nu^{2})}$$

The denominator term $(1-\nu^2)$ arises because a plate bends in two directions simultaneously (biaxial bending), unlike a beam which bends primarily in one direction.

### Step 2 — Apply the Classical Plate Theory Equation

For a circular plate under axisymmetric uniform loading, the governing differential equation (in polar coordinates) is:

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left[r\,\frac{d}{dr}\left(\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r\,\frac{dw}{dr}\right)\right)\right] = \frac{P}{D}$$

### Step 3 — Integrate Three Times with Boundary Conditions

Integrating once with the condition that the slope *dw/dr* is finite at r = 0:

$$\frac{d}{dr}\left(\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r\,\frac{dw}{dr}\right)\right) = \frac{P\,r}{2\,D} + C_1$$

Apply finiteness at center → $C_1 = 0$.

Integrating a second time:

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r\,\frac{dw}{dr}\right) = \frac{P\,r^{2}}{4\,D} + C_2$$

Apply the clamped boundary condition $w(r=a) = 0$ and slope $dw/dr \big|_{r=a} = 0$ (after the third integration). The classical result, after integrating a third time and applying $C_2 = -P\,a^2/8D$, is:

$$w(r) = \frac{P}{64\,D}\left(a^{2}-r^{2}\right)^{2}$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate at the Center (r = 0) to Obtain Maximum Deflection

Substituting r = 0 into the equation above:

$$\begin{aligned}
w_{\max} &= \frac{P}{64\,D}\,(a^{2}-0)^{2} \\
w_{\max} &= \frac{P\,a^{4}}{64\,D}
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting the definition of *D* yields the final working formula:

$$\boxed{\,w_{\max} = \frac{P\,a^{4}}{64\,D} = \frac{3\,P\,a^{4}(1-\nu^{2})}{16\,E\,t^{3}}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The center deflection scales with the **fourth power of the radius** and the **inverse cube of the thickness**. Doubling the diaphragm diameter increases deflection by **16×**, while doubling the thickness reduces deflection by **8×**. This is why thin, wide diaphragms are used for low-pressure sensors and thick, small diaphragms for high-pressure sensors.

### Step 5 — Maximum Bending Stress at the Clamped Edge

By classical plate theory, the radial bending moment at r = a is:

$$M_r = -D\left(\frac{d^{2}w}{dr^{2}} + \frac{\nu}{r}\frac{dw}{dr}\right)$$

Evaluating at r = a and converting to stress via $\sigma = 6M/t^{2}$:

$$\boxed{\,\sigma_{\max} = \frac{3\,P\,a^{2}}{4\,t^{2}}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *w_max* (center deflection, ∝ a⁴) with *σ_max* (edge stress, ∝ a²). They are **different functions of radius** and serve **different design purposes** — *w_max* sets the sensor sensitivity, while *σ_max* sets the failure limit (yield strength of silicon ≈ **7 GPa**).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Proof-Mass Equation of Motion (Accelerometer)

**Statement:** A MEMS accelerometer proof mass *m* is suspended by four identical flexure beams of combined stiffness *k*, with squeeze-film damping coefficient *c*. Derive the steady-state displacement under constant acceleration *a*.

### Step 1 — Draw the Free-Body Diagram

The proof mass experiences three forces along the sensitive (X) axis:

- Inertial force: $F_{in} = m\,a$ (in the frame of the device, this is a fictitious force when the device accelerates).
- Spring restoring force: $F_{sp} = -k\,x$ (opposes displacement).
- Damping force: $F_{da} = -c\,\dfrac{dx}{dt}$ (opposes velocity).

### Step 2 — Write Newton's Second Law

$$m\,\frac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}} + c\,\frac{dx}{dt} + k\,x = m\,a(t)$$

### Step 3 — Identify the Natural Frequency and Damping Ratio

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}, \qquad \zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{k\,m}}$$

Dividing the equation by *m*:

$$\frac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}} + 2\zeta\omega_n\,\frac{dx}{dt} + \omega_n^{2}\,x = a(t)$$

### Step 4 — Steady-State Response for a Constant Acceleration

When $a(t) = a_0$ (DC), all time derivatives vanish and the displacement reaches its DC value:

$$\begin{aligned}
k\,x_{ss} &= m\,a_0 \\
x_{ss} &= \frac{m\,a_0}{k} = \frac{a_0}{\omega_n^{2}}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sensitivity Interpretation:** The DC sensitivity $S = x_{ss}/a_0 = m/k = 1/\omega_n^{2}$. To increase sensitivity, either **increase the proof mass** or **decrease the spring stiffness**. However, lowering stiffness also lowers $\omega_n$, which reduces the usable bandwidth (typically the sensor is operated well below $\omega_n$, e.g., at **ω < 0.1 ω_n**).

### Step 5 — Frequency Response for Sinusoidal Acceleration $a(t) = A\sin(\omega t)$

The transfer function magnitude is:

$$\left|\frac{x(\omega)}{A}\right| = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(\omega_n^{2}-\omega^{2})^{2} + (2\zeta\omega_n\omega)^{2}}}$$

At resonance ($\omega = \omega_n$), the magnitude peaks at $1/(2\zeta\omega_n^{2})$, which is why **over-damping or critical damping** is deliberately designed into commercial MEMS accelerometers (typical $\zeta \approx 0.7$).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Coriolis Force in a MEMS Gyroscope

**Statement:** A MEMS gyroscope proof mass of mass *m* is driven into sinusoidal oscillation along the X-axis with velocity $v_x(t) = v_0\sin(\omega_d t)$. The device rotates about the Z-axis with angular rate $\Omega$. Derive the Coriolis-induced displacement along the Y-axis.

### Step 1 — Define the Coriolis Acceleration

In a rotating reference frame, a particle moving with velocity **v** experiences a Coriolis acceleration:

$$\vec{a}_C = -2\,\vec{\Omega}\times\vec{v}$$

### Step 2 — Cross Product for Orthogonal Vectors

With $\vec{\Omega} = \Omega\,\hat{k}$ (Z-axis) and $\vec{v} = v_x\,\hat{i}$ (X-axis):

$$\begin{aligned}
\vec{\Omega}\times\vec{v} &= (\Omega\,\hat{k})\times(v_x\,\hat{i}) \\
&= \Omega\,v_x\,(\hat{k}\times\hat{i}) \\
&= \Omega\,v_x\,\hat{j}
\end{aligned}$$

(Using the right-hand rule: $\hat{k}\times\hat{i} = \hat{j}$.)

### Step 3 — Coriolis Force Magnitude

$$F_C = m\,a_C = 2\,m\,\Omega\,v_0\sin(\omega_d t)$$

This is a sinusoidally varying force along the Y-axis at the **same frequency** as the drive motion.

### Step 4 — Sense-Mode Equation of Motion

The Y-axis (sense-mode) dynamics is:

$$m\,\frac{d^{2}y}{dt^{2}} + c_y\,\frac{dy}{dt} + k_y\,y = F_C(t) = 2\,m\,\Omega\,v_0\sin(\omega_d t)$$

### Step 5 — Steady-State Sense Displacement

At the drive frequency $\omega_d$ (assuming sense resonance ≈ drive resonance, $\omega_{ny}\approx\omega_d$):

$$y_{ss}(t) = \frac{2\,m\,\Omega\,v_0\sin(\omega_d t)}{c_y\,\omega_d} = \frac{2\,\Omega\,v_0}{\omega_d^{2}}\sin(\omega_d t)$$

The amplitude is directly proportional to the input rotation rate $\Omega$ — which is the desired output:

$$\boxed{\,\vert y_{ss}\vert = \frac{2\,\Omega\,v_0}{\omega_d^{2}}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** To maximize gyroscope sensitivity, designers (i) **increase drive velocity** $v_0$ through higher drive amplitude, (ii) **increase drive frequency** choice carefully — high $\omega_d$ reduces $y_{ss}$ but improves bandwidth, (iii) **lower sense-mode damping** $c_y$ through operation in a vacuum package (pressure below **10 mbar** typical for high-performance gyros).

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MEMS Bulk Micromachining Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Silicon Wafer 100 Orientation] --> A1[Thermal Oxidation: Grow SiO2 Layer 1 um]
    A1 --> A2[Photolithography Step 1: Pattern Diaphragm Windows]
    A2 --> A3[Buffered HF Etch: Open Diaphragm Windows in Oxide]
    A3 --> A4[Anisotropic KOH Etch: Etch Silicon to Form Diaphragm]
    A4 --> A5[Strip Oxide Mask in HF]
    A5 --> A6[Boron Diffusion or Ion Implant: Form Piezoresistors at Diaphragm Edges]
    A6 --> A7[Metallization Al or Au: Form Contact Pads]
    A7 --> A8[Anodic Bonding to Glass Pyrex 7740 Substrate]
    A8 --> A9[Final MEMS Pressure Sensor Die]
```

## 4.2 MEMS Piezoresistive Pressure Sensor — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Applied Pressure P] --> D[Silicon Diaphragm 20 to 50 um Thick]
    D -- Bending Strain --> R1[Piezoresistor R1 Radial Tensile]
    D -- Bending Strain --> R2[Piezoresistor R2 Radial Compressive]
    D -- Bending Strain --> R3[Piezoresistor R3 Tangential Tensile]
    D -- Bending Strain --> R4[Piezoresistor R4 Tangential Compressive]
    R1 --> W1[Wheatstone Bridge Node A]
    R2 --> W1
    R3 --> W2[Wheatstone Bridge Node B]
    R4 --> W2
    W1 --> A1[Differential Amplifier IN Plus]
    W2 --> A2[Differential Amplifier IN Minus]
    A1 --> C[Temperature Compensation Network]
    A2 --> C
    C --> OUT[Calibrated Output Voltage Vout Proportional to P]
```

## 4.3 MEMS Capacitive Accelerometer — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AX[Input Acceleration a along X Axis] --> IM[Inertial Force F equals m times a on Proof Mass]
    IM --> PM[Silicon Proof Mass Displaces by x]
    PM --> FS[Flexure Beams Spring Force k times x]
    PM --> FM[Movable Comb Fingers Move Relative to Fixed Fingers]
    FM --> C1[Capacitance C1 Increases]
    FM --> C2[Capacitance C2 Decreases]
    C1 --> CD[Capacitance to Voltage Converter Differential]
    C2 --> CD
    CD --> AMP[Charge Amplifier and Gain Stage]
    AMP --> FB[Optional Electrostatic Feedback for Closed Loop]
    FB --> PM
    AMP --> OUT[Analog Output Voltage Proportional to a]
```

## 4.4 MEMS Vibratory Gyroscope — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DV[Drive Oscillator 20 to 30 kHz Sine] --> DC[Drive Electrodes Comb Fingers]
    DC --> DM[Proof Mass Oscillates along X Axis with Velocity v]
    R[Rotation Rate Omega about Z Axis] --> CF[Coriolis Force F C equals 2 m Omega v along Y Axis]
    DM --> CF
    CF --> SM[Proof Mass Displaces along Y Sense Axis]
    SM --> SE[Sense Electrodes Detect Y Displacement]
    SE --> IA[Interface ASIC Charge Amplifier and Synchronous Demodulator]
    IA --> CAL[Calibration and Temperature Compensation]
    CAL --> OUT[Output Voltage or I2C Word Proportional to Omega]
```
